Weather Summary: Clear skies and only a slight breeze in the afternoon.  Sunshine felt intense radiating off the snow.
Observation Summary:
The avalanches were calling, so we headed to Rescue Basin to document the recent avalanche activity after two nights without freezing temperatures.

We used ski crampons on the skin into the basin.  The surface crust remained supportable up to 4200 feet. 
Temperatures in the sun were hot! The snow surface was quickly transitioning from crust to wet.
All the southern chutes in the basin had loose wet debris. We estimated that among the loose wet debris, there were 12 D2 avalanches and 3 D2.5 avalanches.
 The least avalanche activity was on north-facing slopes. We only observed rollerballs but exited before the day's peak heat.
We observed one fresh D3 slab avalanche that likely failed on March 16th (yesterday) on a northeast aspect of Nyack Mountain.
Our travel was limited to lower elevations. We chose a conservative route that did not have overhead hazards.
Today was the first day of the season that I skied down in the layer I skinned up in.  Skiing was slushy.
Instability Comments: Avalanches occurred in the past 48 hours.

Observation Summary: Above average temperatures with no diurnal re-freeze for 2 days.
Nearby weather station reported 50* F temp at 5 pm with intense solar input.

Drove along the Northern Swan Range and up to Lake McDonald:

Recent activity in the northern Swan Range consisted of many small to large (D1-2) loose wet slides on S-SW-W-NW
Recent activity in the Lake McDonald area also included large (D2-2.5) slab avalanches on S and SW
All activity is assumed to have occured during the heat of the day on 3/15
Instability Comments: Large (D2-2.5) slab avalanches on S and SW.  Small to large (D1-2) loose wet S-SW-W-NW.
Route: Dashboard survey Bigfork to HH Dam to GNP

Observation Summary:
Early bird get the... mashed potatoes? We went east today, seeing the mid 20s temperature readings at Maria's Pass overnight in hopes of a refreeze. What we found was a surprising mix of conditions. Low elevation tress had insulated yesterday's warm snow, meaning soft skinning conditions to start. We were pleasantly surprised to find much more supportable refreeze conditions when the terrain opened above tree line. We found a mix of wind board  and even some powder stashes on northern aspects. We noted several small point releases from yesterday's warmup on all aspects, but nothing larger than D1. One crown from a hard slab that failed >2-3 days ago (judging by the decomposing debris) on a rocky E facing aspect was spotted near Lena Lake. 

Despite the sun and paltry temps, we felt comfortable skiing steep south facing terrain in soft conditions and were back to car by 1315.

Weather Summary: Morning inversion gave way to very warm mid afternoon temps with nearly calm winds and scattered high, thin clouds.
Observation Summary:
Spent the morning avalanche gawking and cataloging, then skinned part way up Elk Mountain to get a feel for the snow. 

Multiple D2, D2.5 and D3 crowns from natural slab avalanches 3/15 and today. These were mostly on upper elevation, sunny slopes, which was also the most visible terrain. The crowns often linked across terrain features. Some looked to have been triggered by rollerballs, loose wet snow slides, or cornice falls. 
Noted some large (D2-2.5) wet snow avalanches, again on sunny/ windward slopes. 
Our ascent/ descent route to 7200 feet was free of overhead hazard and never exceeded 31 degrees. Anything else would have exposed us to today's multiple avalanche problems. 
Overnight inversions and mostly clear skies left a thin but supportable crust on the slopes where we traveled this afternoon. This would have made for excellent corn skiing, had we spent less time gawking and started earlier.

Camera glitches meant few decent photos. argh.
Route: Highway 2 corridor to East Glacier, then up west ridge of Elk Mountain to ~7200 feet and return.

Observation Summary: Road covered in wet snow.  It blew all the way over the road and went all the way down the slope.  There was enough snow to bury a snowmobile.
Instability Comments: Wet and heavy
Route: Snowmobiling around styker loop.  On the backside of the mountain around 5000 feet

Observation Summary: Good freeze last night, there was a bit of a punch through crust. I think it was a mix of wind and warming from the day before. The crust was about 2-3 inches thick and was supportive in some spots and not so supportive in others. There were no signs of instability or isolated hard slabs/wind slabs. We were back at the trailhead by 12:45. The south facing slope of Little Dog was really starting to warm up as we left.
Instability Comments: Just wet roller balls

Observer Affiliation: Whitefish Professional Ski Patrol & The Patrol Fund
Observation Summary: Rollerballs on solar aspects, propagating fracture on an ECT
Instability Comments: Rollerballs were observed on solar aspects. on my ride out i noticed a large wet slide o the West aspect of Great Northern that looked to have run almost full path

Observation Summary:
Took a quick drive to the Hungry Horse Dam to inventory recent avalanche activity on the west side of the Flathead Range. 

Observed many Wet Loose avalanches and one slab avalanche. See the avalanche tab for more information.
Views were limited to west and northwest-facing slopes.

Weather Summary: Too hot for march.
Observation Summary: Dry snow at middle and upper elevations on polar aspects until our exit at 2 pm
Instability Comments: Saw that crown on the northwest shoulder of Penrose that someone posted the other day.
Route: Hwy up the summer trail to lake, then reverse.

Observation Summary:
We traveled to the southern Whitefish Range to observe how the snowpack handles spring-like temperatures.

This morning, we observed fields of rollerballs on southerly mid- and upper-elevation slopes, which were formed by the initial warming on March 14. We also noticed two small (D1.5) wet loose slides in the Skook Chutes.
Areas affected by the warming had a knife-hard crust up to 5 cm thick on steep mid-elevation southerly slopes, which broke down late morning. This crust thinned and became weaker at upper elevations. 
During today's intense heat, numerous rollerballs and one small (D1.5) wet loose slide occurred on southerly slopes. 
The snow on northerly slopes remained dry as of our exit at 1500.
Instability Comments: Warming temperatures initiated rollerballs and small, wet loose slides during our tour.
Route: The standard skin track up and down Skook Ridge.

Observation Summary: Observed on slab avalanche on Kupunkamint Mountain. See details in Avalanche tab.

location: John F Stevens Canyon/ Marias Pass
Weather Summary: Clear,  air temperature in the 50s (F), very light wind at Marias Pass.
Observation Summary: US Highway 2 Observations
Route: Canyon observations today from US Highway 2.  Started obs at approximately 1430.

Observation Summary: Cloud cover and temperatures favorable this AM.  We bit off a small piece of the Skook Chutes facing southwest. Ski quality was not great, with several crust interfaces within the top 20cm and plenty of wet loose debris in the apron.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: overcast till about 1430, then mostly cloudy. Sun arrived around 1500 and temps began to rise above freezing. freezing line was at vehicle in the AM, but was over 6k by mid afternoon. trace of snow fell from the sky around noon.
Observation Summary:
Cooler temps this morning and early afternoon with obscured skies at mid and upper elevations suprised us as we had suspected most aspects would be warmed by mid-morning.
Slopes above 5000' on NW-N aspects prior to temps warming had settled powder
Observed very large cornices on NE and E aspects hanging over Wildcat Lake
Called it a day as the clouds moved out and temps increased above freezing up to 6800'
Felt comfortable stepping out into bigger terrain with no sign of PS problem and quality safe zones to keep an eye on partners.
Instability Comments: SW and W slopes had avalanche activity from yesterday afternoon.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Little to no wind
Observation Summary: We observed little sign of thermal input into the upper snowpack. The sun was intermittently hitting the mountains surrounding us but we stayed out of the sun for the most part. It looks like SW facing slopes were affected by winds a few days ago. New snow was not effected by wind.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.
Route: Gained the ridge between bear creek and wolf creek. Started at valley floor and went up to 6,800 ft mostly on west and North facing terrain.After seeing no signs of instability, no results in our pits, and a lack of sun warming the slopes, we opened our standby terrain and skied 35-40 degree  plainer  slopes. We avoided any rocky terrain that might harbor or a shallower snowpack.

Weather Summary: Cloud cover went from broken to scattered throughout the day. There was not a cloud in the sky to the north during the afternoon.
Observation Summary:
We took a fast trip up to the Link Mountain Weather Station in the northern Whitefish Range.  

Cooler than forecasted temps, scattered cloud cover, and a light south wind seemed to keep wet activity at bay until our departure at 2 pm.  

We did see a fair amount of rollerballs on steep southerly aspects. It would have been easy to trigger small Wet Loose avalanches in that terrain.
Link station at 2 pm read 25 degrees F and the truck was at 44  degrees F when we returned an hour later. 


Snow on northerly aspects remained cold and dry down to about 5800 feet. The snow turned moist below that elevation. 
Signs of wind-drifted snow existed at upper elevations. 

We kept our travels to terrain less than 35 degrees and didn't expose ourselves to larger avalanche paths.

Partly cloudy skies and  cool temps at ridgelines  kept the new snow, 12 plus inches above 5500' uneffected by wind or sun.  We skied Sw , East and S aspects and enjoyed great skiing on these aspects and saw no signs of instabilities climbing up or skiing down. For now there is no slab above the new stout crusts on solar aspects buried around  1 foot down. This will be a concerning layer when it warms up to welcome Spring .Today  we did not step out on any steeper more consequential terrain because of the overall uncertainties of our seasons snowpack and enjoyed great powder skiing and views of our favorite zones!

Weather Summary: •	Partly cloudy with on again/off again sunshine. No precipitation.•	Light winds from the SW.•	Wind loading today was minimal in this area.•	Daytime high temperatures reached the mid-30s °F at Summit/Marias Pass.
Observation Summary: *Other recent wind slabs were unreactive to ski cuts.•	A new surface sun crust was beginning to form below 6000’ on previously sun-exposed slopes by late afternoon as these passed into the shade (easterly aspects).•	Slopes above 6000’ and sheltered from the wind had about 6-8” of recent settled powder.•	Impressive wind re-distribution of snow in this area with entire mountainsides scoured to the ground on more westerly aspects alongside with wind loaded gullies that had drifts pushing 20+ feet thick.•	Pike Creek SNOTEL looked to be in good shape and the snow depth readings here are accurate.
Instability Comments: •	Other than the avalanche, no other signs of instability observed.
Route: •	Ski tour from Summit/Marias Pass to the summit of Elkcalf Mtn. Passed by the Pike Creek SNOTEL.•	Objectives were to check on the Pike Creek SNOTEL, look for any recent avalanche activity, and check out the recent snowfall and wind loading in this area.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken sky cover in the morning. Overcast in the afternoon. Freezing levels rose mid-afternoon with light snow.
Observation Summary:
Today we went up into the Flathead Range to get a gauge on the amount of dry snow on southerly aspects ahead of the upcoming warming event.

Slabs of drifted snow were sensitive to triggers. These ranged from 4 to 10 inches thick. Slabs were failing on facets above a crust or windboard. 
The top 4 inches of snow was loose and dry. New snow below that was stiffened by strong south and west winds.
There is a crust factory on south aspects. The two that stood out were 1 and 3 feet below the surface. Both of these crusts had soft facets resting on top. These may produce Wet Slab avalanches once melt-water reaches them.  

The upcoming warming event will certainly complicate travel. Fingers crossed for overnight refreezes.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: Wind drifts were sensitive to our weight. We were able to trigger slabs up to a foot thick on small wind whales.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: AM started out foggy, but clouds became broken to scattered as the day progressed. Winds were light out of the SW along ridgetops. Short periods of S-1 snow/graupel during our time in the field. Temps were above freezing when we got to around 4500'.
Observation Summary:
Took a ride into the Southern Whitefish Range today to look at the crown of a recently triggered avalanche.

Found said crown on SE Aspect at 6400'.  Hard slab avalanche that failed at the facet layer above the 2/3 crust.
Shaded aspects skied great with 4 inches of recent snow above a right side up snowpack.
Any slope that has seen the sun has a thin melt freeze crust underneath the 4 inches of new snow. 
Wind affect was minimal.  
No other signs of recent avalanches or instabilities.
Instability Comments: None observed.

Observation Summary: Rain turned to graupel around 4500Heavy 6-10” at 4600’, on the skin up. Ok the ski out anything below 5k wanted to slide or turn into roller balls. Intermittent gusty winds and intense graupel storms gave way to clearing skis and less wind. Lots of wind transport, one hollow collapse on open East facing terrain but couldn’t get any propagation in my pit. Recent avalanches on north face of elk and south facing chutes in north Dickey. Judging from debris looked like new/wet snow Submitted is an old crown for above Dickey lake, impressive width!

Weather Summary: Cloud ceiling hovered around 7k earlier in the day and lifted above surround peaks by afternoon.
Observation Summary:
We went to the Swan Range today to look for signs of recent wind-loading and assess persistent weak layers buried in the snowpack. 

Observed no recent avalanches and no signs of collapsing or cracking. We were unable to test any recently formed slabs closer to the crest. 
Roughly 2 to 3 inches of recent snow. Previous tracks were drifted in over in places indicating recent moderate to strong winds. 
We observed no active wind transport throughout the day.
Snowpack tests showed mixed results. The propagating snowpit test we had was on a south aspect. I assume this is the same interface that led to human-triggered avalanches in the Whitefish Range. 
Cornices are LARGE along the crest. They will require a wide berth once things start to heat up again. 

Video and more pictures to come.
Route: Rode up towards Hash, below the crest, and over towards Strawberry.

Observation Summary: Our party of 2 was surprised that the clear snow in West Glacier turned to fat flakes at the Rescue creek th when we started our tour at 9 am. Those flakes were damp falling from the sky till we climbed to 5 k  soaking our layers. Here the snow surface was much drier sitting on top of the preserved 12 plus inches from last weeks storm.  It was obvious that the skiing would be good above 5k. We climbed to 6400' keeping a wide berth from the looming,  but unseen Nyak N face. We enjoyed 2 laps of fluffy snow to the lake then exited out Rescue creek. The wind never picked up at all,  it was very calm and snowing steadily the entire outing.  Exiting did not get sporty till 4500' where the snow turned clear again. The wet snow created rollerballs on turns, but never entrained.Looks like Spring on the highways,  but still Winter up high!

Weather Summary: Light snowfall and rain throughout the day. Winds were light. Visibility was limited all day.
Observation Summary:
We took sleds from Canyon Creek today and had a day of relying on our backup plans.

After parking sleds west of WMR, we toured to Hellroaring Peak.
We observed evidence of southwest winds building drifts and fresh cornices.
Cornices were mostly stubborn. Multiple stomps would eventually cause the cornice to fail.
A melt-freeze crust was most prominent on solar aspects, however we found a surface crust on all aspects, at varying depths.
2 inches of new wet snow accumulated throughout the day.  Precipitation turned to rain around 4000 feet.
Instability Comments: No signs of instabilities were noted.

Observation Summary: Our group took a brief outing to the top of Ghoulie Point where we observed a relatively stable snow pack. It was snowing during are whole excursion, leaving about 1-2 inches of wet denser snow. One of  my worries was the potential for wind slab development on northern aspects, but through investigation we found little to none in terms of wind slab development. Especially on the North - East aspect that drops into Old man's bowl. Of course there was additional snow blown in there, but not nearly enough to develop wind slabs. We eventually followed the ridge to the top of chicken bones (South aspect), where the conditions changed from nice and powdery, to wind sheered and solar affected snow. leaving us with a very crunchy, and not so fun layer of snow. We decided to not ski Chicken Bone, and simply skin back up to Ghoulie Point where we dropped the west aspect back to around tbar area. The snow on this aspect was nice and powdery, with no noticeable slab development. (At least in term of upper layers).  And one more thing: as a group we discussed that the snow found on chicken bones would be a perfect layer as a bed surface, especially with the recently added snow today.

Noted stable snow conditions in Old Man's bowl dropping in from Goulies Point.  A surface crust had formed at this southeast facing aspect at the ~6600ft location but softened a few hundred feet down where the slope flattened and received less exposure to the 3/9 afternoon sun.  1-2" of fresh snow were present in the afternoon.
Route: Goulie Point, Old Mans

location: Gullies Point- Avy 1 Patrol Fund Team Obs
Observation Summary: Patrol Fund Avy 1 Class Observation:Dug 3 pits.  S facing aspect. ~6800’ elev.  ~mid-twenties average slope angle. Notes3 distinct ice layers were noted. No propagation. No reactivity. No obvious signs of instability. Monitored for wet slab development.

First things first, I observed some FAC-adjacent angels save my butt today on the road to the Strawberry Lake trailhead after burying my truck axel deep while trying to turn around. Be warned, trust the rut and think twice before trying to drive the road too far. There aren't any good places to turn around if you don't make to the trailhead.Thanks to those angels, however, we were able to ski today! We took the highway skinner to the Spider Bowl high point and skied a direct W aspect down to the creek, then maneuvered our way back to the vehicles. The snow got warm and moist at low elevations, but early afternoon high clouds and a brisk breeze seemed to keep the mid and upper elevation snow in good shape. We observed a few small roller balls on a due S aspect by some rocks, exactly where you'd expect them, and they were no concern. Some rocks were also tumbling on to the skinner when it where up straight up the guts of the drainage.
No sign of instability. Only observed rollerballs and pinwheels. S-SW aspects have developed a thin crust, while N-NW aspects snow remains soft.

180-200cm of snow across 5 test pits. CT Results: No results to CT19-CT29.  ECTN19, ECT29, ECTX.  pit results found 20 to 30cm below surface of snow. C I autious travel with a purpose of exploration and digging snow pits today. 0 inches new snow in the last 24 hours. Sunny and slight breeze/calm on this Easterly aspect.

Today we toured to Skookaleel adjacent to WMR. Sunny south facing aspects had a sun crust that was breaking down by around 12:00 pm, except near the top where wind seemed be keeping the snow colder. We dug a pit on a south facing aspect at approximately 6750'. Our pit had a partial propagation (about halfway across the column) above a 1" thick crust 20 cm down, with moderate force (15 taps). Shaded aspects still had dry snow upon our exit around 2:00 pm.

Weather Summary: 20 F at start of tour, 900 am. Car read 50 F when I got back at 1430… felt/ skiied like it too :/
Observation Summary: Wind, snow, and some far away crowns. nothin remarkable, other than seeing two other parties on the trail…
Instability Comments: Avalanches seen a ways away must have been from late feb/early march
Route: Toured up to about 6800 ft via summer trail and returned the same way

Weather Summary:
- Below freezing at road elevation at start of tour at 10am, between 40-50F at road elevation at 15:30
- Experienced intense WSW wind gusts with moderate sustained winds on both forested and exposed ridges, noticed wind activity throughout all elevations on my descent 
- Wind speeds seemed to increase at about 2pm
- High thin clouds dominated the sky with periods of clear sky
- Snow was sticky and warm on all aspects below 4900ft on my way down
Observation Summary: Sun and wind dominated the day, and with good visibility various crowns along the flathead crest were noticeable.
Route: Toured up to 7,000 ft via summer trail + ridge line and skied down the same way

Observation Summary: My partner and I got out for a long walk on the east side of GNP and enjoyed the clear skies, warm(ish) temperatures and stunning views. We observed no obvious signs of instability and climbed/skied on all aspects today. Easterly aspect snow surfaces began to soften from solar input around noon and southerlies followed shortly thereafter, but moistening was limited by the consistent moderate SW winds. Winds cranked up after 1:30pm and became more gusty variable in direction. We noticed small pockets of 5-15cm fresh wind slabs above softer old snow that seemed well bonded and quite soft distributed throughout our tour. Snow depths were also variable largely due to wind redistribution... it would seem. Overall, things seemed pretty darn wind-hammered and locked up but we still avoided steep north-facing slopes with expected thin cover and interspersed rocks.

We saw large crowns on steep NE aspects off the shoulder of Penrose and wind affected snow on other aspects. 
We did not dig into the snow at all. Snow surfaces at higher elevations remained dry on all aspects. Moderate winds with high gusts helped this and some cloud cover.
We enjoyed a nice long run from 7600'.  Dry snow transitioned to wet at 5000' and the trail out was sticky which made it safer to ski out.
Really nice that last week storm snow has been holding good skiing!
Instability Comments: Saw lots of large crowns on steep NE facing slopes. Some N facing slopes looked to have some wind affected texture to them.

Observation Summary: Sunny skies and tropical temps. S and SW solar aspects still were capped with a 1-3cm sun crust at the time of our exit around 1300. On our descent of skook chutes, we kicked off a small (D1) wet slab that broke ~20' wide and ran about 50 vertical feet. Subtle changes in aspect made a large difference in snow temp and riding quality today.
Route: Quick tour down Big Trees and up the ridge to Skook Chutes, then down. Found mostly dry snow on shaded north aspects on our descent to the canyon and a mix of dry powder, sun crusted, and quickly decomposing sun crust solar aspects.

location: Site visit to slides on Chicken Bones
Weather Summary: Clear skies and sunny until noon. Winds were light and clouds rolled in around 1215 pm. Warm air temperatures, even with light winds.
Observation Summary:
We went to the southern Whitefish Range today to visit a Level 1 course and visit the site of two reported avalanches.

Reported avalanches were confirmed to be two separate events in the same area. 
Both slides had characteristics of wind slabs with failure on a buried weak layer.
Crown depths ranged from 10cm to 40cm. The average depth was approximately 20cm.
One slide was approximately 200 feet wide, while the other was approximately 50 feet wide.
We skied south and southeast aspects. Solar input continued to warm the surface snow, making for mashed potato snow consistency turns.
The snow surface on northeast aspects stayed dry, however, our travel was limited on these aspects.
Instability Comments: Instability was limited to rollerballs on sunny slopes.

Observation Summary: We found clear calm skies in the morning transitioning to high clouds and high winds in the afternoon. Numerous old crowns in various aspects but mostly around rocks and cliffs. High winds kept south aspects intact all afternoon. Skiied two north facing runs less than 40 degrees. No signs on instability found.

Good sized glide crack 6900ish north coal creek. 240 cm total snow and around 15 cm new snow.. Lots of wind loading; nothing recent moving. 
Saw a  couple D3 that went full path on Hay creek but they appeared to be several weeks old.

Our skin track brought us around and over very large areas of avalanche debris that have affected the majority of the drainage. I am unsure on the exact timing of when these avalanches took place, but they appear to be relatively older.We experienced little wind but noticed significant snow transport on higher ridge lines, especially Nyack.
Instability Comments: None observed
Route: Skinned up Rescue Creek drainage and up SE ridge to 6,600ft. With a number of noticeable crowns lingering around the drainage we were still weary of the faceted layers found in our pit and kept our skiing closer to our skin track and away from the rocky areas that become more prevalent as you go up the ridge to Penrose.

Observation Summary:
It was a gorgeous afternoon up in the Swan Range, with Spider Bowl offering up big views and preserved stable quality snow on sheltered aspects. We observed no obvious signs of instability during our brief tour and stuck mainly to W, NW, and N aspects when climbing/descending. Thank you to whoever set the lovely skintrack! Temperatures were right around freezing but a light SW breeze helped generally preserve the snow surface conditions above 5k', although the top 3-5cm began to thicken up on west aspects by ~3pm. We noted widespread surface hoar above 5k'.
We did see a couple of old small point-release wet-loose avalanches below exposed cliffs that either occurred earlier today or yesterday on south aspects. Below 5k', southerly slopes are melting out FAST.
Also worth noting - the road to Strawberry is rough due to some ambitious 4x4 romping - expect deep hooky ruts!

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Mostly sunny with some high cirrus moving overhead early afternoon, making for thin overcast at times. Winds calm in the basin, but we could see active snow loading on Nyack Mountain early afternoon. Didn't see the same on Mt. Penrose. 28* at the highway at 11:45. Warm but not hot in the sun in the basin.
Observation Summary:
We did a post-meeting trip into Rescue Creek to check for avalanche crowns and debris from the late February avalanche cycle. 

Debris from multiple large (D2.5) and very large natural avalanches (D3-3.5) covered most of the basin floor from about 4000 feet to well over 5500 feet. It wasn't wall-to-wall coverage, but it was a near-continuous carpet on the Nyack Mountain side. 
We estimate most of the slides released on Feb. 28 or 29, with one - the largest - possibly releasing late in the cycle, on March 1. 
The dry slab avalanches slides produced this debris mostly released on north- or east-facing slopes. There was one debris pile in a south-facing runout, though that may have released on an easterly part of the start zone.
We saw almost no rollerballs and definitely no wet loose avalanches on sunny slopes. 
Winds were actively blowing snow around on the upper reaches of Nyack Mountain. 

We enjoyed a snack in the sun and the magnificence and power of the place before threading  turns through the alder on the unaffected side of the drainage and out. 
*Note: the catalogue of avalanches listed here is likely incomplete. Dates and other values are best guesses, as we didn't inspect any crowns. Basically, there was 2-3 days of natural avalanche activity in this basin involving large- very large soft and hard slab avalanches from various elevations; it's hard to determine the character without knowing the exact time the avalanches ran.
Route: Up the drainage to about 4800 feet and return.

Observation Summary: Partly to mostly sunny skies with light to moderate westerly winds made for an excellent day with exceptional views.Snowpack is very thin on southerly slopes below 4000'. Unable to ski down the lower reaches of the Howe Ridge trail.The debris from the previously reported (on Jan. 30th) very large avalanche in the west bowl terminated at about 4200' and crossed the hiking trail.Snowpack deepens quickly above 5000'. Found variable conditions with a thin, breakable surface crust on sunny aspects, but could still find nice turns in soft powder in sheltered terrain below 6500' on more shady slopes.The snowpack on the ridge above about 6500' is capped by a rime crust with a fresh crop of surface hoar growing on top.No obvious signs of instability, no shooting cracks, audible collapses, or any slab releases on test slopes.Noted one large D3 hard slab in the bowl to the north of Stanton Mtn. More details in the avy tab.A quick hand pit near the summit at 7730' on a southerly aspect had the Feb. 3rd crust (~15 cm thick) about 30 cm down with a layer of facets above. This was in a more wind scoured location.
Route: Up the SW ridge to the summit of Stanton Mtn. Down the same route

Weather Summary: Blue Skies and great views. Cold in the shade and even in the sun if the wind was persistent.
Observation Summary: The solar radiation certainly felt nice and our party did not notice any rollerballs or snow dropping from the trees, however, the snow did appear to be transitioning from cold and dry to warm and wet.
Instability Comments: No instabilites observed.
Route: Observed Mostly S-SE aspects up to 6800ft.

Weather Summary: Bluebird! Still cold though. 4000k was about where we saw the snow surface change, only in direct sun however.
Observation Summary: Day off tour in the Northern Middle Fork. -pit at 6600' on a N aspect was unreactive. Facets above the 2/3 crust are more faceted/less rounded than what I saw yesterday(3/6) in the Northern Whitefish Range. - there is a thin crust buried 35 cm down. This had the most potential with CT 24. -Plenty of wind loading below the ridge lines. We saw one old crown from a wind slab that had pulled out. It was above cliffs at approximately 7,000 on an East aspect. - No rollerball activity or any warming of the snow surface until around 4,000'.

Weather Summary: Mostly clear and sunny. Temperatures in the shade remained cool, but the March sun was quite intense.
Observation Summary:
I visited Cascadilla Creek on a day off to take advantage of the clear weather and photograph large fracture lines on the northeast face of Nyack Mountain. 

Recent snow and wind are beginning to fill in the multiple crown lines, but these features remain noticeable.
I can not verify if the crown spanned the entire face from ridge to ridge. If not, two very large hard slab avalanches cover most of this face's 2000-foot width.
This morning, I found 6-10 inches of recent, dry surface snow on north and south aspects at mid-elevations.
Warming temperatures and intense solar radiation moistened the surface on slopes receiving sun. However, I only noticed a few rollerballs and pinwheels upon my exit at 1400. 
No new avalanches were noted in this drainage since my last visit on March 2.
Instability Comments: Today's sun and warming temperatures initiated a few rollerballs and pinwheels by my exit at 1400.

Weather Summary: Light SW winds.  Lenticular clouds formed over the Missions during the afternoon.
Observation Summary:
Beautiful day and great riding in the Swan Range:

It appears the southern end of the advisory area was far less impacted by last week's impressive winds
We found minimal signs of recent wind affect along an exposed ridge
Snow pits revealed several weak layers in the top 1-2 feet 
These layers produced mixed results in stability tests
Middle- and upper-elevation solar aspects stayed dry well into the afternoon
Lower-elevation surface snow transitioned to moist throughout the day, but no new roller ball activity
No obvious signs of instability

Despite generally benign-looking conditions, we were still unwilling to trust buried weak layers.  We avoided steep and rocky slopes that were shaded from the sun.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability
Route: Traveled on nearly all aspects and elevations

Observation Summary: Great riding conditions in the sun. Almost got bit by the lingering persistent weak layer and set of a wind slab on an easterly slope that stepped down to the full deeper slab. No one in the group was caught or injured but are all thankful it wasn’t a different outcome. Careful out there folks!

Weather Summary: •	Mostly clear with plenty of sunshine.•	Light winds from the SW with occasional moderate to strong gusts.•	Wind loading today was minimal in this area.•	Daytime high temperatures reached the mid-teens °F at 7000’, the low-20s °F around 6000’, and the mid-30s °F on the Canyon floor.
Observation Summary: •	No recent avalanches observed.•	No avalanches occurred in Shed 9 during last week’s cycle.•	The crowns from the previously reported events in Shed 7, Path 1163, and the north face of Devils Hump were still partially visible.•	Wind slabs were widespread above 5500’ but mostly unreactive to ski cuts. A few cuts produced 1-2’ fractures.•	A new surface sun crust was beginning to form below 6000’ on previously sun-exposed slopes by late afternoon as these passed into the shade (easterly aspects).•	Dug a snow pit in the upper reaches of Shed 9 at 6740’ elevation, SE aspect, 27° slope.o	Total snow depth here was 51” with a poor structure snowpack, and unstable results in two stability tests.o	ECTP x2 with moderate force on a layer of facets on top of the Feb. 6th crust (see pit profile).•	Repeated an Extended Column Test in Shed 7 West at the same location as last Thursday (Feb. 29th).o	Snow depth here was 71” with the Feb. 3rd crust now buried about 3 feet down. Structure was like that in Shed 9 with multiple facet/crust combo layers beginning about 20” down.o	A few ECTNs in the upper 20”, but no fractures on the facet/crust combo layers deeper in the snowpack.
Instability Comments: Minimal obvious signs. Just some minor cracking in the recent wind slabs
Route: •	Ski tour up the Shed 6/7 ridge, continued up above Shed 7 W, over Pt. 6876’ and on to the Snowslip weather station and the uppermost reaches of Shed 9. Descended the skin track to Shed 5, then down this path.

Observation Summary: -Traveled on ridge-tops and low-angle burned glades, and some thinly covered low elevation pickup stick/jungle gym terrain.-Crowns from last week on Cameahwait and Marion Bowl were still visible, but largely filled with recent drifted snow.  (Horrible light at the time for pics.)-Dueling cornices, though the ones on easterly aspects dwarfed those on westerly aspects.  -While kicking off a mini-fridge sized cornice over an East-facing slope at 7000' I remotely triggered its whale-sized neighbor.  No cracking or avalanching occured on the slope below.  Most of the cornice did not trundle downslope.  (see pics)-Thursday was clear, cold, mostly calm , though light SW winds, with moderate gusts picked up in the evening.   Friday morning was cold and clear with moderate SW wind at 7460'.   Wind transport was minimal due to sastrugi surface on windward slope.  Small plumes of snow were visible off the likes of Mount Stimpson.  High clouds moved in mid-morning.  At 12:30 pm it was 32 degrees at 3870'.-Found some areas on East-facing slopes above 7000' near saddles that had funneled Easterly winds where the snowpack was less than knee-deep and consisted entirely of large facets.  These areas were sometimes not much more than 100 feet away from deeply wind-loaded east-facing slopes with overhanging cornices.-Ski quality was about what you'd expect from a foot or so of 4 finger hardness slab.   -Other than the touchy cornice I saw no signs of instability.  Thanks to FAC and other diligent observers    I knew better than to jump on those tasty-looking NE-facing steeps.

During our 4-day tour in the heart of the Great Bear Wilderness excellent views of Flathead country exposed the recent, natural avalanche cycle from our persistent slab problem. •On Thursday one large persistent slab avalanche was observed on the NE aspect of Baldhead in the Skyland area (D3- NE- 7400'). Crown depth was estimated at 4-5'. •Friday we toured the country near Castle Lake (outside of the forecast zone) and saw a medley of persistent slab avalanches along the Flathead Range (see photos). Some were recent and others had occurred during the wind/snow event in late February.  •We found stable conditions with great skiing on shaded and sheltered slopes. We were not interested in skiing any steep, shaded terrain above 6000'.
Route: Snowmobiled via Skyland Road, skied into Castle Lake area.

Weather Summary: Gorgeous day out there.
Observation Summary: 12-15 inches of dry storm snow on N facing aspects from this last week. Snow pack right side up.
Instability Comments:
Other than the dry loose problem noted, we observed no instability.
Noted a dry loose avalanche on Skook Chutes, natural, D1, R1, ran approximately 100ft.
Route: Skinned out from the resort, and headed east to find cookies, powder, and sunshine. Found all three.

Weather Summary: Mostly bluebird and balmy temps in the teens.
Observation Summary:
We had a gorgeous day of riding in the Ten Lakes area.  
-Plenty of new snow (10-12 inches of recent snow on the road).
-Test pits gave us confidence to explore some steeper terrain and poke out of the timber.  The buried Feb. 3rd crust is nearly a meter below the surface.  The faceted snow above the crust is rounding and was unreactive in our tests.
-There was evidence of wind, although windslabs were generally not very thick(less than a foot) in our travels.  We could see plenty of dunes and pockets of thicker windslabs, but did not travel over them.
-Besides some loose dry that was sloughing in steep terrain below the cliffs, we didn't see any sign of recent avalanches.  
In general it looks like the Ten Lakes has recieved a healthy dose of snow this winter.  Lately its fallen mostly straight down.  Very exposed ridges and high peaks show evidence of wind, but protected terrain is holding 10-20 inches of dry powder. This dry powder, if we see afternoon sun warm up the snow surface in the next few days, could pose a loose wet problem.
Instability Comments: We saw no recent avalanches (besides minor loose dry), no cracking/collapsing, or instabilities in our tests.
Route: Up Stahl Cr., over the saddle to Theriault lake,  around the groomed trail back to the pickup.

Observation Summary: Avalanche debris

Enjoyed intermittent blue sky and good snow conditions throughout the day. A steady 10-15 G 25 WSW wind was scouring the face, and loading the upper portions of the NE bowl.  It looked like about 4-6" of fresh snow since the last sled up the road in protected areas. We observed light hoar frost on open areas first thing this morning. We skied a low angle treed NE aspect, stepping out carefully on our second lap and avoiding wind slabs. Skiing the SW face was scoured in higher elevations, and became more tenable around 6500 feet, then had a light solar effected crust starting at 5800 and growing thicker down to 5000ft where we transitioned. Stability overall seemed decent, though the size and severity of wind slabs certainly affected our travel.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy and cool temps. Wind speeds were moderate along upper ridgelines. Observed active wind transport throughout the day.
Observation Summary:
Today we visited the Tunnel Snow station for routine maintenance. There were brief breaks in the clouds that allowed views of the alpine.

Observed 6 slab avalanches. These all appeared to be soft storm slabs. Three of the avalanches were large (D2). See the avalanche tab for more information.
The upper snowpack is right-side-up with no persistent weak layers in the top 150 cm.
We can still easily locate facets above the early February Crust. They are rounding and the layer is 1 finger hard. Improvement, but still exist.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken skies with moments of intense sunshine. Solar slopes open to the sky became moist up to at least 5600', A surface crust began to form by late afternoon when cloudy skies and light snow showers returned to the canyon.
Observation Summary: • Traveled into Southern Glacier Park today for a welfare check on the Shed 11 weather station and to collect snowpack data in the BNSF Avalanche Safety Program Area.• All seemed well at the Shed 11 Wx. Looking at the Wx data tonight, digital read outs generally agreed with our manual weather observations near the site.• Took the time to collect pit profile data and conduct multiple stability tests. All results pointed towards instability surrounding our Persistent Slab problem. Structure seems to remain especially poor in this part of the forecast region. See snowpack tab.
Instability Comments: Recent wind slab avalanches observed on Snowshed Mountain, and our pit location near the Shed 11 Wx revealed unstable results in all four of our stability tests. CT22 Q1, ECTP24, ECTP27, PST 40/115 (END). All test failed on a layer of 0.5mm rounding facets 68cm below the surface.
Route: Bear Creek to Shed 11 Wx via Southwest Ridge of Running Rabbit Mountain. 3900'-6500'

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Partly cloudy with intermittent snowfall all day and light wind.
Observation Summary: When making first cut into the lower gullies, a wind slab broke about a foot deep and 50ft wide, cleaning out the gully and running a few hundred feet.
Instability Comments: No noticeable instability on suppounding slopes. Snow density changed while descending the slope into the cliffy area, small gullies were cross loaded and broke when entering.

Observation Summary: Winds and fresh snow throughout the day continued to load north and east aspects. We noticed small debris paths under some cornices. Exposed, SW aspects had a breakable, 3cm crust . We had decent riding on protected, low-angle, W aspects.
Instability Comments: Minor cracking  at 6000ft just before ridge on exposed, SW aspect. Cracking occured on dense, 3cm wind crust. Dry loose of small concern on 30°, NE aspect into CM bowl. A little collapsing on ridge above 6500 ft.
Route: Skinned Switchback Trail up to Camp Misery ridge and over to the weather structure. Skied  30°, W aspect above Station Creek, 30° NE aspect into CM bowl and out the road.

Observation Summary: We found about a foot of unconsolidated powder that showed no real signs of wind effect, save for the very tops of ridges.  No evidence of slab formation in the storm snow.  Sun made a nice appearance but didn’t affect the snow much where we were (N facing terrain, predominantly). The storm snow down low (below 5000’) was a bit denser and shallower allowing a skier to feel the crust from this past week’s rain event. On steeper aspects, sluff, though noticeable, was manageable. We skied steeper pitches one at a time, eyes on as much as possible just to be safe. We skied in the lower angle pow with friends and giggles just to be fun.

Recycled our skin track from yesterday. About 3-5” of new. Minimal wind, occasional snow flurries and sun breaks that put out some heat! Saw the same crowns on Nyack and a couple on great northern, some northerly aspect right below cliff bands, guessing 6500’ and likely the same persistent slab. Yesterday’s breakable crust was more supportable today.

Dug a small pit and performed two isolated column tests. One broke after two taps about 100 cm down and the other broke after 8 taps about 40 cm down. I isolated a third column and pulled it toward me with my shovel. It broke cleanly at 100 cm.Results were somewhat inconsistent with each other, but all signs pointed toward a reactive snowpack.
Route: Popped onto the radio towers from the resort to dig a pit and assess the snowpack. Dug a pit on a north-facing aspect and then skied the south face to end up back in bounds

Observation Summary: Observed no instabilities. ECTN 14 @25 cm depth. Non propagating results. 7000’ S aspect. Were surprised to see an alarmingly deep avalanche on S face of Link.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy day with short periods of clearing and a little sun. Calm winds all day. No precipitation throughout the tour. When we were driving back, there was a short burst of wet snowfall.
Observation Summary:
Today's tour to Mount Brown lookout was dominated by blowdown from the past week's winds.

We did not observe signs of instability today. The brief periods of visibility limited our views of the surrounding peaks.
The recent rain crust (buried February 26th) extends in this area up to 6000 feet. The crust was not supportable to the weight of our skis until 5500 feet.
Above 6000 feet, 3 to 4 inches of new snow rests on a firm surface from the past week's wind scouring and drifting.
We did not get propagating results in our long column test at 7400 feet. 
Cornices and wind drifts were stubborn. Stomping on them did nothing.
By the afternoon, the snow surface was moist below 5500 feet.
Actual ski turns stopped at 5300 feet, survival skiing lasted until 4400 feet, and walking/climbing over downfall to the trailhead.
Instability Comments: We did not see signs of instability.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: alternating clouds and partly sunny with some light snow tossed in. great day to be in the mountains.
Observation Summary:
Found nice snow conditions while traveling through mid and upper elevations today. 

243cm deep snowpack at 6500' on a north facing slope.
Early Feb. crust/facet combo buried 114cm below the surface. Facets appear to be rounding, but still very present.
ECTX on a right-side up snowpack above the crust layer (see photo)
Could see recently formed cornices and wind slabs on southerly aspects of Link Mountain
No instability noted on small convex rollovers that were approaching 40 degrees.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability on northerly aspects at mid and upper elevations today. Did see one avalanche on Link Mountain (below) that failed either last night or earlier this morning (sunday) on a southerly aspect.
Route: olney to red meadow for sledding and touring. returned the same way.

Observation Summary: While touring, noticed that a recent avalanche occurred on Peanut Butter Bowl separate from previous slides from two weeks ago.  See photo.South facing slopes above Marion got cooked and heavy.  Sheltered north facing aspects still skiing smooth & silky.
Route: Toured up to Marion Lake to enjoy popular south facing shots down to lake along with north facing runs from Essex Mountain.

Observation Summary: We found unsupportable crust below 5300’, and above that the snow was soft and lovely, easily 12” of unconsolidated fluffy snow. We observed no signs of instability, though did spy some big crowns in steeper terrain around us that looked like they’d failed in the past few days. We skied mainly east aspects, in very mellow terrain. The weather was quiet, making for good vis and barely a whisper of wind all day. We observed some dual-facing small cornices growing on the ridgetop above 7000’, and what looked like wind effect on more open slopes, but none in the sheltered terrain where we skied. Lower elevations are finally filling in, making for a classic middle fork day with the low-elevation beat-down feeling short-lived enough and worth the rewards.

Beauty of a day for a walk'n'wiggle only made better by surprise friends with cookies! No obvious signs of instability observed while on tour today and travelled around the compass between 4500 'and 6600'. Weather consisted of broken/scattered skies, consistent comfortable temps in the low 20s F, calm winds and brief moments of light snowfall accumulating a trace dusting atop the quality 8-12" of consolidated recent storm snow.Dug a quick pit at 6450' on a N 36* slope - HS 255cm - ECTN19@225 & CTM19@225PC on subtle hardness change (4F- to F+)... The snowpack seemed generally quite consolidated and consistent in the upper meter with a pronounced buried crust @155cm that had small rounding facets above and more developed facets below that only fractured with a liberal shovel pry.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed while on tour today.
Route: WMR east route - Ghoulies - Old Mans - Kona - Old Mans - Daddyland - WMRToured and skied all the way around the compass (with limited time on westerly aspects) and from 4450' to 6650'.

Observation Summary: Despite the wind in the canyon it was still and snowing hard. Only for for a quick afternoon but 3” snow fall while we were gone. Some sluffing in the new snow. 5300’ seem to be the transition point of good skiing to more spicy.

Weather Summary: Most of the day was cloudy with light winds. Light snowfall occurred on and off throughout the day.
Observation Summary:
We went to Casacadilla to take a closer look at a large avalanche on Nyack Mountain reported by the BSNF Snow Safety team. 

We had a brief window of visibility in the morning that confirmed that a recent large avalanche failed on the northeast face of Nyack Mountain.
A 2.5-inch rain crust made travel slow. The crust was breaking under our skis until 4700 feet. Above this elevation, the crust was increasingly supportable but also slick. Skinning with a few inches of low-density snow on this knife-hard crust was also slow.
There was widespread evidence that low and mid-elevations in this drainage were impacted by rain. We estimate this rain occurred on February 25th.
With the rain,  many of the steep chutes in Cascadilla had wet loose debris. Some wet loose debris appeared to trigger thin slabs at low elevations.
Well-developed runnels that have since frozen added to a challenging surface for skiing.
Light snowfall on and off throughout the day accumulated 2 inches of low-density snow. We experienced harmless sluff on some steep slopes.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability were observed today.

Observation Summary: No signs of instability, only observation was signs of wind (see photo) however due to the low angles I was skiing no issues with wind slabs observed.

Weather Summary: Several "pulses" of snow produced enough accumulation to cover our skin track and down tracks throughout the day. The most productive snow accumulation was before early afternoon.
Observation Summary: We observed multiple (10+) D1 avalanches throughout our tour. The new storm snow was touchy. We monitored the new snow rates, and would have been concerned if  We dug a pit near the headwaters of Lakolaho Creek, on a N aspect, at 6600 ft. We observed no propagation, ECTx, and the fractures we observed in a CT were not planer. We maintained wide margins, only exposed ourselves to non consequential terrain, and intentionally stuck to low angle/ low consequence slopes throughout the tour. We did poke around (dug a put, many "quick" informal pits, some stomping, some probing) to see if we could find evidence of buried SH or a persistent slab problem set up (concerned for a storm slab or a skier to triggered slide to step down to a persistent slab). We did not see any evidence of deeper instabilities on the slopes we traveled on and tested.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (specific)
Instability Comments: Shooting cracks observed on slopes where rollovers were present, and on specific terrain features > 35'.
Route: Toured from the Canyon Creek Rd up the Smokey Ridge Trail corridor, onto Skook fcae, and then towards the headwaters of the Lakolaho Creek drainage . We skied S and N aspects between 6800 and 5800 ft.

Observation Summary: Skied West facing slopes up to 7200 ft  above Upper WF lake. Haven't had many days with this Quality of skiing all year.The winds dissipated through the morning as we gained elevation surprisingly, but it never stopped dumping.  The upper rain crust went away at 5500 feet and was later covered by 6 inches of light density fresh. At our high point we skied unaffected 16 inches of new dry snow. We dug 2 hand pits on our tour mid day.  First 1 at 6500' found the weak layer (facet crust combo) 70 cm down.  Didn't break out with hand shear. At 30 cm down storm slab was reactive but unconsolidated. Second hand pit at 7 grand showed the top cm stiffening up to a wind slab, not very reactive.We skied 4 laps and had to rebreak trail at points each run due to the refills.Good riding found on protected treed slopes less than 35 degrees. Figure it's a different story higher up.

Weather Summary: • Saturday offered mostly cloudy skies with light to moderate SW winds, and stormy (S5) at times.• Sunday began with a moment of heavy snowfall in the early morning, but quickly turned to scattered clouds and calm winds with very little precipitation. The sunshine broke through around 2:00pm. The snow surface remained dry and fluffy during our departure at 2:30pm, however I suspect it may have done some damage by early evening. It remained partly sunny during my drive back to the valley.
Observation Summary: Excellent skiing in the Flathead Range this weekend. 48-hour snow totals amounted to about 2" on the canyon floor and 10" inches at our high point of 6400'. No signs of instability in the recent snow, however the Persistent Slab structure lurks... We favored windward/solar slopes less than 35 degrees.Large hard slab avalanches observed on the Flathead Crest.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability in the recent snowfall.
Route: Dickey Creek. 3800-6400'

Weather Summary: Most of the day was high clouds and calm winds. In the afternoon, there were short periods of sun.
Observation Summary:
We toured east of WMR today to get more snowpack data after the intense winds on Thursday.

Widespread wind-affect has turned the surface snow into a stiffened styrofoam.  The buffed surface was fast for skinning, but punchy for skiing.
We did not get propagating results in our snowpack tests: ECTN12, CTN, and 95/100 for our Propagation Saw Test. 
We skied east and southeast slopes that had cross-loaded wind drifts without signs of instability.
At our final descent, we got a shooting crack at the entrance of the south-facing slope of Half Moon, which encouraged us to ski the timbered edge.
Instability Comments: Isolated harmless cracking in wind drifts under our skis and one isolated shooting crack on Half Moon.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Warm cloudy day transitioning to a spring like sunny afternoon. Gonna need to install a misting fan on my hat at this rate in the sauna of the forest!
Observation Summary: Had to come back to the spider to relive some good turns, ended up being worthwhile. Plenty of debris in the natural landslide from previous rain. Pretty darn warm out, really feeling like spring is springing below 5500 feet. Snow was good and the out was quick. Periods of sunshine in the afternoon were nice, and we timed it right and missed any kind of sun affected snow we might have ridden had we dropped later.
Instability Comments: West/southwest aspects here have been pretty sheltered from the wind and we only found wind slabs on the most exposed NW facing ridges, more of a crossloading situation in the right areas. Pit results did not propagate, however our column test sandwiched like a layered cake from the recent crusts, each one failing subsequently after the next in the first 1 foot. No signs of instability or whoompfing here.
Route: Drove up road past camel hump that blocked previous driving access. Road is melting out at lower un shaded spots. Skinned up creek, then up west/southwest facing aspects to 6051 feet. Rode sheltered terrain between 28-32 degrees on W facing aspect.

Weather Summary: Wet at the parking lot. Windy on the ridge and finally dried out and started snowing about 10:30.
Observation Summary: Day off ski/sled in McGinnis cr. Conditions were deep and wet below 5500'. Above there we gained the ridge and it was wind board. Found a north aspect at 5,800' that skied good enough to get a few laps on. Several audible collapses below 5,500' kept us off steep terrain in the lower elevations. Saw one recent natural that occurred today on a south aspect at 6,000'. Occurred sometime between 12:00 and 13:00. Looked to be a few feet deep and several hundred feet wide.
Instability Comments: Several collapses in the lower reaches around 5,000'. East aspect.
Route: Skinned/snowmobiled up the Depuy/Kimmerly divide.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Drastically warming conditions with moderate to strong winds above 6000 feet.
Observation Summary:
We visited the southern Whitefish Range to examine the developing wind and storm slab problems and the Persistent Slab structure. 

There was intense wind drifting in open areas, particularly along an upper-elevation ridgeline where fresh cornice formation occurred. Wind transport occurred in timbered terrain to a lesser extent. 
We noted large debris piles from a recent cornice failure and witnessed a natural failure. These whales were reactive to the weight of a skier, although we gave many of them a wide berth due to their size and precarious perch. 
6-8" thick, soft slabs formed in sheltered terrain. These occasionally cracked underfoot, but no shooting cracks or collapsing was observed. The overnight snow was dense and rested on Wednesday's low-density powder. 
We had propagation in a snowpit at 6650' in both the storm snow and on a buried weak layer. See the snowpack section for more details.
Instability Comments: We noted cracking in soft slabs in sheltered terrain. Cornices failed both naturally and from the weight of a skier.

Weather Summary: •	Mostly overcast with occasional broken skies in the afternoon.•	No precipitation all day until about sunrise when a rain/snow mix began on the Canyon floor.•	Very windy, sustained moderate winds from the SW with strong to extreme gusts.•	Wind loading was intense with several hundred-foot-long plumes of snow off the ridge crests above 6000’ at times.•	Freezing levels climbed throughout the day reaching just above 6000’ by late afternoon.
Observation Summary: -The most recent melt-freeze crust from Monday (Feb. 26) on the Canyon floor thinned out and disappeared around 4800’. This now has about 2-4” of wet snow on top.•	About a foot of recent snow above 5500’ with heavy wind loading from the SW winds.•	Fresh wind slabs 1-4+ feet thick were observed above 6000’, primarily on N thru E aspects. Limited cracking from ski cuts in these slabs.•	Observed one audible collapse on the SE ridge about 6200’ elevation.•	We dug a snow pit within 50 yards of the recent hard slab crown in Shed 7 West (pit profile). We could not safely access the crown today.o	Total snow depth was about 69” with the early Feb. facet layer buried about 35” down.o	Stability tests results were variable with a mix of stable and unstable results.o	We suspect the failure layer of the nearby avalanche was the layer of facets above the Feb. 3rd crust.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Route: •	Observations from Hwy 2 and a ski tour up the SE ridge of Snowslip Mountain to the upper elevation starting zones in the Shed 7 avalanche path.

Weather Summary: Strong winds at 5000' and up. Occasional rain at lower elevations and snow at higher elevations.
Observation Summary: Snow was very wet at lower elevations and pretty slabby at mid/upper elevations. Saw a recent crown that likely broke in the previous 24 hrs on a North aspect. Skiing was funish, slabby snow transitioning to grabby as we descended with an occasional soft turn in the trees.
Instability Comments: Considerable wind affect above 5500'. Windslabs were hard and slabby often cracking under weight of a skier. Jumping on slabs would cause them to crack and fail.
Route: Climbed and skied W aspect of Diamond peak

Observation Summary: We toured up to 7300' on Sixmile Peak. We encountered strong W winds that wanted to rip gear out of our hands while transitioning. From there we headed to calmer meadows, seeking low angle, mid-elevation E facing terrain which held the best snow of the day. Here, we found 1-1.5 feet of snow above the stout crust, on similar elevation S aspects, there was only 4-6 inches atop the crust. The wind was strong enough to load slopes far off ridgelines. 1-2 inches of new snow at middle elevations and the end continued loading throughout the day. Every rollover and test slope we walked on or ski cut on E aspects released a wind slab. These were generally small, but we did find some 8-10 inches thick. Despite the lower new snow totals than further north in the Swan, dangerous slabs existed and we kept our terrain low angle.

Weather Summary: Wind was active all day. Snowfall was only an inch throughout the day.
Observation Summary: Day off snowboarding in the lost Johnny drainage. We found great riding in terrain sheltered from the wind. Leeward slopes had wind slabs 12-16 inches thick that were reactive to our weight. We closed terrain that we had initially wanted to ski. Once we got near the ridge it became obvious that any travel on the east facing(leeward) slopes would most likely result in triggered avalanches. We opted to stick to low angle, sheltered, and south facing slopes. They rode great and provided a great days worth of powder skiing.
Instability Comments: Any slope that had seen wind was building a slab and was reactive to our weight. Cracking around our skis on the uptrack and on the descents we found any steep slope would crack with our weight. Slabs were generally 12-16 inches thick.
Route: Up the lost Johnny road to the end and then skinned up the south facing slope above the road.

Weather Summary: Winds increased throughout the day with very light snowfall.  Storm seemed to roll in aroun 1630hrs.
Observation Summary:
Some mechanical issues shortened our day and confined us to mostly windward and solar aspects. No matter,  we found exceptional riding on low angle and wind sheltered terrain.

 4-6 inches of recent snow with little to no snowfall throughout the day
We saw many signs of recent wind at middle elevations but drifts were soft and stubborn
Moderate winds yeilded light blowing snow at upper elevations
We intentionally and easily triggered a small (D1) on the leeward side of wind affected ridge at upper elevations
The recent snow had transtitioned to moist below 5000' during our descent
Rain started to come down as we packed up the sleds at 1630hrs. 

 
Media to follow
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (specific)
Instability Comments: Kicked off small (D1) soft slab on a wind-loaded test slope.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Intermittent snow showers (S1) throughout the day with gradual clearing by early afternoon. By 2pm shifted to scattered clouds with patches of blue sky.
Observation Summary:
We investigated mid and upper elevation slopes in the Northern Swan today (2/27), where up to a foot of snow fell over the past few days. Moderate winds from the SE left reactive wind slabs on NW-W aspects at upper elevations.

Small wind slabs were reactive to the weight of a rider with shooting cracks traveling several feet on NW aspects. There was active snow transport across the drainage adding to previous developed cornices.
We found a persistent weak layer in 3 snow profiles (N-NE-E) buried up to 100 cm. We didn't have any propagation on this layer in our ECT tests. A PST on a N aspect propagated thru the full block at 68 cm.

Our mindset for the day was assessment. With new snow and moderate wind speeds from various directions across the forecast area we avoided slopes over 30 degrees and had a great day in the snow. Light to moderate snowfall in the morning yeilded to scattered clouds with the sun making an appearance by mid-afternoon.
Instability Comments: Wind slabs cracked and failed along W-NW ridgelines above 6500'. We avoided slopes over 30 degrees where a loose dry problem most likely would have existed.
Route: HH Dam to Beta road. Hiked from road to ridge above Beta Lake. Returned the same way.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Small snow flurries intermixed with periods of spotty sunshine and blue skies.
Observation Summary: Beautiful day in the Swan range playing on the spider, the creek keeps filling in a bit more each time I make it out here. Far less elk weed loving to get in here than a few weeks ago. Plenty of old rollerball debris and possibly some sub D1 wet slides from the sun a few days ago. Snow was good riding, skin track was a little dust on crust on the way out. Getting back to the car, we looked back at the Swans to notice all the mountain peaks showing the trailing streak of white indicative of some major wind transport going on loading up the East Side of the Swan range.
Instability Comments: The West/Northwest facing aspect we skinned up and rode was stable, on some of the ridge that faces more NW we found small unreactive wind slabs. Sluff was light and manageable, confined to mostly gullies and wind sheltered terrain.

Weather Summary: Weather was variable, sunshine and mixed flurries/snowing.
Observation Summary: There were mixed weather conditions on our way up,  some blue skies followed by snow and cloud and wind. No signs of instability. Upon reaching the summit, we changed decisions from skiing back down the route we came up and opted for the North Western facing slope, mixed between 30-40 degree angles. We skied one at a time down West facing slope. Around ~6,800ft second skier triggered an avalanche. Both skiers skied out of the avalanche path , no one was dragged or carried.  Estimated crown depth to be 1.5ft and 40 ft across. We suggest a wind loaded area failed,  stepped down, and broke at the persistent layer, gathering more snow and speed as it moved approximately 600ft to the bottom of the bowl.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.
Route: Sled and ski from Challenge cabin, parked on Skyland near Morrison Crk. Skinned up west-facing aspect of Slippery Bill (most southern) to peak at 7,421ft.

Observation Summary: Started in the rain, ended in the rain.  The new (or future) normal?Skied primarily W facing aspects above Upper Whitefish Lake.  Traveled between 4400' and 7200'.  Snow was real firm (supportable crust) on much of our ascent in the morning; it transitioned to soft snow around 6200'.  Skiing was surprisingly fun (or at least "not terrible") and surfy for the upper thousand feet - worthy of a couple of laps.  Winds did gust strongly on and off while we were up there, but nowhere near what we anticipated, being so directly windward.  Picked up about 3" down low throughout the day, and maybe 4" up high.  Last 500 feet was... mank.  Challenging.  Snow transitioned to rain shortly after Upper WF Lake on our way out.  Was still drizzling at the TH when we pulled out at 5:30 pm.Worth it.  This time :)

Avi 2 field day observations: We skied to Beaver Ponds via the East aspect off the back of Flower Point chair. We skinned up a North aspect near the Kona area. We dug 3 pits at various elevations on this slope around 6400’ elevation. The only significant finding we observed was an ECTN24. This failure was not at the thicker crust layer and it was not a smooth, clean break. Through the afternoon, the wind and snow picked up. There was more cohesion within the new snow as the day progressed. Hand pits revealed a potential storm slab at a wind exposed area on the saddle and ridge between a North and South aspect.

Short tour off Gullies and East along the ridgeline.  Found surprisingly good skiing on solar aspects, with 3-5" of new unconsolidated snow. Winds were gusty on the ridge line, with signs of wind transport (skin tracks filling in, wind sculpting) increasing throughout the afternoon. We dug a quick pit on a N aspect at 6300' and had an ECTX result.

Weather Summary: Got colder later in the day. Wind gust 12-15 mph on ridge.
Observation Summary: Crust on dust on crusty ski with some softer spot on the lee side of trees.
Instability Comments:
In south facing snow pit we had two layers with Q2 shear with facets above and below. Major ice layer at 13 cm below surface and was about 5 cm thick.
In north aspect test pit we had slight propagation at 29 taps about 4 cm above the ice layer.
Route: Skinned up from canyon creek saddle up skook ridge then down skook face and back to skin track.

Observation Summary: Patrol Fund Avalanche 2 course field day observations: We dug 2 pits 20 yards apart on a S aspect at 6450’ elevation. Our tests revealed a weak layer about 40cm down. Pit 1 - ECTP14. Pit 2 - ECP22.

Our party left WMR at the bottom of Ridge Run and skinned up to the top of Skook Face. Top of Skook Face showed thin (3-4 inch) stubborn windslabs on the north aspects, isolated to just below ridge lines. Cracking was isolated to just beyond skis while testing these slabs. At the top of Skook Face, our group (level 2 avalanche class) dug 2 pits: one in the area exposed to wind and one in an area more sheltered from the wind. The pit that was sheltered from the wind had snow depth of 150cms and resulted in an ECTX. In the pit that was more exposed, snow depth was 170cms and our stability tests had results of ECT N 29, failing on a layer of buried surface hoar 30-35 cms below the surface. Once we found this surface hoar layer, we did another ECT to see how it would react and got a result of ECT N 16 on the same surface hoar layer. We continued to seek out this surface hoar layer and were able to find it in various hand pits as we progressed westward along the ridge.

Fearing a dearth of observations  from the WMR-adjacent backcountry, we headed out into the murk. Skies were obscured for our whole tour, with light ridgetop winds and calm elsewhere. Precip was very light, with a mixture of frozen and non-frozen around 5600ft. Temperatures hovered around freezing. We dug on a North, lee aspect at 6400ft with a snow depth of 230cm. We had no results on any of our tests and did not observe any weak layer directly above the early Feb crust. Of note, we did see a couple of what we believed to be glide cracks across the way on a south aspect.

Rode the snow machines to 6 grand and climbed Link Mountain.  Snow was wind effected on the ridgeline, but we did not observe any cracking.  We skied the N aspect from 7200' down to the lake.  Snow was supportable pow.  We climbed to the summit of Lake Mountain (7800') and skied the long W facing aspect to drainage bottom. Surprisingly, the snow was fun and soft on this aspect as well.  We then climbed the ridge to Whitefish Mountain's summit, where we dug a pit on an East aspect at 7300'.  A 4 inch wind skin was on the surface of the snow where we dug.  HS 250 cm, weak layer was down 55 cm.  ECTN26 50 cm down.  We skied this aspect to finish a fun day.  Our friends continued from Lake Mtn on to the Everest of the Whitefish Range, summiting and skiing the S face of Nasukoin!!The storm was threatening all day but produced nada.  Winds were moderately gusty but mostly calm in protected zones.  Hope we get a couple inches from the next doozy storm!

Observation Summary: Observed a rather unreactive snowpack. Our results were as follows:South aspect:ECTN23ECTN24ECTXNorth Aspect:ECTXECTX
