Route: Part of the Patrol Fund's Avalanche Level 2 course. We dug pits at two locations. The first was on the south facing aspect of Radio Towers, about 30 feet above the Hell Fire ski run - elevation roughly 6600. The second was on the north facing aspect of Ghoulies just out of bounds - elevation roughly 6500.

Weather Summary: There was consistent wind all day blowing plumes of snow off the surrounding ridge lines.
Observation Summary: We skied chalky wind buff on mid/upper elevation <30° slopes.  Winds were moderate out of the west and actively filled in our skin track. Recent cornices failed 6'' down. Snow was buffed on SE aspect and provided consistent turns on top 4''. February crust buried 8-18'' above 5600'. Crust interface 1-1.5mm facets.
Instability Comments:
-Audible collapse at 6800' on SE aspect 28° slope - failed on 1 mm facets above February crust
-Cracking in stiff snow near ridge top on lee slopes
Route: We gained the SE ridge through a densely forested obstacle course.Skied SE terrain <30°Skied N terrain <5600'

Observation Summary: Dug around in the snow as part of the Patrol Funds level 2 Avy Course on hellroaring peak. We dug two pits on a South aspect at approx 6400’. Total snow depth was 165cm, multiple small crusts throughout snowpack. A thick pencil hard crust was noted through the bottom 35cm or so of the pit. One of the southerly pits had a ECTP28, down 37cm. Interestingly, the other pit approx 10 meters away on the same aspect at the same elevation had ECTX.We also dug one pit on a northerly slope at 6500’ and noted one thin crust approximately 39cm and a thicker crust a bit below that. In that pit we got a CT24, down 39. A PST didn’t break until almost entirely through the column, but we were then able to then get a clean break with a shovel shear. The slab broke, however it didn’t slide off the isolated column.Takeaways:Southerly aspects seem to have more crusts but not apparent slab.Variations exist even across similar aspects and elevations.Northerly aspects may have better skiing but there is a persistent slab that is likely low probability of triggering but could have high consequence if it does.

A group of five (4 skiers, 1 snowboarder) in an avalanche course tried to observe buried surface hoar that were recently observed in the whitefish range a day prior. We were on a NW aspect at an elevation of 6,362. We were unable to confidently locate the buried surface hoar. At 126 cm we observed a hard crust and did two ECT tests with results of ECTN24@/31 and ECTN22. We also did three PST tests at 140cm but no propagation or collapsing occurred. 2-3” of New snow was deposited yesterday (2/22). Today (2/23) had no wind and clear visibility with scattered clouds. Overall, the facets observed seem to be rounding, however as we continued with snow column test and applying excessive force with a shovel pulling  behind the column we noticed the slab broke at an unidentified layer about the crust.  Our interpretation was this could be the buried surface hoar starting to round. However, we decided that this surface hoar layer should be treated with caution and as it may still exists and present problems at steeper angles.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Observation Summary: see above
Route: We decided to explore new to us ski spots up the Middle Fork instead of sticking to the old standby.  Traveled up standard Scalpblock trail (SW, W aspects) until about 6000 feet and then toured directly up to the summit and headed off N NE aspects for some skiing and obs.-On West aspect there was a breakable crust until 5,900 feet under a few cm of new snow.-Temperatures were above freezing below 5000 feet.-On ridges there was no sign of recent significant wind slab formation although ridges are less prominent than others in the range.Enjoyed skiing a new area but we concluded that over 3000 ft. elevation gain required to ski less than 1000 feet slopes makes it a less than ideal ski touring area.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Poor visibility, light winds, couple flurries
Observation Summary:
Monthly transceiver practice at the WMR beacon park, then a quick walk to Hellroaring Peak.

Found a couple layers of buried (decomposing) surface hoar roughly one foot deep at 6700', North aspect
This layer propagated in an Extended Column Test
No other obvious signs of instability
No sign of recent wind transport
Solar aspects had two thin melt-freeze crusts in the top foot of snow
The top crust was supportable on open slopes and punchy in the trees
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability

Weather Summary: Partly Cloudy and warm temperatures.
Observation Summary:
Today we went up to the Tunnel Ridge to do a wellness check on the wind station.

We observed two wet debris piles on southerly-facing aspects. I suspect these occurred on Monday, February 19th. No crowns were visible in the upper start zones. These were likely Wet Loose. 
Observed one natural slab avalanche on a ramp off of Mt. Grant. The crown appeared to be at roughly 6400 feet on a northeast aspect. Flat light made it difficult to get any quality photos. 
Snowpit tests on both north and south aspects revealed unstable results. Both snowpits failed on facets and surface hoar above the early February crust.

The upper snowpack structure is not inspiring. Although facets are showing signs of rounding, extended column tests had some energy when they propagated.

Took the snowmobile near the top of the Tunnel Rd. Ran into the forecasters who I’m sure will have a more detailed report. During the skin in, there was a noticeable 1-2” crust at lower elevations which turned into unconsolidated heavy snow as we continued to climb. There were signs of roller balls on most Northerly aspects although I didn’t notice any running today despite temps at or above freezing.  No wind affected snow noted. We dug a quick pit on the saddle at approx 6200’ on a WNW slope. Total snow depth approx 230cm, ECTX. A thick crust layer was noted approx 45-50cm deep that didn’t react to an ECT but when I pried with the shovel it slid on that layer. We skied a densely treed WNW slope and found decent heavy snow but ended up in terrain a bit steeper than we planned. No instability noted but again it was very densely treed. And the bush still exists in all drainages!

Temperature (F): 25 on ridgetop and 32 on red meadow road
Weather Summary: cloud deck raised and lowered several times during the day. Socked in at upper whitefish lake, but raised to about 6500' before falling back down around 1600. S1 snow above 5500' with rime around 4600' on our way back to Olney.
Observation Summary:
We traveled up to Link Mountain to de-rime the weather station today.

Pleasantly suprised to find nice coverage below 5000'
The only significant avalanches we were able to see were older debris piles in the larger slide paths both off of Link Mountain and above Red Meadow Lake. Cloudy/Obscured skies limited visibility at higher elevations.
Temps hovered around the freezing mark along the road, but cooled to mid-20s as we gained elevation. 
Although winds were light out of the SW, gusts up to 22mph along the ridgetop forced us to add an additional layer as the Wx station was cleaned off. 
1 D1 wind slab was released on the ridge just below the Wx Station. Wind slabs were limited to small size in our location.
We chose slopes under 35 degrees that provided fun turns and ample stoke upon returning to our sleds.
Instability Comments: Small wind slabs on ridgeline on leeward side of Link Mtn. Reactive to a rider, but very limited in size and didn't entrain additional snow. We observed no cracking or whumphing during our ascents or descents. Small pockets of dry loose snow released while descending, but were easily managed through good travel techniques.
Route: olney to red meadow pass, ascend SE face to link mtn. return same way

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Warmer day. Large wet snowflakes fell for most of the morning and tapered on and off during the afternoon. Not a lot of accumulation was noted.
Observation Summary: Although there was no propagation on our ECT test and we did not see any other signs of instability, we felt that the identification of the problem layer, and the recent observations/events in the middle fork, were enough for us to feel a high level of uncertainty. The transition from unconsolidated and non-cohesive recent snow to a soft slab-like structure also added to our concern.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability noted outside of snowpit
Route: Skiied E facing aspect at/below 7000 feet.

Observation Summary: Warm day in rescue and the sun was coming in as we were leaving. We skinned up to the bowl just below the pass and got eyes on a big crown on the E-SE face of Penrose running about 1000 feet across the face. The crown was probably 1000 feet above us but the debris from the slide ran down almost to the second step in the drainage. It had some new snow on it so it was probably a bit older Throughout the day things were getting warmer. There was minimal stuffing from trees but the snow was noticeably softening. We dug a put at about 5000 feet on an E aspect and found a stiff crust with 1.5 feet of lighter snow on top. ECTN 13 with that crust 1.5 feet down being very stout at this lower elevation.

Took the meandering alley-oop up to the Penrose shoulder and found somewhat punchy upside down pow. The trees on solar aspects of Penrose were raining and shedding hard. Otherwise pretty calm weather and avy activity.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Temperature (F): warming, but still below freezing at noon
Weather Summary: High clouds in the morning with plenty of solar input.  Some clearing  in the afternoon.   Still below freezing on North aspects near 6000' at noon.  Fog near ridgetops as warm air from the South met the cooler air on the North aspects.  Light Southerly wind.   Above freezing at 2:00pm on mid elevation S aspect.
Observation Summary: -Focus was investigating how the warming trend was affecting stability on N aspects in terrain East of WMR.-Found a more cohesive slab on top of the crust/persistent weak layer sandwich.-Surprised to find no failure or propagation in two Extended Column tests, given the persistent weak layer problem.-Observed multiple parties ski 35-38* degree wind sheltered slopes with a rollover without incident.-Stayed well away from steep solar-exposed slopes, but found excellent skiing on low-angle South aspects that were just beginning to crust over as clouds thickened in the late afternoon.
Instability Comments: Only sign of instability definitely observed was rollerballs from tree bombs on steep sun-exposed terrain.   The light was flat when I got a look at Skook Chutes from across the canyon, so I am not positive, but it looked like a point release had triggered a small slab.  Again, not positive, but that is what it looked like.
Route: Toured from WMR baselodge  at 4500' to 6400' via S, NE and West aspects to investigate N aspect.

Observation Summary: Looks like December coverage, skis like April. •Overcast skies broke around 1100 and gave way to powerful sunshine which affected any solar aspects top-to-bottom (ie rollerballs/trees dripping/chirping birds)•The solar input from Saturday left a very thin M/F crust on the surface of most S/W aspects. In shaded terrain we found dense powder and overall optimal skiing conditions. •Winds at upper elevations were calm with light gusts. No snow transport was occurring anywhere along the FH crest. •Overnight, a dusting of snow had partially filled in existing skinners. Of note, the tracks that were visible on Mt Adams (saturday), had been filled in and were hard to see. Assuming light snow accumulation and recent winds were responsible. •On our descent, we skied a W aspect and the aforementioned M/F crust was beginning to break down and create a wet surface. We did find dry snow on small NW folds within the topography. •Numerous D1-2 wet loose slides were observed in the Sheep and Essex Creek drainage, all originating in steep, rocky, solar prone terrain.
Instability Comments: Surface instability was occurring from rapid warming (ie rollerballs and trees dripping up to 7500' W)
Route: Toured into Essex Creek (0900), climbed and skied a W aspect from our high point of 7875'

location: Another skier caught and carried, Marion Lake
Weather Summary: Mostly clear skies, warm but not hot in the sun, calm to light winds.
Observation Summary:
We investigated the crown of the avalanche accident that occurred above Marion Lake. Unfortunately, we added to the tally of large, human-triggered avalanches in that area when I triggered 2 large avalanches (SS-ASu-R2-D2-O) on our descent, one of which caught and carried me but did not bury me. 

The slide occurred on similar terrain as the nearby slide: a northeast-facing slope at 7000 feet.
The start zone slope angle was 33-34*. It was adjacent to a steeper slope to the rider's left. It was roughly concave, except for a small convexity on the rider's right side, which also contained some small trees.
The slide released almost immediately when I entered the slope. I tried to ski off the slab to the skier's right. Close, but not quite. 
The debris carried me about 200 vertical feet. I was under the snow for much of that but not buried. The debris continued another 100 vertical feet or so.
I lost some equipment but was uninjured, apart from painful bruises and several small cuts.
My partner and I self-evacuated. 

Needless to say, I was surprised by the slide. The weak layer at the crown and slopes adjacent to the 2/17 slide looked unassuming - not much softer than the layer above, facets rounding, no propagation in tests. 
Some factors contributing to the accident:

It was late in the day and we were looking for a quick, easy way back to the TH. We were on top of the ridge when we finished our investigation. Instead of backtracking to the easterly slope with 16 tracks on it, we descended an east-facing ridge so we could stick to sunny slopes if conditions allowed, or find a low-risk entry to the shaded slopes above the lake.
Complacency. We underestimated the slope we chose and did not leave an adequate margin for surprises. It was less steep than the terrain we'd been in during the investigation. While it was a mostly concave slope, it was still steeper than 30* and facing northeast. 
Recency bias: We over-emphasized our obs from that day - which included no signs of instability and unalarming test results. We underestimated the glaring but older crown 500 feet away.

It's been 14 years since I last took a ride. This better be the last one. It was a violent ride.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche
Instability Comments: None, except recent avalanches. We traversed 35* slopes adjacent to the 2/17 slide without whumpfing or cracking. Skiing the ridge above the slope that released did not produce any shooting cracks on the adjacent 38* slopes.

Observation Summary: We dialed back our decision-making today after seeing yesterday's reports of the persistent weak layer failures in the Flathead Range. We observed no  signs of instability and found boot-top powder on N aspects. One short run from 6300' on S facing aspects held an egg-shell thin or non-existent sun crust that did not interfere with skiing. Weather was mild, calm winds, with a high cloud ceiling and views of GNP. The sun even made a brief appearance around 1pm. Lovely day to be in the mountains.

Weather Summary: A day of good visibility with a cloud ceiling above Nyack mountain, periods of sun in the afternoon that caused some warming but nothing that would cause us to change our travel plans.

Steep slopes haven't moved since last observation on Friday and the neighboring Crystal Creek drainage as well as Cascadilla Creek have seen some traffic over the weekend. Despite this, no sign of new crowns or instability to note. Many sluff piles on steeps.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Warmer day - no winds - the sun did pop out and heat up the area after noon.
Observation Summary: Lots of tracks in Basin on NE facing aspects. We wanted to check out the SE facing aspects on this cloudy day to see how the last couple days sun had affected the snow. Found surprisingly unstable conditions on SE aspect - appeared to be the same issue weak layer we were finding on NE aspects yesterday. No signs of recent wet loose or dry loose slides. The sun did pop through the clouds around noon and began heating up the aspect considerably. No roller balls were noted.

Weather Summary: Clear all day with calm to light SW winds. Wind speeds increased slightly in the afternoon.Overnight lows hovered around 0 deg F and daytime highs reached near freezing at Marias Pass and the high-teens, low-20s deg F above 7000'.
Observation Summary: -Cold conditions around sunrise gave way to a nice sunny and relatively warm day.-We mostly traveled on sunny aspects and found 15-30 cm powder snow on top of the Feb. crusts. In locations below about 7000' this soft snow rested on top of the 1-2cm thick Feb 6th crust. Below this there was about 5-10 cm of facets resting on top the much stouter and thicker Feb. 3rd crust. These facets were more developed and weaker than what we found in the same layer in nearby terrain earlier this week.-Above 7000' feet the Feb. 6th crust thinnned out and disappeared. The stout Feb. 3rd crust however remained present on sunny aspects all the way to our high point at 8610'. On a west aspect at 8500' it was about 20 cm thick.
Instability Comments: Kicked off about a half dozen small wind slabs that were failing on facets underneath. These were about 8-16" thick but would only propagate 2-6'. Many other similar test slopes with wind slab were unreactive to cuts.
Route: From Marias Pass went up the Autumn Ck trail and then up/down the SW ridge of Little Dog

Weather Summary: Minimal wind affect. 8 degrees start of tour, 23 degrees on the ridge, 18 at the car upon return.  Winds were so shockingly calm, we didn’t want to talk about it. No snow transporting within our visual field.
Observation Summary: Snow stayed dry on both NW and NE aspects throughout the day down to 4800.’ That early February crust seems to be nicely buried in this region by a settled 6” of recent snow fall. Small cornice formation (see video) on north facing ridge lines. Observed surface hoar around 5800’ on a N aspect.
Instability Comments: We saw a few dry loose slides on NW facing slopes that were natural and just below a rock band. No cracking, whoompfing, or collapsing.
Route: Sledded up about three miles before peeling off and skinning up the gut of the Pinacle creek drainage to the top of tunnel ridge. Saw all the things and places from there with the good vantage and visibility.

Weather Summary: Clear sunny skies - no wind - very cold in the shade - solar radiation strong.
Observation Summary: Deep soft snow in northerly aspects.  Observed a dry loose avalanche on SE aspect - believed to be caused by solar radiation.  Found a deep weak and reactive layer at 55cm below the snow surface on a northerly aspect at 6200 feet.
Route: Accessed via rescue creek trail - climbed saddle south of Mt Penrose. Observed northern and southeast aspects.

Weather Summary: Unlimited visibility with views from Mt Cleveland to the Cabinets and down to the Missions.. No wind, great touring temps..

Route: Up road, up creek, up to the ridgetop, and reverse.  W, NW aspects mostly.

Weather Summary: Calm winds,  increasing clouds through the day.
Observation Summary:
A beautiful day at the brush ballet:

This area has 16-24" of unconsolidated snow seemingly well-bonded to the 2/3 crust(s)
Witnessed no obvious signs of instability
Observed minor natural sluffing in steep rocky terrain
Found small surface hoar on shaded aspects at mid elevations, did not find buried surface hoar in hand pits
Noticed plenty of evidence of recent NE winds as low as 5500'
Wind slabs were thin and stubborn along an upper elevation ridge
