Weather Summary: Clear and sunny. Super cold in the shade and warming and getting wetter in areas exposed to sun.
Observation Summary: We went into Moose Lake to ski the South face of the ridge North of the lake. As we approached that face, that aspect had a significant sun crust. We changed our plans and toured to the saddle North of Moose Peak. Climbed Moose Peak, and descended to a skiable area on the Northern aspect of Moose Peak. We dug a pit, and got ECTX and CTX results, with no failure or propagation. We skied the Northern aspect and it was good with 1-2 feet of soft snow on top of the crust. We also skied off the East face of Moose Peak which was starting to develop a sun crust and warming but not as bad as the South facing aspects.
Instability Comments: None observed.
Route: Toured in from Moose Lake to the saddle Northeast of Moose Peak. Skied off the north face from above that saddle then back up to near the summit of Moose Peak and skied the East Face of Moose Peak then back to the lake.

Weather Summary: Clear, calm, with warming trend.  Cold air pooled in Canyon Creek, keeping the pow cold.
Observation Summary: Observed no signs of instability on non-wind affected steep North-facing slopes  between 6400' and 5600'.  Turns on 35-40 degree pitches produced no sloughing or cracking.  Observed surface hoar up to 5mm in size in mid-elevation basins.   Found a breakable 2cm sun crust on steep southerly aspects and a much thinner sun crust on steep Westerly aspects.   Loose snow sloughing, whether dry or wet was  minimal on Big Slide.  Some drifts and mini-cornices were on the ridgetop from the recent NE wind event.
Instability Comments: Observed no signs of instability  except for limited loose snow sloughs on Big Slide.
Route: Toured from WMR base lodge at 4500' to 6400' via low elevation Southerly aspects and mid elevation Westerly aspects to access the cold, cohesionless powder on Northerly aspects.

location: Skier caught, carried, and injured - Marion Lake
Observation Summary:
Preliminary information. Will be updated as info is gathered. FAC plans to visit the site Monday. 2/19.
Summary: 
A skier triggered a slide that caught another member of the party below. The victim was carried through small trees and came to rest partially buried and injured. The party administered first aid and alerted EMS (InReach and cell phone call from ridge). EMS received the call at 16:50 and dispatched Two Bear Air. Two Bear inserted a Deputy and then extracted both the victim and the deputy from the scene. They transferred the patient to an air ambulance at Hungry Horse Reservoir (the patient was able to walk between helicopters). The patient was hospitalized overnight and treated for serious but non-life-threatening injuries.
Preliminary events and details:
Two parties of 2 left the Izaak Walton trailhead planning on summitting and skiing Mt. Adams on the crest of the Flathead Range. They left at different times and were unaware of each other's plans. The first party approached across Marion Lake with a descent off the south ridge of Pt. 7798 into Essex Creek. This large avalanche path has numerous names, including Marion's Big Mouth. The second pair approached up Essex Creek.
The parties met on the uptrack to Mt. Adams and skied the east face of the peak as a single group without incident (it had also been skied the previous day). They then climbed out of the Alameda Lake Basin in Essex Creek to the south ridge of Pt. 7798 above Marion Lake. The east-facing slope above the lake is sometimes known as Peanut Butter Bowl. It had been skied the day prior by at least two riders and by at least six riders earlier in the day of the accident. Another party skied steeper, southeast-facing slopes to skier's left about 90 minutes before the accident. That party reported conducting an ECT that broke but did not propagate at a storm interface roughly a foot below the snow surface.
On the ridge, the group of four split into 2 parties comprised of 1 member from each of the original groups. The two parties were equipped with radios, and avalanche rescue gear. One pair (Riders 3 and 4) planned to ski the concave, east-facing slope with existing tracks (Peanut Butter Bowl). The other pair (Riders 1 and 2) chose a line to skier's right that is northeast facing, with a steep, convex entry. Rider 1 skied the upper part of the slope with no incident. They stopped below the run.
Rider 2 entered the slope and triggered a large, soft slab avalanche. Rider 1 alerted the rest of the group of the slide and watched Rider 2 ski off the slab to skier's left. Unfortunately, this did not give them time to escape the debris rushing down at them. The debris carried Rider 1 roughly 50 meters through some small trees. They came to a rest mostly on the snow surface (legs buried). Rider 1 did not lose consciousness. Rider 1 was able to make brief radio contact with the rest of the group. They lost a ski and pole. The slide occurred at about 16:30.
Rider 3 stayed on the ridge where they had cell service. Riders 2 and 4 rode down to Rider 1, where they assessed the victim and planned a rescue. They decided Rider 2 would look for the victim's lost gear while Rider 4 assessed and stabilized the victim, whose symptoms included arm, chest, and back pain. After 5 minutes or so, the group decided against self evacuating due to the back pain and potential for further injury. They alerted EMS via InReach and cell phone.
EMS received notification at 16:50 and launched Two Bear Air. They inserted a deputy, then extracted the victim and deputy. They transferred the patient to an air ambulance at Hungry Horse Reservoir (The patient was able to walk between helicopters). The patient was hospitalized overnight and treated for serious but not life-threatening injuries. Riders 2, 3, and 4 self evacuated.
One member of the party cited several factors that contributed to the accident:

Confirmation bias - they'd skied a larger, more consequential line earlier in the day (Mt. Adams) and there were other tracks nearby.
Complacency and fatigue: it was late in the day, and the riders were tired. The 4 riders had already skinned over 7000 vertical feet.

The avalanche was a large avalanche (D2.5) that likely failed on old snow - either an interface between storms or a thin layer of facets a few inches above the Feb. 3 ice/ crust layer. The slab may have been composed partly of wind-drifted snow. It released at roughly 7100 feet on a convex, upper elevation, shaded slope. The debris stopped mid path, at about 6400 feet, after covering part of the uptrack set in Peanut Butter Bowl the day before and used by other parties the day of the accident.  
The avalanche forecast for Feb. 15-17  listed a Persistent Slab avalanche problem in terrain with these characteristics, with a Moderate (L2) avalanche danger. The party was riding one at a time, though Rider 1 did not stop in a safe zone. Their response to their accident was exemplary.
Two other near-misses occurred the same afternoon on shaded, upper elevation slopes in the next drainage north.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche

Observation Summary: -Snowpack on NW slope at 5600’ HS180cm: 50cm of storm snow atop early 20cm thick faceted February crust : ECTN with moderate force on 10cm thick 1mm facet layer sitting atop the February crust -Upper elevation wind slabs were unreactive on SE aspect -2mm surface hoar at middle elevation -South aspects had a sun crust forming after todays intense sunshine

Today our touring party observed clear skies and big views in the Flathead Range. The temperatures warmed quickly from -7F at the trailhead into the mid 20sF by 1pm @7000' and winds were calm. These clear skies and warming temperatures initiated loose dry, then wet loose avalanches that had the potential to trigger other larger deeper avalanches and we began to see natural activity around 11:30am.We observed no obvious signs of instability on shaded aspects until unintentionally triggering a large avalanche near the end of the day. This avalanche was a near-miss and we are very fortunate that nobody was caught or carried in it. This avalanche fractured with the third skier on the slope at the skier's skis and ran full track through a convoluted avalanche path. Our group made several mistakes that we hope to learn from:1. We did not complete a last minute check quick pit/ECT - we probably would have gotten a result that would have turned us back2. We were path dependent - it was the end of a long day and this was the most direct skiable path back to our sleds3. We were not familiar with the complete run - it wasn't a run we would have enjoyed much even without the avalanche and felt very unsafe in retrospectWe were aware of the persistent slab problem (thank you Mark and Sarah) and discussed it at the beginning of the day then tested and looked through it during our tour - except on that final slope. That was a key mistake that could have had dire consequences for us. Please keep heads up out there and don't get lulled into a false sense of security because you aren't finding the problem everywhere - that's the sneaky part about persistent weak layers.
Instability Comments:
- Solar aspects were warming throughout the day but it seemed that SE aspects specifically had the most WL activity by the end of the day
- Observed no obvious signs of instability on non-solar aspects until unintentionally triggering a large persistent slab avalanche on a N aspect at 7100' (avalanches tab for more info)
Route: Sledded in the Dickey Creek road then toured up South Dickey Creek, over Alameda Pass and into Marion Lake before returning to Dickey Creek.

Weather Summary: Clear skies, no wind, and sunshine.
Observation Summary:
We toured in the Skiumah drainage today after six weeks since FAC's last visit.

Access and snow coverage have improved, but still plenty of alder trees and other surface hazards in this drainage. Skinning and skiing on the trail were sporty 
In terms of observing recent avalanche activity, Skiumah gets 5 out of 5 stars.  In contrast, ski conditions were frozen avalanche debris with a little soft snow, which earned Skiumah 1 out of 5 stars. 
Temperatures stayed well below freezing, as the sun did not hit the basin until after 130pm. The surface snow that we traveled on stayed dry all day. We did not travel on snow that was getting solar input.
We observed fracture lines from a hard slab avalanche of both persistent slab and wet slab characteristics that likely failed during late January's atmospheric river.
Additionally, soft slab slides of loose dry characteristics were noted on steep aspects across the compass.
For comparison, there is a photo from February 2023 of the same cliff band we visited today which highlights both (2024)'s increased avalanche debris and relatively shallow snowpack.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability were observed today.

Observation Summary: W winds from monday dumped snow in rescue creek and made the snow surface incredibly variable. Cornices observed on pen's SE ridge. S aspect of pen above 6k' boasted consistent, stable snow.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking on a wind affected crust at low to mid elevations (4k'-6k') on E aspects at the bottom of the drainage. No collapsing experienced. Wind affected snow on SE ridge of penrose through middle/higher  elevations (6k'-summit). Mid elevations, S aspect of penrose grew wet and heavy through the afternoon but no major wet-loose concerns.
Route: Rescue creek to penrose summit. S, SE, E aspects observed.

Observation Summary: Sunny skies and clam winds. lots of solar input and warm temps in the afternoon. S aspects near rock starting to point release. Skiied avalanche terrain on south and west aspects. Old crowns at the bottom of cliffs and on the west upper face of Edwards. See photos

Weather Summary: Calm, clear/sunny, below freezing.
Observation Summary: On the top of slopes exposed to the recent NE winds the snow had been scoured away, in some spots even exposing the slick 2/7 crust.   Immediately in the lee of these locations there were hard wind slabs up to a foot thick.  These tapered quickly downslope  in more wind sheltered terrain.  It was easy to find non-wind affected boot-top settled pow to ski.   There was no sun crust on the low angle southerly slopes I travelled on.   Thin wind slabs I encountered would crack in a minor way around ski turns.   Observed no evidence of recent avalanches in nearby steep terrain.  Heard two collapses:  one in a shady alder field below 5000' near a creek, the other  near the pit location on a SW aspect at approx. 5800'.    Uncertain of how reactive the crust/weak layer combo is in other locations/aspects/elevations, but from the structure and the propagating test result, I chose to have fun on sub-30 degree slopes, enjoying the sunshine.
Instability Comments: 10-15cm recent wind slab cracked in a minor way in localized areas.   Cutting above skin track produced stubborn failures below the wind slab.  Heard two collapses:  one in a shady alder field below 5000' near a creek;  the other near the pit location at 5800', SW aspect, 25* slope.
Route: Skinned up Southerly slopes just east of WMR from 4600' to 6500'.  Descended similar slopes.

Weather Summary: Sunny all day, cool in the shade
Observation Summary: Wonderful day in the Cascadilla drainage, sunny skies all day and cold temps to keep things stable. Lots of stuff activity but no recent avalanche activity observed. Lots of old debris from the wet cycle we had, some of these places piled up deep this place went pretty big during the atmospheric river/warming events of a few weeks ago. Awesome 24 inches+ of dry snow on the sheltered aspect we rode.
Route: Skinned up powering road to access trail, up western rim skiing down Northern aspect.

Weather Summary: Clear, cold, cloudless day with stellar visibility.
Observation Summary: We observed slide-for-life conditions (bulletproof windslab) on SE-S aspects at mid-elevations (below treeline) in the southern Whitefish range. There was some cracking and a few isolated whumphs in the most developed wind slabs. Pockets of soft snow could be found in sheltered areas and the solar input didn’t seem to have as strong of an effect as we anticipated.
Instability Comments: Isolated collapsing and cracking in wind drifted snow slabs.
Route: South towards Big Slide and back on mostly SE-S-SW aspects mid-elevation band.

Weather Summary: Beautiful day. No clouds, no wind, and sun all day.
Observation Summary:
Clear skies, sunshine, and powder were the conditions today in Essex.

Air temperature stayed below freezing all day, however the solar input was strong. We observed some small roller balls, minimal shedding of snow off trees, and a slight sun crust forming at our descent at about 3 pm. 
One objective for today was to dig into upper-elevation shaded slopes to investigate the current persistent slab structure. 

The average depth of the recent storm snow was 55 cm. This new snow is settling, but not yet a distinct, cohesive slab. 
We found a "crust complex" comprising varying densities of crust and faceted layers. Please see the snowpack tab for more information. 
In both snow pits, we did not get propagation in our long column tests on north aspects. 


Given the high-pressure weather, we expect steep, sunny slopes to be primed for loose avalanche activity.
Instability Comments: Few signs of instability: small roller balls, an isolated small slab, and a little snow falling off trees.

Weather Summary: Clear, cold (single digits/teens), and calm our first two days (2/16-2/17). Even on Wolf Gun mountain (2/17), winds remained calm to light out of the east. Yesterday (2/18), things were a touch warmer (20s) with mostly cloudy skies. Winds remained calm in the valley bottom and snow surface remained cold fast and dry all day long.
Observation Summary: • 3 day patrol into the the head of Logging Lake. We traveled here to collect snowpack data, document recent avalanche activity, and restock/inventory two historic patrol cabins.• Travel from the river to the foot of Logging Lake was relatively smooth. Just barely enough snow to keep skis on your feet and minimal downfall to navigate. We hoped to find a deeper snowpack as we traveled further up the valley, but discovered the opposite. Like much of the park this season, this valley appears to  have been starved of snowfall. • On Saturday 2/17, we battled our way to upper elevations to assess avalanche conditions in the area. We ascended a west facing ridge on the front of the Livingston Range, and climbed up towards the false summit of Wolf Gun Mountain. We dug on a SW facing slope at 6900' in an area with uniform snowpack coverage. Many places closer to the ridge that we were climbing had significantly thinner coverage (25-50cm).• We discovered a weak snowpack structure with multiple layers of concern and depth hoar at the base of the pack. Our pit profile with details is attached. We speculated that a similar structure might be found at Stanton Mountain in the McDonald Valley right now. Both of these mountains are situated on the western front of the range and experience significant scouring by systems with strong Southwest Winds.
Instability Comments: Recent avalanches observed on three different mountains in the valley. Dry loose activity as well as one persistent slab.
Route: North Fork of the Flathead to the head of Logging Lake for two nights.

Weather Summary: Cool temps up high kept the snow light. Sun was out in force but saw no tree bombs or other signs of intense warming.
Observation Summary: We observed hoots, hollers, and sunshine giggles. We found evidence of just-barely a sun skin forming at lower elevations mid-afternoon. Southerly slopes were scoured and northern slopes only showed mild evidence of a barely formed denser wind layer that demonstrated no propensity for cracking.
Instability Comments: Surprisingly, we had trouble finding many signs of instability. Minimal sloughing (slow and not entraining new snow) present on steeper (around 35-40 degrees) slopes.
Route: Toured Fried Chicken, Baby’s Butt, Beaver Ponds, and Top of the World.

Observation Summary: Today we learned the love language of the snowpack around WMR was words of affirmation. We found no obvious signs of instability, including jumping on convexities along N aspects along ridgelines. We found small wind slabs that proved unreactive. After a warmup run on mellow slopes and no signs of instability we progressed into avalanche terrain as the day went on. Near the end of our tour we encountered a solo snowboarder in Canyon Creek SE of Beaver Pond walking without a transceiver that was lost and did not know how to get back to the resort. There wasn't enough time for this person to ski out the canyon back to lifts so one of us skinned to the ridge and initiated a rescue. A big thank you to the WMR Patrollers who responded and saved this person from a very cold night out. A good reminder to always be prepared and know where you are skiing before dropping in.

Weather Summary: -6C in the afternoon. Warm sunshine on solars and calm/light NW winds.
Observation Summary: It happened again... We got crusted on a holiday... Let's give a "warm" welcome to Cupid's Crust?• Ample solar won the fencing contest between the powder and the sun. Afternoon air temps hovered around -6C, however there was zero wind below ridgetop and the surface snow became hot pow despite the cool temps. Quality skiing persisted until descending below 5000' where the snow was sticky and wet. Your ski bases will thank you for not skiing below that elevation in GNP this season anyways.• Due to concerning weak snow above the 2/3 crust, we stuck to slopes less than 30 degrees today. No signs of instability observed during our travels. Recent avalanche activity (estimated 2/12-2/13)
Instability Comments: No signs on instability today other than recent avalanches from the past few days. estimated to have occurred 2/12 or 2/13
Route: Lake McDonald Lodge to Mount Brown Lookout.

Weather Summary: Cold, bluebird morning! Pleasant in the sun, but noticeably cold in the shade. Light northerly winds on ridgetops. Some clouds moved over the valley early afternoon.
Observation Summary:
We investigated mid-elevation slopes east of WMR for wind slabs deposited by northerly winds late yesterday (2/13) and overnight, and for persistent weak layers buried by recent storms. 

No evidence that the northerly winds left more than small, thin, isolated slabs, though the snow surface on NE and E facing slopes was often wind-textured and slightly denser than on other aspects.
We found persistent weak layers in 3 snow profiles (NE, E, and SE. These did not propagate in large column tests. We experienced one whumpf and no other signs of instability.

We had a stepping out mindset, which meant we were comfortable skiing and riding one at a time on slopes up to 35 degrees clear of likely trigger points (convexities, rocks, shallow spots) and terrain traps. 
The sun was glorious, and only the top inch or so of snow on open, southerly slopes got moist.
Instability Comments: One distinct whumpf on the shadowed side of a tree at about 6000 ft on a SE-facing slope. Shook some snow off trees 20-25 feet away. My guess is that this spot lacked the sun crust found in our profile on a SE slope.

Weather Summary: clear and cold this morning with gradual warming till mid afternoon. light/moderate winds were out of the north. clouds gradually increased to scattered by 1600.
Observation Summary: A pleasant day for backcountry travel. We found few signs of unstable snow at mid and upper elevations. Small pockets of wind slabs above tree line were mostly unreactive to ski cuts. Surface hoar was found buried 15-20cm below the snow surface on southerly aspects, but did not fail during instability tests around 7000'. Easterly aspects did not have any buried surface hoar layer around 6200'. Slopes were supportive on various aspects and elevations making travel much more pleasant than it was last week in the same area.
Instability Comments: Generally stable conditions on Southerly aspects. We did find small pockets of buried surface hoar on SE-SW aspects in areas around trees where winds couldn't knock down the SH. (see photo below)
Route: snowmobile to end of groomed road, crossed creek, and ascended peak. returned the same way.

Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies and intermittent very light snowfall all day. Total daytime accumulations were about 1” on the Canyon floor and 2-3” above 6000’.•	The well-advertised Arctic airmass pushed over first thing this morning. By sunset temperatures had only dropped into the low-teens °F above 6000’ elevation and the low-20s °F on the Canyon floor.
Observation Summary: •	Snowpack remains quite thin below 5500’ elevation. Snowfall from the past five days totaled about 6-8” with 0.40-0.50” water equivalent.•	Above 5500’, snow depths increase substantially with about 10-18” of soft powder snow resting on top of the truly remarkable Feb. 3rd crust which is over a foot thick in places.•	On sunny aspects there is a thin sun crust from Feb. 6th which is now buried about 10-16” down from the surface.•	There is a layer of facets located between the Feb. 3rd and Feb. 6th crusts that we’ll need to watch as the snowpack continues to develop.•	No signs of instability observed.•	Winds were light from the east to NE today with minimal wind loading. •	Dug a snow pit in the Shed 7 West path at 6650’, SE aspect, 30° slope. Snow depth here was 51”. A layer of facets exists between the Feb. 3rd and Feb. 6th crusts (pit profile). A steep temperature gradient was present in the upper half of the snowpack. Extended Column Test yielded ECTN 21 just under the Feb 6th crust and then CT 10 Q2 just above it.
Instability Comments: •	No avalanche activity observed.
Route: •	Hiking and ski tour on Snowslip Mtn to the upper starting zones in Shed 7 and Shed 5.

Observation Summary: What a great day to be out in the mountains! My partner and I skied canyon laps until the Flower Point chair closed today and observed no signs of instability. Next, we took a long walk to exit the ski area heading South down ridges and skiing southerly aspects.  By this point the forecasted weak Arctic mass had entered the area and chilly E winds started blowing. We observed some snow transport but no slab formation (yet?). While skinning a steeper convexity on a W aspect we heard one small collapse and saw one 8ish ft. shooting crack emanating from the skinner - these were our only observed signs of instability today.

Toured over to Skook face, Dorothy, and one Skook chute. No formal snow tests performed. Heavy snowfall at times. Interestingly, we were fairly sheltered from the wind which was really blowing at the top of the resort when we returned. Minor and manageable sluff slides prevalent with one small isolated soft slab release on a concave rollover that did not travel more than 15-20 feet. Group felt comfortable skiing slopes >30*.

Weather Summary: Overcast skies and light snowfall all day long. Snowfall rates were mostly 1cm/hour, but 2cm/hour at times. Mostly calm below ridgetop and light NE winds on ridgetop. Winds were not strong enough to transport snow. Snow surfaces at ridgetop appeared undisturbed by wind overnight as well. At least this is true up to our high point of 6500'. -10C air temp around noon at 6200',
Observation Summary: Dangerous day in the mountains. We stayed safe and had a great time at middle elevations by choosing to travel on slopes less than 30 degrees and not exposing ourselves to hazardous slopes overhead. Below 5000' we were faced with slopes that exceeded 30 degrees, but we felt that danger was lower at these elevations due to thin overall coverage and lesser recent snow totals.We observed one natural storm slab avalanche and remotely triggered another from the ridgeline. Details included within this observation.
Instability Comments: Significant snowfall in the last 48 hours is beginning to tip the scale. New and recent snowfall has amounted to a 50cm+ slab resting on top of faceted snow above the 2/3 crust. We did not observe feedback in the form of cracking or whumpfs, however we found straightforward feedback in our snowpit stability tests as well as in observed natural and human triggered avalanches.
Route: Highway 2 to Paola Ridge. Traveled mostly on E-SE-S.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Wet snow and mid 30s temps below about 3500-4000 feet. Steady S1-2 in Noisy Basin til mid afternoon. Winds calm or light in sheltered terrain; light w moderate gusts on exposed ridge.
Observation Summary:
Shiny, happy people in Noisy Basin today, because it was a storm day!

The overnight snow continued today, with high snowfall rates (1" an hour or more this afternoon).* 
Winds were Calm to Light in Noisy Basin, but Light to Moderate on Aeneas Ridge, with some snow transport. 
Upper elevation slopes were talkative: a collapse, shooting cracks, and 2 remotely-triggered avalanches (see avalanches). Except for the collapse, the talk was in leeward terrain that harbored soft slabs thickened and stiffened by wind (see problems). 
Mid elevation slopes were mostly quiet, with a right-side up snowpack that did not produce propagation or sudden failures in our tests.

*It snowed enough to erase snowmobile tracks up the road, but that's nowhere near enough to smooth out the deep, hard, punishing bumps. Ooof.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (specific)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: One distinct whumpf at 6900-7000 feet a southwest aspect. Shooting cracks (20-75 feet) on lee side of ridge crest, in soft slabs of drifted snow. 2 remotely-triggered slides.
Route: Noisy road by snowmobile, then ski up Noisy Basin to Aeneas Ridge near old microwave building. Return more or less same way.

Observation Summary: Traveled on and skied north/northwest facing aspects up to a high point of 6,000 ft for the day. Boot-supportable crust with 3-8" of snow on top. Lots of old wet loose debris from early Feb warm up. Observed isolated cracking under the skin track on steep convexities near the bottom of an open drainage, but surprisingly did not observe any wind affected snow on the ridgeline. Winds were generally calm with a cloud ceiling at ~6500' dropping to 4500' steadily throughout the day. Steady S1 snowfall from noon onwards. 25F at the car when we left, increasing to 32F at our 5pm return to the car.

Weather Summary: There were about 2 inches of medium density snow overnight and it snowed on and off all afternoon, accumulating to about 2 more inches. The snow started light in the early afternoon with moderate to heavy snow falling intermittently into late afternoon. Temps hovered around 20 degrees with no wind.
Observation Summary: We found no avalanche instabilities during our tour on north and south facing aspects other than manageable sluffing on steeper terrain. There was no wind loading and we skied close to a foot (at higher elevations) of medium-density pow on top of a crust on north facing aspects down to about 5,500 feet. The descent down Big Slide was surprisingly good the whole way down, but the skiing gradually became crustier as the elevation dropped. Low elevations remain thin and schwacky, but better than anticipated.
Instability Comments: No instability other than minor, manageable sluffing.
Route: Ghoulies > Top of the World > Kona >  Big SlideWe skied primarily north facing aspects up to 6,500 feet with one long south face run down Big Slide to the base area

Observation Summary: • Today's tour took us into middle and upper elevations in the Southern Whitefish Range. This area picked up an additional 8cm since yesterday. The new snow is slightly denser than in previous days (Fist+ hardness).• We poked into inconsequential upper elevation leeward slopes to search for wind slabs. Winds were light on ridgetop and not quite strong enough to transport any recent snow. Very minor saltation observed. No recent wind slab development observed.• On solar aspects we discovered a thin pencil hard sun crust (1.5cm thick) that had formed yesterday afternoon. Breakable, but developed enough to affect the skiing quality a little. This crust was very thin to non-existent above 6500', and also did not exist on sheltered areas with larger/tighter tree coverage.• We noted the present structure in the upper pack to be suboptimal/concerning, as we look ahead to an active weather pattern with significant snowfall expected in the coming days. On the underside of the sun crust, we observed a thin layer of near surface facets and 16.5 cm of fist hard snow sandwiched between the early Feb knife hard crust. (Picture attached).• We believe the early February crusts is primed to become the bed surface of a persistent slab avalanche problem early next week if the incoming weather forecast verifies, and we receive abundant snowfall.
Instability Comments: Loose Dry Problems persist on all aspects at middle and upper elevations.
Route: Afternoon tour out of bounds at WMR to Skook Face.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Most of the day was overcast except for occasional clearing periods with sun breaks. There was no precipitation or wind.
Observation Summary:
We visited the southern Whitefish Range to present at the Patrol Fund Level 1 class, complete our monthly beacon practice, and examine the near-surface conditions closely.

We thoroughly enjoyed visiting with the class. What a friendly group of students and instructors! 
The crust formed during the abnormally warm weather of late January, and early February is 42 cm thick on an east aspect at 6400 feet and 35 cm thick on an SE aspect at 6600 feet.
We could easily trigger loose snow slides in the 6-8" of unconsolidated dry snow resting on the crust on steep east aspects. Please see the avalanche tab for more information. 
At least seven loose dry slides occurred in the Skook Chutes over the past day or two. The avalanche tab has more information.
We are primed for a wet loose cycle once the sun and warmer temperatures occur.
Instability Comments: Loose-dry avalanches remain a concern, particularly in northern aspects where 6-8 inches of unconsolidated snow rest on a stout crust.

Observation Summary: 8-12 inches of fresh snow on a firm crust, on all aspects we skied (mainly E). Not firm enough to be totally supportive when bootpacking though... the crust made skinning on steeper sections equally challenging, despite the new snow.The gorgeous sunny day allowed us to get up high - 7800' - and we were treated with mostly light winds. Snow was certainly getting transported by occasional moderate gusts, but we had very minimal cracking and only on the most affected parts of the ridge climb.Coverage was low enough to be worried about sharks lurking under the fresh snow, even that high. We experienced sloughing on the short very steep sections we skied, but it was very manageable given the terrain we were in. Temps stayed cold throughout the afternoon despite the sunshine, although South faces certainly warmed up and lower elevations melted out quick. A rare treat of a day (this season)!

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Calm, Cool, and cloudy. Good conditions for preserving the powder.
Observation Summary: Our observations today agreed with the day's forecasted danger. We found 25cm of cohesionless dry surface snow primed for loose dry activity on a knife hard crust. Winds were very calm, temps were cool, and the sky was mostly cloudy, so we believe this problem will persist on all aspects at middle elevations and upper elevations into tomorrow.The mid-pack appeared very strong as a result of the big warm up earlier this month. We noticed some weak grains in lower 32cm of the pack, but we did not observe any results in the lower pack during our stability test.We conducted 3 stability tests at our pit site. (6510' sheltered north facing)• CT17 @ 136 (Q2- Resistant Planar)•ECTX•ECTP23 @ 130Pit profile attached.
Instability Comments: 25cm of recent snow is still mostly cohesionless in sheltered terrain, with a supportable knife hard crust beneath. Loose dry problem evident on steep slopes.
Route: Taylor Creek Road to the Hellroaring Saddle. We wrapped around to the north side of the ridge below Radio Tower to conduct a lesson in snow pit data collection.

Observation Summary: Dry loose avalanche with new layer that hasn't compacted yet. Watch for continued slab signals with more precipitation and time because the crust layer at 136cm forms a slick bedrock surface.
Route: NE aspect off of Hellroaring close to boundary marker. 6510' elevation. Slope surrounding pit site 34*.

Weather Summary: Low visibility, low wind, overcast. No precipitation
Observation Summary: CT21 @125cmCT27 @105cmECTN16 @125Recorded depths from ground up
Instability Comments: NA
Route: Left WMR, gained ridge towards hellroaring peak, observed south and northeast facing slopes. Middle elevation.  Performed pit checks in our avalanche 1 course.

Observation Summary: Today we skinned up the Jewel Basin road and then up to checkerboard via the northern ridge. We saw only a few signs of recent wind affected snow with no cracking or collapsing. The snow was mostly unconsolidated and we did not find any slabs although there was some sloughing on steeper sections. We dug a pit on a NW aspect at 6200' with non-propagating results, although several weak layers were observed. The skiing quality was excellent with some bushwhacking getting back to the road.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Observation Summary: Travelled up to Gibrlater Ridge. New snow ranged from 5cm-30cm on top of a supportable crust. Test slopes produced small dry loose slides. Snow on the ridge was being transported from the SW building small cornices. Stomping cornices produced dry loose slides running in the top 5 inches. We skied steep north facing terrain with manageable sluffing. Lower in the descent we skied 40 degree terrain where a rider triggered a soft slab that caught and carried him approximately 75 feet. He was neither buried nor injured.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche

Observation Summary: We found isolated cracking on wind affected aspects around 6k east and north facing. Cloudy skies with wind gusts into the 20s. We avoided wind affected slopes and slough from the dry loose problem when descending. Good riding to be found on planar slopes and cold temps (20s F) all day.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Temps: 20 when leaving the car, 34 when returning (3pm).  Sun was in and out throughout the day.  Calm.
Observation Summary: A thin layer of hoar atop the storm snow made for some fast turns. The storm snow was not cohesive.  Some sloughing observed in steeper sections.
Instability Comments:
Saw some old debris (covered by recent snow) on various aspects.
Only instability noted today was human related and involved sliding sideways, backwards, and definitely downhill whenever traversing or angling up steeper slopes with the fluffy storm snow resting atop a stout slippery bugger of a crust.
Route: Sledded up the road, which was thin (and by thin, I mean there was pavement involved) to Stahl creek, which wasn’t quite as filled in as we might have liked. Travelled up to the summit and down the south aspect of the peak.

Observation Summary: A fun tour trying to once again fight the elkweed for the goods. Them bushes can't hold us back!! Mostly calm day with little wind, light snowfall, some brief periods of sun in the afternoon. No big evidence of slides anytime recently. Above 6000 feet coverage is getting gooood. Below that, hit or miss on deep to shallow.
Instability Comments: Much sluff but no major slabs from the storm
Route: Up access road to Strawberry Lake trail, jumped off at 2nd big switchback and beat our way to the snow.

Observation Summary: We enjoyed a comedic battle today with a slick crust and overhead alder. At some points today both the crust and the alder won.. but that didn't stop us from enjoying 15-30cm of dry powder. The fun was slightly dampened at the snow pit - 6000' SE  HS:130 ECTP23 70cm down on facet crust combo. My crew stayed off the main attraction and enjoyed more bush bashing and lesser slope angles. Snow at the ridge line stiffened ever so much before dropping into dickey creek. We did not observe cracking or cornice development at the ridge top. We pulled off impressive feats of flexibility, and reaction time skiing down the trail back to Marion Creek TH.

Skied N and E facing slopes off WMR. Found 3-5" of new cohesionless snow on top of a firm boot-supportable crust. Sluff ran quickly on steep pitches >35 degrees but was easy to manage. On and off snow flurries produced little accumulation. Winds generally light, shifting from calm SW winds to light NE winds as the day went on. Saw no signs of wind transport.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Glop line was around 4000 ft today.. not too bad getting above that but It’s too warm for February :(
Observation Summary: Good dry snow above 4500 feet.  20-30 cm capped  the crust at middle and upper elevations.. creek is definitely still open all the way but it’s skiiable as long as you’re… prudent
Instability Comments: No signs of wind loading, no cracking, not even sluffing really.
Route: Observed SW, W and NW  aspects… and to the two guys with the sled.. definitely appreciated the use of your skin track.

Observation Summary: We had a lovely day out in the Swans with 8+" of stable new snow and even a brief sucker hole of blue skies. We skinned/skied SW, W, NW, and N aspects between 4k' and 6900' on slopes up to 40* and primarily only observed localized (around skis) cracking in the surface 2-4" of snow. We observed longer shooting (~50') cracks twice during our tour within the surface 4" of snow and concluded that these were either due to a touch more wind (at ridgetop) and warmer temperatures (at 5300' post-small storm slab avalanche) consolidating surface snow. We couldn't feel the late January crusts skiing, but still battled it skinning... and there appears to be a double crust formed above the late-January crust from the mid-week sunshine on SW and S aspects (didn't get on anything E today).

Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy conditions limited our views of the surrounding peaks. S1 snowfall rates until 1230pm.  No winds throughout the day, even at upper elevations.
Observation Summary:
Compared to my last tour to Mount Brown in December, today felt more like winter. 

Ski turns in the 3 to 5 inches of powder on a supportable crust from 7400 feet to 5900 feet, may have been the best turns we've had all season.
Below 5800 feet, surface obstacles encouraged us to ski down the trail to 4700 feet.  From there, back to the trailhead we alternated between snowplowing on dirt and hiking.
Wind drifts were relatively small and non-reactive along the ridge. Recent northern winds likely eroded the typical (southwesterly) drifting.
As reported, southern aspects had a stout crust under the new snow. However, we did not observe sluffing or experience challenging skinning conditions.
Below 4700 feet, snow depths ranged from zero snow to 35 cm (13 inches).
Instability Comments: No instability was observed.

Observation Summary: General stability but limited visibility kept us from skiing above treeline.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability.
Route: Toured up into the Sperry Chalet basin in hopes of skiing alpine terrain with the stability. Poor visibility kept out party from venturing above treeline where the flat light turned disorienting.

Weather Summary: Light snowfall throughout the day, up to S2 at times. Winds were light, but tapered off as the tour progressed.
Observation Summary: Edge control was the name of the game today. On descents a light touch was all that was needed, however, ascending required hips forward and edges engaged. There are nice turns to be had out there, it is just a matter of finding the right aspect. It was nice to see new snow falling throughout the day and attempting to bury the troubles of the last few weeks..
Instability Comments: no cracking, whumphing, only collapsing was observer trying to skin up steep angle slopes.
Route: Goulee ->Micho Mine ->Top of the World ->Canyon Floor

Weather Summary: Surprise sunshine and blue sky day
Observation Summary: Sunshine, no wind, minimal sluffing. We weren’t on the S side long enough to observe how much warming occurred or see much shedding happen, though there’s not much snow left in trees to shed.
Instability Comments: ECTP 24, breaking 85cm down on basal facets at the base of the snowpack, at 6200’ on a N aspect.  We observed no signs of instability outside of our pit.
Route: Up to Marion, up S aspect, down N aspect

Weather Summary: Overcast morning transitioned to scattered/few clouds by late afternoon. Temps were around 28 degrees F at 930am this morning in Polebridge. Forgot to take a temp reading later in the day, but they well surpassed the freezing mark in areas exposed to the sun. Lots of trees dripping along the Bowman Lake Road.
Observation Summary: Surprised again by a sunny afternoon in the park. Enjoyed a cross country ski with an old pal up in the North Fork. Road conditions were cold, crisp, and excellent in the morning, and a little soft by late afternoon. Thick overcast dominated the valley until about 12:30pm when the clouds rapidly dissipated revealing nice views into the high country. Did not note any recent activity in the Bowman Valley other than recent wet loose slides way in the back of the canyon towards the divide. Was surprised to not see any action in the new snow on the steep southerly aspects of Rainbow or Numa despite ample sunshine and light winds. Was also unable to get eyes on any older crowns on these peaks from last weeks cycle. Either the new snow has disguised any older crowns or things stayed a bit cooler in this part of the world? We could only speculate.
Instability Comments: Wet loose avalanche activity viewed from afar. Hard to say if these were triggered by yesterday's warm up or today's warm up. Both days had ample afternoon sunshine and light winds.
Route: Polebridge RS to Bowman Lake.

location: Dorothy Mountain - Whitefish Range - WMR sidecountry
Weather Summary: Very warm in the sun, cooler in the shade. Clear skies in upper elevations with low ceiling. .
Observation Summary: We observed what we perceived to be surprisingly warm conditions at 6800 feet partly due to the intensity of the solar radiation. Clear skies and minimal cloud coverage. Trees were dripping water at 6800 feet after 12pm. The 6-8in of recent snow on north-facing slopes remained moderately light while snow on East and South facing slopes was heavy and wet.
Instability Comments: Southern Facing slopes which were picking up solar radiation displayed micro wet loose movement similiar to dry loose sluff when skiied. 6-8 inches above crust was heavy and showed a desire to clump and form rollerballs. We were expecting to manage dry loose sluff but were surprised to find ourselves concerned about wet loose.
Route: Smokey Range Trailhead. Observed all aspects. Max elevation 6800feet

location: Anna Creek: Swan Range East Side
Weather Summary: Scattered snow showers
Observation Summary:
We spent 2 nights at the Anna Creek cabin in the Swan Range. Due to a lean snowpack year, we were not able to access middle and upper-elevation terrain via snowmobile. 

4 to 6 inches of new snow fell between 2/7 and 2/9. Today (2/9), snow squalls produced snowfall rates of 2 inches an hour. We drove out of the precip on our way north today. 
On 2/8 we easily triggered Dry Loose avalanches in 40+ degree terrain. Slides ran fast and far on top of a hard crust. These were specific to northerly aspects. 

Sluffing was easily mitigated with ski cuts. Steeper does not equal better. Sluffs would clean out all of the recent dry snow and leave just the crust to descend. Lower-angle slopes were more enjoyable. 


On 2/9, roughly 4 inches of new snow fell overnight. We observed signs of wind-drifted snow near ridgelines above 6500 feet. 

Triggered shooting cracks up to 20 feet in length. Slabs of wind-drifted snow rest atop a layer of facets above a crust. This structure will make them slow to gain strength.
Slabs of wind-drifted snow averaged 8 to 10 inches thick and were soft. Today, these were easy to manage with ski cuts, or by tucking in lower down on the slope. I don't anticipate these slabs to be "easy to manage" in areas that received more snow, or as slabs continue to thicken.



media to come.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)

Weather Summary: Clear and colder highs yesterday low 30s at valley floors, 10 degrees this morning with BB about 3-4 inches new dry and light snow. Bluebird day today with no wind.
Observation Summary: Loose dry avalanches
Instability Comments: Recent loose dry avalanches after 6-8 inches of new snow over the last two days . Temps 10 degrees at valley floor this morning. Quite a difference a week makes. Loose wet slides occurred last week in nearly identical locations.
Route: East Flat top and Singleshot Mountain

Observation Summary: Lots of older wet slides from the warm days last week. Some minor dry loose slides as well from what looked like either today or yesterday. Lots of sluffing on the steep stuff.

We noticed evidence of wind blown snow at ridge lines, but didn’t see any evidence of instability. With the warm temps and new snow, we were expecting to see wet loose activity in the afternoon, and saw rollerballs, sluffs, and avalanches on S facing aspects.

Weather Summary: •	Scattered clouds with no precipitation and pretty much calm winds at all elevations.•	Daytime high air temperatures reached 40°F on the Canyon floor, the mid-30s °F at 6400’, and right at the freezing mark at 7000’.
Observation Summary: •	Snowpack is thin and patchy or non-existent on sunny slopes below 5000’ elevation.•	Recent snowfall totals from the past few days ranged from 2-3” on the Canyon floor and 6-8” above 6000’.•	This recent snow was cooked today on sunny aspects and a new surface sun crust began to form as this terrain became shaded later in the day.•	Noted a few recently formed 6-12” thick wind slabs above 7000’, mostly on easterly aspects. These were unreactive to ski cuts.•	Underneath the recent snow we have a thick new crust due to last week’s warmup and the subsequent cool down over the weekend. This was supportable in skis and sometimes boots. We measured it at 6-7” thick around 6000’ on sunny aspects.•	Dug a snow pit in the Shed 11 path at 6400’, south aspect, 29° slope. Snow depth here was only about 20” and basically consisted of three layers: the recent snow, the recent crust under that, and then wet grains to the ground. Extended Column Test yielded ECTP 21 and 19 right underneath the crust (pit profile).•	Dug a second snow pit in the uppermost reaches of Shed 10 at 7300’, ENE aspect, 30° slope. Snow depth here was 36” with only about 3” of recent snow resting on top of a 2” thick, 1F-hard crust formed over the weekend. Snowpack was dry throughout. ECTN14 just under the crust and CT12 Q2 on same layer.
Route: •	Hiking and ski tour on the south face of Running Rabbit on the Shed 11/10.7 ridge to the weather station. Then upward over the south summit of Running Rabbit above Path 1163, across uppermost Shed 10, and out Grizzly Gulch.

Weather Summary: Thick stratus hung over West Glacier this morning. While driving towards Marias Pass, the stratus began to break up east of Coal Creek/Crystal Creek. This was still true late in the afternoon when I returned back to the west side.
Observation Summary: If you haven't heard yet... the snowpack across every mountainous area in our state is presently well "below normal" (NRCS 1991-2020 Median) . Here in the northwest corner of the state however, we should consider ourselves lucky in some respect; At 69%, the Flathead and Kootenai Basins have a higher percentage of normal SWE than any other watershed in Montana. Sadly, our neighboring mountains east of the divide are in last place of this winter's race for water. Combined, the Sun-Teton-Marias Basins are recording 42% of normal.Zoom into this grim picture even further and you'll notice that the Many Glacier SNOTEL is currently reading 16% of normal! Only 9 miles to west on the other side of the divide, the Flattop Mountain SNOTEL is at 70%. Anyone who has driven Highway 2 recently to the east side has noticed this abrupt change as you travel between Essex and East Glacier. I overheard on an NPR report this morning that the snowpack at Marias Pass is presently the lowest it has been in the last 90 years... I had not been east of Essex yet this winter, so I felt compelled to go see for myself. I took a short afternoon ski up the Autumn Creek West Trail to find out what a 90 year low looks like.
Instability Comments: The combination of warm afternoon sunshine and calm winds was initiating several small (D1) loose snow avalanches on upper elevation south facing slopes.
Route: Short afternoon tour up the Autumn Creek West Trail. (4600' to 5200')

Observation Summary: •Coverage in the Middle Fork is scant below 5500' depending on tree canopy (in relation to snowfall intercept). Our descent line took us through a burn scar where there was a deeper base which allowed us to conservatively ski to 4500'.  •The supportable crust from our last warm up existed to our high point of 7400'•Recent snow (4-8") made for enjoyable riding on low angle terrain. We did ski a steeper run (38*/SE) and could feel the underlying crust when turning. •The backdoor cold front that came in Sunday had buffed/scoured upper ridgelines and deposited dense wind slabs along atypical aspects (W). These windslabs (16") were unreactive and stomping cornices was like trying to stomp a curb.•Winds remained calm (although southeasterly when present) and sunshine prevailed throughout our tour. Overall, temps hovered right around the freezing mark but surface snow (up to 7400') on solar aspects was beginning to warm. •During our descent, we triggered a point release, wet, loose avalanche (D2) that ran half-path (2000'). It's origin was slow and steady as it gradually scoured away the recent snow and slid readily on the crust. •Boot-top, cold powder was experienced all the way to the railroad tracks and skied best in low angle terrain.
Instability Comments: Generally stable conditions prevailed. Recent snowfall began to warm on solar aspects, but remained dry on northerlies. Numerous point release, loose dry sloughs (D1) were observed in steeper, shaded terrain, while point release wet, loose avalanches (D1-D2) were observed on solar prone aspects.
Route: We climbed a NE ridge from 3800' to 7400' and descended a SE ridge.

Observation Summary: Skied 2-4" of dust on a stout crust, with southern aspects holding more snow than northern ones. Experienced minor sluffing on steep test slopes. Wind transport of new snow seemed minimal, with snow still on branches near ridgelines. Otherwise no other signs of instability. A light on/off snowfall increased in intensity on our mid-afternoon exit, with a half inch accumulating on the cars in the parking lot.

Sledded up Jewel Basin road to the cabin and then toured up to the radio tower. Sidehill skinning was sporty to say the least with the new snow sluffing off on the rock hard crust below. New snow ranged between 4-8” and was variable and lightly wind blown. On the radio tower ridge we observed cross loading and uphill cornices across the entire ridge. The drop in to Picnic Lakes was very low vis and icy as all the snow had sluffed off. The Lower elevation/angle skiing into Black Lake was much better.

We noticed considerable melt had occurred at the trailhead and this was no different as we made our way to the turnoff for Elk.  It would appear that the warm temperatures and rain in the last week reduced the snowpack to very little on all aspects up to 7000' in this area.  We noted many exposed rocks and trees on the majority of aspects of Elk and Sub-shields that typically are far beneath the surface by this time of year.  Anyhow great day for a walk to Ole Creek.
Instability Comments: None observed
Route: Typical Elk Mountain approach from trailhead to the south on Fielding Trail.  Intent was to summit Elk and ski the the SW gully off of the summit. Observed E, W and S aspects predominantly.

Weather Summary: Cool, cloudy, and calm day in the West Glacier region/McDonald Valley today. Light snow showers (S1) entered the valley around 2:00pm. By 3:30 rates picked up to S2 and are holding steady there all the way to canyon floor elevations. Roughly 2cm has accumulated so far as of 5:30pm.
Observation Summary: Spent the afternoon continuing clearing efforts up the Sperry/Mount Brown Trail and assessing general snow coverage and access conditions. We are completely clear of downfall to 4800' aside from one large tree that was a bit much for my saw (easy step over/walk around). Might spend one more day up there soon to try and get things tidied up to 6000 or so. There was quite a bit of downfall so far this year, so for those of you who dealt with that recently, you will be glad to see a clean slate next time you come up. At least until the next big wind event...
Instability Comments: Good stability at lower elevations, but much danger by proximity to many unburied objects.
Route: Lake McDonald Lodge to about 4800' on the Mount Brown Lookout Trail.

Observation Summary: Headed to Jewel to see if the inch of SWE reported on the Snotel was accurate. An inch seemed a bit optimistic as general new snow depth near Camp Misery in the morning was 4-6 inches of dry snow. Underlying crust pencil hard. Summited Radio Ridge just as west winds and clouds moved in, followed by moderate snow the rest of the day. The recent NE winds had stripped the upper east faces of all new snow, leaving bulletproof entrances and interesting ridge top sculptures. Lower faces and all terrain down to Black Lake had 4-6 inches new snow on the crust. Decent skiing but still bottoming out. Surface sluffing but new snow bonded well to crust. Upper SW slopes held the deepest snow, loaded from the recent NE winds. Snowed all afternoon with strong ridge top winds out of the west. Each run got a little better and sleds had 2 inches on them by 3 pm.

Firm crust, no whoomph, for skinning with increasingly more dry-fresh sitting on top as we ascended. Three debris piles above 5000 ft from apparent wet-loose a few days old. We observed crowns along the largest path suggesting it reached a deeper, weak layer. This large debris pile came from the SE aspect of penrose. Two smaller piles ran down from the N aspect of the drainage. Cornices stood on the ridge of this lee aspect.
Instability Comments: Dry-loose of greatest immediate concern. New snow throughout the day continued to bury a recently formed week layer.
Route: Rt. 2 to 6000 feet N aspect in western-most drainage of rescue creek. Traversed to ski E aspect.

Observation Summary: Today was like an unexpected April pow day…very low expectations but we still had some fun turns. The slide on Dorothy’s may have already been reported but it was likely a wet slab that broke free during the warm up of last week. It was in the skiers right bowl and the debris pile was much larger than expected. We could see a crown in the top of the bowl, and I would say the size was D 2.5-3. We didn’t dig a pit as everything was pretty much locked up, and we skied slopes above 35 degrees with some slough to manage. Despite the winds on the front of WMR, we experienced minimal wind or wind affected snow throughout our tour. Probably 4-5” of new snow on top of the crust at most elevations.
Route:
We went to Skook and Dorothy’s with low expectations but we’re pleasantly surprised with some pow skiing (albeit with a crust underneath). It was like an unexpected April snowstorm with below freezing temps.  

The slide on Dorothy’s may have already been reported but it was the skiers right bowl off the top. The debris pile was much larger than expected and based on the character of the pile, it was a loose, wet slab that broke free before the cold front of last night. We could see a crown at the top of the bowl and I would estimate size being D 2.5-3. See photo. 

We didn’t dig a pit as everything was pretty well locked up. Despite strong wind effect on the front of WMR, we experienced minimal wind impacted snow on our tour. 

Everything was

Weather Summary: Very light snowfall prevailed all day, but only amounted to a trace. Cloud cover began to break up around 4:00pm. Calm winds all day. Even at our high points of ~6800 on the ridge. Not even a breath up there. Observed some recent small drifts in the new snow at ridgetop to suggest that light winds might have been present overnight.
Observation Summary: Day off investigating the skiing on the other side of the river. Expectations were low today, but they were also exceeded. We rejoiced to find measurable amounts of new snow, cool temps, and a nice windless day in the Middle Fork.• If I worked for the Middle Fork marketing team, which I don't, I'd tell you tales of surfy pow and the deepest three inches we've ever seen. Instead, I'll tell it like it is. It's still pretty rugged out there; we've got a shallow snowpack and messy woods. The good news is that things don't look too far from normal up high.  We've got almost two meters of snow on the ground above 6200'. • Today, we found a stout supportable crust that aided our travels through the brushy bottoms, and a skiff of new snow from the last 48 hours to gift us with a good day of skiing. No signs of instability aside from some minor sloughing experienced on slopes steeper than 35 degrees. Would expect more significant sloughing possible on slopes that are continuously 35+ degrees for long distances.
Instability Comments: Snowpack stability was good today. Manageable sloughing on upper elevation slopes steeper that 35 degrees.
Route: Highway 2 (3800') up to Tunnel Ridge (6800)'. Skied two laps up high before exiting back the way we came. First lap on a west facing slope, and second on northeast facing.

Weather Summary: Despite the sun trying to peek out throughout the day, it kept snowing lightly for most of the day accumulating an additional trace of new snow (so deep right now!) with pleasant below freeing temperatures (finally) and not even a hint of wind (lovely). The sun started to poke out around 4pm!
Observation Summary: We enjoyed a pleasant day up Marion with fun light new snow on a stout generally supportable crust. We saw a variety of old avalanches, triggered a variety of small predictable sluffs, and giggled a whole lot. Skinning/skiing conditions were variable and mildly technical due to the stout crust.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability noted aside from an unexpected propagating result in a stability test (mentioned in snowpack section)
Route: Up from Essex via the Marion Lake trail then across the lake and nabbed a couple laps off the shoulder before exiting via Essex Creek.

Observation Summary: From travels on 2/4 and 2/5 I found no evidence that slab avalanches or any sizable wet loose slides had run on Big Slide during the last extended warm spell.   Under the 10-15cm of recent snow the crust was smooth and supportive.   I chose to take a day off on 2/6.  On 2/7 I returned to Big Slide to find that most  of the face below the rocky band had been raked by roller balls up to 6" in diameter, and at least one D1 point release avalanche .    (No surprise, given Tuesday's sun and warm temps.)   Luckily,  Wednesday morning was  much sunnier than predicted.  At 10:00am the surface sun crust was    supportive from 6200' to 5200' on a SE aspect.  Skied like a groomer.  At noon, the sun had softened the    2/6 crust to a pleasantly ignorable condition.   Skied pitches of 30-40 degrees in less rocky (and thus non-rollerballed) areas.    Descended into the cloud deck at about 5200' at 12:15pm.    On an open, west-facing slope in the cloud I found a breakable surface crust from 5200' to 4800'.   Below that there was just barely enough snow in the forest to make my way out on skis.Investigation of the substantial Early February crust was limited to ski pole probing.   Did not dig to investigate what may lurk under that crust.On shady NE-facing slopes the surface snow was faceted and made for mildly challenging skinning given the slick crust it sat on.

Weather Summary: Cloudy skies, seasonal mild temperatures and light snow was enjoyable
Observation Summary: The snowpack at upper elevations locked up nicely and it was fun to ski the fresh 1-2 inches on smooth steep surfaces. Traveling was difficult at upper elevations because of the firm surface below the new snow. I always had to find lower angled slopes to ascend and at one point booting was deployed to gain elevation.
Instability Comments: Small amounts of new snow would sluff or roller ball steeper terrain,  but never piled up or ran fast.
Route: Was hoping to take advantage of the Snowpack finally freezing at upper and mid elevations so the plan came together staying up high and skiing S facing  burn zones heading east from WMR.  All of the S facing burn runs were smooth with no avalanche debris in them. Each run skied liked the late season Spring storm that feels like it keeping the season alive! The new snow 1-2 inches of rather dense pow roller balled/ sluffed on steeper slopes, but it was never enough to pile up.  On the final decent I skied from 6300' to 4600'and enjoyed the supportable again Spring like conditions to a Stoltze road and xc home.

Weather Summary: light snow fall with little wind. Sun have little effect on the snowpack
Observation Summary: At the elevation that we dug our pits, we observed a robust crust under the 5cm of new snow. The near surface crust was thicker on our SE pit. The upper portions of the snow pack was starting to refreeze and decrease in water content. New snow seemed heavy and was binding well to the supportable curst.
Instability Comments: No instabilities observed.
Route: We skied out of the WMR boundary at Taylor creek road. We left the road and gained the ridge to 6,500 ft below Hellroaring peak where we dug pits on the SE and NE aspects of the ridge. From our pits, we descended back to the resort onto Hellfire ski run.

Weather Summary:
Rain on the drive to the trailhead. Mixed precipitation turned to snow as we gained elevation on sleds. Low clouds all day limited our visibility. Snow flurries on and off throughout the day. Easterly winds along the continental divide.
Rain/ snow line climbed during the day, from roughly 4000' to 4600'
Observation Summary:
Our sled and ski tour to the Skyland area today started and ended in the rain. However, the middle of our day included some nice turns in new snow.

3 inches of new dense snow at 6100 feet and 4 inches of dry snow at our high point of 7700 feet.
Skiing was creamy on a supportable crust. Described best: "cream cheese on burnt toast."
Blowing easterly winds were forming thin wind slabs at the ridge of the Continental Divide.
The stout crust was supportable on most surfaces and ranged from 15 to 20 cm deep. 
We managed harmless sluffing of the new snow on the crust on steep slopes. 
Visibility was limited, so our observations of recent avalanche activity was limited. See the avalanche tab for more details.
Instability Comments: We experienced harmless sluffing of the new snow on the underlying crust.
Route: End of Skyland Road, west-facing slope of the Continental Divide

Weather Summary: Good visibility.  Mild.
Observation Summary: Old wet slab debris from early in the week.
Instability Comments: none observed that occurred today
Route: Up summer trail with views of headwall above lake.  Travel was limited to lower elevations.  Avalanches  observed we’re around 5500 ft on N/NW aspects

Weather Summary: There was a little blue sky in the morning and overcast in the afternoon. No wind and most of the day was above-freezing air temperatures.
Observation Summary:
We headed to the Rescue Creek drainage today, looking for evidence of the past week's avalanche cycle.

Travel into the basin was fast on the supportable crust that was knife-hard to 4600 feet. 
Above 4600 feet, the crust became gradually less supportable and skinning was challenging.
Using binoculars, we spotted multiple fracture lines on an NE aspect approximately at 6,700 feet (see avalanche tab for more).
Wet avalanche activity was observed on many slopes.  Most appeared to be wet loose (soft slab) debris rather than wet slab avalanches.
Runnels dominated most of the surface snow.  They were spotted on every slope aspect and initiated from the tops of ridgelines.
One seasonal glide crack appeared to be opening on a SE aspect, at 7000 feet.
Instability Comments: No recent instability was observed, except that the surface crust became less supportable as we climbed in elevation.

Observation Summary: Traveled into various slopes in Firebrand Pass.   Weather and snow pack reflected a typical spring backcountry day with no significant findings:-Thin snow pack throughout the travel region had a good overnight freeze which produced a supportable crust which made travel easy-Temperatures did get above freezing during the corn cycle in the afternoon.   No wet slides observed today or evidence during of any activity during this last weeks avalanche cycle-We did dig into the snow pack on a SE aspect and discovered rounding of the facets-

Weather Summary: Temperature inversion with balmy conditions early in the morning at mid and upper elevations. Light wind kept the snow slightly cooler in the open compared to sheltered terrain.
Observation Summary:
We visited the southern Whitefish Range to examine a natural slab avalanche on the popular side-country run Skook Face.

The avalanche occurred on the skier's right side of the broad Skook Face, where the slope angle pushes 40 degrees. Debris was large enough to bury or injure a human. Please see the avalanche tab for more information.
We noted multiple slabs and loose slides in the Skook Chutes from recent warming. The generally thin snowpack limited the slides' travel distance and destructive size. Please see the avalanche tab for more information. 
The thin snowpack on the Skook Face was an eye-opener. The lack of a well-defined cornice line on Skook Ridge at this time of year is unheard of.
Instability Comments: I initiated small, harmless rollerballs on steep slopes with wet surface snow.

Observation Summary:
Updates for those interested: 
3k-4K: some nice soul spent their day skiing and cutting trees up just past the mt brown junction. Thank you. Easily skinnable with sometimes spicy ski.
4K-5k: barely skin able, we walked down
5k+: semi supportive and semi skiable until you wrap around and it goes due south. Snowpack here is totally isothermic, about 120 cm, and train track like skiing. Roller balls otherwise nothing much today.

Weather Summary: Yesterday temperatures reached well above freezing even above 8,000 ft.
Observation Summary: Presence of wet slab avalanches
Instability Comments: 55 degrees in St Mary yesterday and several loose wet slides were observed.
Route: Large avalanche which stepped down to the ground yesterday on the east side of Glacier NP. Estimate the crown to be several feet thick on the left side of the frame.

Observation Summary: Avalanches observed at great distance through telescope (and trees).  Photos graciously supplied by a friend who lives nearby this location.
Route: Avalanches observed at great distance through telescope (and trees).  Photos graciously supplied by a friend who lives nearby this location.

Observation Summary:
Temps were around 45, mostly sunny skies, minimal wind, very low/no snowpack throughout the region. 
Walking > Skiing sometimes.

Weather Summary: Lovely day out with direct sunlight most of the day. Water production at the snow surface slowed down/shut off with increasing cloud cover at 2:15pm.
Observation Summary: We observed wet, warm, and shallow spring-like conditions throughout our tour and experiments today.
Instability Comments: We experienced a few local collapses while ascending and descending most likely from failure of the well developed (yet rounding) facets below the decomposing late December crust.
Route: We toured (hiked and skinned) from the Shed 7 pullout at 4400' to our high point at 6500' on SW, S, and SE aspects along the Shed 7 East ridgeline. We chose to avoid the avalanche path runout areas as much as possible given the potential hazard for large destructive natural avalanches and stuck to the thick timber. We ended up bootpacking to ~5500' before switching to skis.

Weather Summary: Valley inversion with isolated clouds. Light winds kept the snow surface cold, but ambient air temp was above freezing once we left the valley floor.
Instability Comments: Snow surfaces not recieving direct sunlight were locked up tight. Solar aspects were softening near the surface. Felt like a spring snowpack while traveling above 6500'.
Route: Parking area to Camp Misery to Noisy Basin SNOTEL site. Ascend to Aeneas Ridge and return to sleds at SNOTEL site

Weather Summary:
•	Scattered clouds with no precipitation and light winds from the SW.
•	Inverted air temperatures in the morning with a superficial refreeze of the surface snow up to about 5000’ elevation. Overnight temps only dropped to the high-30s °F above 5000’.
•	Daytime highs reached the mid-40s to low-50s °F on the Canyon floor and the mid-40s °F above 6000’ elevation.
Observation Summary:
•	No new avalanche activity observed today save for one little D1 wet slab we triggered while skinning up the ridge. This occurred at about 6800’ on a SW aspect, 38° slope.
•	Observed a more recent looking crown that likely ran yesterday on the east face of Snowshed Mountain in the Flathead Range, HS-N-R2-D2-O.
•	Hanging Tree Main also ran, likely sometime on Sunday, Jan. 28th, HS-N-R4-D2-O. This crown extended across the path. A few hundred feet below the crown, the debris gouged to the ground and took out the winter’s snowpack. Debris terminus location could not be determined.
•	We investigated the crown of the previously reported a large avalanche that ran in the I-Beam Main path, HS-N-R3-D3-O.
o	We suspect this ran sometime on Sunday, Jan. 28th. There was some more recent snow on the crown and inside some 2-3” cracks separating sections of slab that failed, but then arrested on the slope.
o	The failure layer was the faceted snow resting on top of the now much decomposed late Dec. crust (pit profile).
o	Debris ran about 1500 vertical feet to near 5800’ elevation terminating well above the highway.

•	Snowpack is rapidly melting below 5500’ with plenty of bare patches showing up on sunny slopes.
•	We experience numerous audible collapse that became more frequent as we travelled above 6000’ elevation.
•	Snowpack structure is poor with facets around the late December crust along with faceted layers beneath the crust that are now becoming weaker with the presence of liquid water.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (specific)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Route:
•	Ski tour up the ridge dividing the I-Beam and Hanging Tree Main avalanche paths on the west face of Running Rabbit Mountain.
•	Objectives were to investigate the recent avalanche activity in this area and look for any new avalanches.

Weather Summary: A very foggy morning broke down by the afternoon. partly sunny skies with increasing cloud cover towards the continental divide. Light winds and warm.
Observation Summary: Crappy day for skiing. Great day for looking around. We've had a productive avalanche cycle in Central Glacier Park this week. Installed the Isaac Walton/Essex beacon checker this afternoon. Thanks Flathead Nordic Backcountry Patrol!
Instability Comments: Poor refreeze recently and unseasonably warm temps have resulted in a productive avalanche cycle with wet slabs on PWL and loose wet activity. Forecast verified.
Route: Recent avalanche activity viewed from Going-to-the-Sun Road.

Observation Summary: I observed at least 5 slides in the fiberglass area of the canyon creek trail. One slid made it out on to the trail, the rest were well above. Snow was wet and loose with many roller balls throughout the trail.

Weather Summary: Daytime high temps reached 50 deg F on the Canyon floor and into the 40s up at 6000-7000 feet elevation
Observation Summary: Observed multiple hard slab avalanches that ran sometime in the previous 24 hours on the west face of Running Rabbit Mountain. Also two, D2 size wet loose events on the south face of Running Rabbit that ran 1500-2500 vertical feet. See avalanche tabs.No recent avalanches observed yet on more shady aspects at upper elevations.Thick fog kept me from viewing any distant upper elevation from West Glacier until I was just west of Essex.Wet loose avalanche and slab avalanche activity on the Canyon floor has mostly ended. The snowpack here appears to have adjusted to the warming and has begun draining water as it is rapidly melting.
Route: Road observations from Highway 2

Observation Summary: We found ~10 cm of new cold snow above 6k' with a generally well-consolidated slab overlying a facet/crust combo over varying depths dependent on elevation/aspect. Entry and exit is still a bit schwacky due to low snow, but was expected and par the course.We dug a pair of pits with similar ECTP results with hard force on the facet/crust interface (SE and NE at 6550' and 6890' respectively) below the well consolidated slab of recent snow.We observed no obvious signs of instability, avoided slopes >35* and skied SE, E and NE aspects during our casual tour. The skiing was surprisingly good above 6k' and predictably damp but doable below that.
