Weather Summary: Light mist or freezing rain most of the morning (RV to RL; ZR). Break midday, then returned early afternoon. Winds light below summit ridge; moderate gusts at times on ridge.
Observation Summary:
We visited the Link Mountain Weather Station to clear the rime off the tower and sensors. 

Soggy day, with mist or very light freezing rain in the morning and again early afternoon.
We found a thin, freezing rain crust above about 5000 ft. This did not ruin the riding, though it didn't improve it.
Mild temperatures and more rain softened this crust at mid-elevations by early afternoon. 
The crust prevented rain from saturating the near surface snow. We did not see fresh rollerballs and did not trigger wet loose snow sluffs on steep, mid-elevation slopes. 
We felt no collapses, even on slopes similar to those that collapsed 1/24.
Instability Comments: No whumpfs in vicinity of whumpfs reported Wednesday 1/24
Route: From Olney up to upper Whitefish Lake then Red Meadow Pass by snowmobile. Then skin up to/ ski down from Link Mtn wx station

Observer Affiliation: The Mountain Riding Lab / The Mountain Guides
Observation Summary: Went up Lost Johnny Road and rode the basins mainly below Peter's Ridge. The riding was surprisingly soft and fun above 5500'. The upper layers of the snowpack are much more consolidated and made for very some solid stucks if your track broke through to the lower layers. Observed a 1/2'' thick crust layer on all aspects except shaded Northern faces. The crust was audible and noticeable but not thick enough to really affect the riding much. Performed many slope cuts on steep test slopes and saw no signs of instability throughout the day.

Went into the Challenge Cabin for the day while some buddies were staying there.While traveling we did not experience any signs of instability (pits were a different story).  The snow was remarakably better than we had anticipated, winds were very light, no gusts, and the snow fell on and off throughout the day.  There was 20 cm of new snow that, above 6000', sat on a supportive surface.  We traveled from 5600' to 7000' and had progressively deeper new snow that made for excellent skiing.  A far cry from our experience in the Middle Fork last weekend.Digging did reavel the deeper persistent layer, which we had react with hard force.  At 6900' on a SE aspect we found a 10 cm thick ice crust (finger hard) starting 60 cm down.  On a column test it failed on CT6 on top of the ice crust (60 cm down).  On an ECT we found ECT26, failing below the layer, 70 cm down.  No propogation, HS 105 cm.  The 20-30 cm sitting on the ground seemed pretty saturated & frozen, but there was another 20-30 cm of very weak facets sitting above that, beneath the ice crust described above.Our friends dug a pit at the same elevation on a NE aspect and did not have the persistent layer react during tests, until they really started wailing on their ECT.  Their pit was 135 cm deep.For what it's worth, the top 20 cm of snow was certainly reactive in both pits - CT8, ECT4, ECT11.  We stuck to lower-angle, straigtforward terrain and didn't have any issues whatsoever with snow moving.  The new snow was on the denser, hero-y side.  All in all the touring was good medicine after the last couple weekends, and so much better than we had expected.  If there's any silver lining to the last couple weather systems, it did wonders at this location for burying and sealing in much of the bush.

Weather Summary: Near-below freezing tempsOvercast with an elevated cloud ceiling providing view into the valley Moderate winds from the West
Observation Summary: Did not notice unstable snow conditions on this outing outside of pit results. The presence of the crust layer on top influenced our decision to forgo any normally enjoyable terrain. Noticed cornices on both E and W-facing aspects. The weather was calling for windy conditions so we expected some wind loading on E slopes. Did not find any recent signs of wind loading which we believe was a result of the frozen crust layer and lack of snow available for transport.
Instability Comments:
Noticed substantial debris on NE (6300ft) and SE (6400ft) facing slopes above 30 degrees. Initially believed to be a point-release surface slide or simply an impressive amount of rollerballs. Upon inspection, it appeared to be a wet loose slide within the top layer of snow. No notice of a crown. Difficult to tell how long ago the movement occurred - guessing 2-3 days.

No cracking or collapsing was observed.
Route: Snowmobile Jewel Basin Rd- Very whoopyCamp Misery Trailhead - toured East up drainage - gained notch and climbed to Radio Ridge from E aspect- max elevation 7000ft

Weather Summary: Drove to the trailhead with intermittent rain, which stopped about 930am. Most of the days was overcast, with only occasional light winds.
Observation Summary:
We went to Southern Glacier National Park to check on the shallow snowpack.

We verified that this part of our forecast region has a weak snow structure (maybe the weakest).
We had repeated propagating results in large column tests at mid and upper elevations.
We noted a skier-triggered hard slab avalanche on an east aspect at mid-elevation, estimated to be a week old.
We dodged large fields of frozen roller balls, likely from the most recent warm-up of last week. 
The upper half of our ski descent was nice, carvable turns. Below 5800 feet, the shallow snowpack made our exit sporty with a jungle of alder, lodgepole, and exposed downfall.
Instability Comments: No recent avalanches were observed. One isolated collapse occurred at upper elevations.

Observation Summary:
Recent natural avalanche activity and loud whumpfing collapses sidetracked us from our original plan to de-rime the Link Mountain weather station.

Observed a recent large (D2) hard slab avalanche that failed on facets above the 12/22 crust. This avalanche failed on a southeast aspect at 5400 feet. More info in the avalanche tab. 
We experienced a loud collapse that sounded to have traveled a long distance. This collapse also failed on facets above the 12/22 crust. 

We dug into where the slipe failed and the weak layer on the southeast aspect was exceptionally soft and weak. 


We dug a snow pit on an adjacent east-northeast-facing slope where we triggered the shooting crack. On this aspect, we did not find as poor of a snowpack structure as we did on the slope that collapsed. 

. Today, we observed a lot of bullseye information to keep us out of avalanche-prone terrain. The riding above roughly 6000 feet is playful and fun. At around 5000 feet the snow is moist and it skis you more than you ski it.

Weather Summary: Low visibility, calm winds and mixed precipitation at mid-elevations. At upper elevation, light snow and light winds.
Observation Summary:
We toured in the southern whitefish range to dig into our Persistent Slab problem.

Rain at 4500 feet. Mixed precipitation around 5000 feet. Snow above 5500 feet.
On southern aspects, at 5820 and 6500 feet, we found a structure of our Persistent Slab problem: facets above the 12/22 crust.
On northern aspects, at 6800 feet, the 12/22 crust was less defined, with more uniform density within the snowpack.
Key takeaway: the distribution of the Persistent Slab problem structure was more apparent on southerly aspects (SW, S, SE).
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking around our skis. No avalanches were observed.

Observation Summary: 1/20 - Dug a pit at 4500' on a South facing slope in Cascadilla. Snow depth of 130 cm. The recent storms contributed about a meter of right side up snow sitting on top of facets, ECTX.1/21 - Toured into Rescue Creek Basin and observed isolated cracking in the upper snowpack. Pit on a NE facing slope at 5000' confirmed failure at ECT8 at ~40 cm between recent storm layers without propogation. 160 cm of snow at test site.No whumping or recent avalanches were observed at these lower elevations. Challenging travel conditions and low clouds limited observation further up the valleys.
Stuck to skinning and riding the road today due to the ice crust.  Pictures shown at 4000’ from Saturdays freezing rain crust. 80-85cm of soft snow underneath that gave upon wrist compression test.

“Surf-it City” In the name of objective science, we conducted an experiment: what if you took an  individual who survived the oppressive conditions in the Middle Fork the last two days and got them into the Whitefish range today?Turns out, it works. Sledded (over a great many whoops) into Kimmerly today. Five new cm capped the very skiable crust from yesterday and the storm snow beneath. The only major obstacles included thick fog (though the sun nearly poked through this afternoon) and refrozen roller balls on steeper aspects. The snow surface stayed dry and temps hovered around freezing. Light to no wind to speak of.
Instability Comments: One whumph in a lee area just below a ridgeline on a west aspect.

Observation Summary: Rained continuously from low to upper elevations, very apparent warm air inversion from the reservoir (cooler) to about halfway up lost Johnny road (warmer).

Weather Summary: Winds remained light all day; mostly calm conditions prevailed.
Observation Summary: Take home message from todays tour- both the climb and descent were extremely difficult. The skiing was horrendous.•Overnight readings showed upper elevations warming to near-freezing (this progressed to above-freezing throughout the day/mid 30s) and valley cold pools remained strong in the Essex area below 5500'. •Freezing rain overnight created an eggshell-thick crust up to 5500'•Above 5500' glopping and deep trail breaking were on the menu as rain and warm temps saturated the upper 2" of the snowpack, capping dry powder underneath•Dripping branches, tree bombs, and rainfall deterred us from going any higher than 6800'. We experienced oppressive glopping conditions on the descent and ended up freeing our heels, stomping downhill, with nature's heel risers adding to the misery. •We eventually made it back down to the valley cold pool where a breakable-eggshell-freezing-rain-crust existed with bottomless, baseless powder underneath. •Any crowns observed in the alpine occurred mid-storm and were mostly filled in (not exceeding D2 in size). Multiple wet loose, point release (D1s) were observed in steeper terrain and didn't run long or entrain much debris.
Instability Comments: Although settlement-cracking was observed among all elevation bands on isolated features, collapsing was common and surprisingly widespread below 5500'. Above 5500' whumpfs/collapsing were not common.  A few, upper elevation windslabs were present which offered reprieve from deep trail breaking. No instabilities observed within the windslab and winds didn't appear the have much affect in this area.
Route: Climbed from 3900' via NE ridgeline and descended from our high point of 6800' via E ridgeline

We saw a few areas of recent wind affected snow on the ridgeline but didn't experience any cracking or collapsing. We did see signs of warming - including rollerballs and significantly heavier/wetter snow compared to the previous week.

The other Essex post was very thorough. Below 5k was bottomless cold powder that settled and cracked with every step with freezing rain crust on top. Above 5k was some of the worst glopping skinning we have ever experienced.

Observation Summary: Quick obs.No obvious signs of instability observed on nne or sse aspects east of WMR.Warming snow, Tree bombs, and smallish roller balls observed.
Route: WMR sidecountry

Weather Summary: Cold at the trailhead and got warmer as we climbed in elevation. Clouds limited our visibility. Winds were calm.
Observation Summary:
What a contrast from yesterday. Today's temperature inversion turned yesterday's powder into mashed potato snow.

At the trailhead, temps were around 15 degrees, and the recent snow was capped with a freezing rain crust.
The handful of ski groups exchanged colorful descriptions of conditions. One veteran skier said: “This may be the worst day of skiing I can remember."
At 5500 feet, precipitation was rain, trees were shedding snow, and the surface snow was saturated.
The silver lining is that this warming event is building our base for the rest of the season.
Instability Comments: Trees were shedding snow above 5500 feet. Some roller balls. We had cracking around our skis below 5000 feet.

Observation Summary: Noticed small, snowmobile caused avalanche off the trail in the second canyon on the way to the summit of Big Mountain. The avalanche runoff was off trail.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Observation Summary: Pit on west face at about ele. 6500 ft, 50 vert. ft off ridge line. Snow was wind affected across ridgeline. No cornices observed but lots of wind scour and wind compacted snow. Pit was dug on W face. Approx 35 degree slope angle. Temp. approx 25.Depth to ground was approx 36".A: Fresh snow, low consolidation 0-14"B: Consolidated, firm layer 15-30". C: Hoar and crystals forming weak layer/limited bonding. 31"-36" A few quick tests were run and snow pack was reactive with a clean shear 1 at Ec13 and Ec15 (two separate tests). Block jumped and was quite reactive. A proxy propagation test was run shear did not propagate. Conclusion: In areas with deep snow and fewer rocks, horizontal stability was remarkable despite reactive blocks.Ski report: Snow/sluff layer was dense and ski cuts barely propagated.  Pack was stable through the descent.
Instability Comments: Pinwheels were developing with warming temps at about 6000 ft in dense fog. Snow melting off trees at 5700. Otherwise no shooting cracks or instability observed.
Route: Olney access to Werner. Road building for sleds was difficult due to thick and heavy snow.

Weather Summary: Cold temperatures in the morning with light snowfall. Overcast skies for most of the day.
Observation Summary:
Today was more wallowing than trail-breaking in the Middle Fork of the Flathead Range.

We noted a widespread soft slab avalanche cycle at low elevations from Wednesday's high precipitation intensity. 
18 to 24 inches of storm snow appears to have doubled our snowpack at low elevations.
There was little evidence of settlement, and the low density of this recent snow makes for wallowing conditions.
Travel was challenging, as the new snow was unsupportable, and we both sunk into downfall pockets up to our waist.
The cloud deck limited our ability to see upper elevations, so we could not verify avalanche activity in this drainage at mid and upper elevations.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (specific)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: Cracking was observed at a density change in the new snow. The top 6-8 inches of the recent snow was slightly heavier than the foot, or more, recent snow that it rested on.  Collapsing was only noted on the flats along old Highway 2.

Weather Summary: Wasn't breezy enough to move snow. There was no fog and it was quite bright despite the falling snow. The falling snow was a mix of needles and perfect kindergarten crystals.

Departure temp was  -8, return temp was  +12 at 230pm.  The breeze flipped from NE to WSW at about 1100am.  It snowed lightly  after 10am with an inch on the car at 230pm.  Bird watching was slow - one chickadee.

Weather Summary: •	Broken or obscured skies above 6000’. Intermittent very light snowfall with trace accumulation by the end of the day.•	Winds were calm on the Canyon floor and light from the SW above 6000’.•	Inverted air temperatures and we were peeling layers as we climbed higher. Daytime high temperatures warmed to the high-20s °F above 6000’ but only reached about 10 °F on the Canyon floor.
Observation Summary: •	Can confirm the previously reported avalanche activity in Shed 7 West that ran the night of Jan. 17th. There were multiple soft slab crowns that failed in the storm snow, one nearing D2 size.•	A very small, D1 soft slab ran the night of Jan. 17th in Umbrella Fence with debris burying a few wires in the slide fence•	The avalanche in Shed 9 that ran sometime on Jan. 10th or 11th was a hard slab that failed on a layer of facets resting on top the late-Dec. crust (crown profile below) HS-N-R2-D2-O.o	The crown ranged from 20-40” thick and was about 100 yards wide.o	The slope angle where we dug into the crown was only about 30°.o	It was difficult to tell how far the debris ran, but it appeared much of the mass only made it 500-700 vertical feet down slope before it was caught up in small trees.•	Starting to develop a clearer picture of the general snowpack structure across the Program’s terrain.o	Below 5500-6000’, where there was little to no snow before the New Year, there is a layer of depth hoar at the base of the snowpack that has been buried by 2-3’ of snow from the January storms. Ski penetration is often still to the ground.o	Above 6000’, where a more developed snowpack exists, the 2-4’ of snow from the January storms is resting on top of a weak layer of facets that rests on top of a stout melt-freeze crust from late-December.•	Both weak layers are producing audible collapses and shooting cracks. I lost count of the number of audible collapses, but I have not experienced that many in one day for quite some time.•	We did not trigger any avalanches on test slopes or from safe ridgelines that we traveled on.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed
Route: •	Ski tour up into the Shed 7 and Shed 9 avalanche paths then descended the Shed 5 path.•	Objectives were to assess snowpack structure, perform welfare checks on the Shed 7 and Snowslip weather stations, and investigate an avalanche that failed last week in Shed 9.

Observation Summary: Marias pass went from champagne powder to Pacific Northwest mashed potatoes overnight Friday. We experienced a breakable, shin-bruising rain crust at the valley bottom which at 6000 feet gave way to very moist surface snow, temps around 35 degrees, and tons of skin-clumping, difficult uphill travel. We dug a pit at approximately 6800’ in a north east facing slope. The top meter of snow was relatively cohesive soft slab and the bottom 60 centimeters was garbage facets. We expected terrible pit results but did not get any full propagation. Just 50 yards away on a south aspect, the entire slope collapsed audibly, and we got several shooting cracks. There was evidence of a previous northeast wind event, drifting snow on atypical aspects, and on the ridge top we heard multiple large audible collapses in wind drifts. One was significant and felt by our entire party. Skiing left much to be desired and one in the group commented that this was “ACL” snow. On the ski out, freezing rain gave way to actual rain and tree bombs and roller balls were plentiful below 6,200’. The amount of rain was surprising. Rain mixed with low tide conditions made for a spicy exit. Gore Tex was tested as we were all soaked when we got back to the sleds.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (specific)  Collapsing Observed (specific)

Weather Summary: •	Snowfall and winds both died down around midnight last night.•	Morning dawned with clear skies and calm or light easterly winds on the Canyon floor and light to moderate NE winds at ridgetop elevations•	Temperatures warming to the single digits °F on the Canyon floor by the afternoon.
Observation Summary: •	There was a widespread cycle of small soft slabs failing in the new snow last night. These were generally small, destructive size 1-1.5 of 5 (D1-1.5) but noted some debris piles nearing D2 size.•	Some notable avalanche activity above the rail included:o	A couple D2 size debris piles in Path 1163 that ran about half path, terminating well above the grade.o	A crown in Shed 7 West that may be pushing D2 size. Recent snow had covered up much of the evidence. Debris terminated up in the lower start zone well above the rail.o	Multiple small crowns (D1s) in Shed 4D, Path 1164 and other similar steep cut banks.  •	Outside the Program Area on the nearby east face of Mt. Shields it appeared that a larger, hard slab may have failed on an older layer of snow buried deeper in the snowpack (photo).•	Also observed some D1 to possibly D2 size crowns in the Cascadilla drainage.•	Snowfall totals on the Canyon floor for the Jan. 17th storm ranged from 13-17” with 0.70-0.90” snow water equivalent.•	Weather stations above 6000’ are reporting snowfall storm totals of 12-21”.
Route: •	Windshield tours in the Canyon between Nyack and Marias Pass to look for recent avalanche activity.

Temperature (F): 3 degrees, rising to 10 throughout our tour.
Weather Summary: Beauty of a day
Observation Summary: We only skied one lap (slow going both on the snowmobiles and skin track, and we ran out of time) but it was great snow!  In general we still kept things pretty cautious and didn't ski anything steeper than 35 degrees.  Seems like areas protected from the wind are where it's at.
Instability Comments: Surprisingly we did not see any signs of instability.  Despite getting our sleds stuck a handful of times (or maybe more...who's counting?) and using that time to also check snowpack with some handpits, we did not find any instabilities.  Often the backslope of the roadcut will show signs of instability, and with trying to trigger small slabs we didn't get any to move.
Route: Snowmobiling on roads starting at about 3000' up to 6300'

Weather Summary: Cold and clear day. Only felt intermittent periods of light winds.
Observation Summary:
Not every day do you get a 1 to 2-foot dump followed by a cold sunny day. Snowmobiling yesterday was bumper-deep, and today was more like windshield-deep. We traveled into the Flathead Range to inventory recent avalanche activity. 

Observed many D1-D2 soft slab avalanches at middle and upper elevations. 2 avalanches appeared D3 in size. 
Some avalanches were thin soft slabs that appeared to have failed on a density change mid-storm, while the larger ones looked to have failed deeper in the snowpack. See the avalanche tab. 
We triggered one long shooting crack with the snowmobile on a roadcut at lower elevations. We did have localized cracking around our skis. These seemed to break roughly 8 inches below the surface. 
Encountered some wind-stiffened snow above 6000 feet on a ridgeline. Observed no active wind transport throughout the day. 

media soon
Instability Comments: Numerous recent avalanches were observed. Many small storm slabs and a few larger slab avalanches that we suspect failed deeper in the snowpack. Was able to trigger a long shooting crack on a steep roadcut at lower elevations.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Snow S-1 with some S1. Graupel at times.  Wind calm.
Observation Summary:
We kept our plans flexible to adapt to changing conditions:

Found 4 inches of overnight snow in Swan Lake and moderate to heavy snowfall
Increasing avalanche danger and deteriorating road conditions persuaded us to opt for a less committing plan
Unfortunately, that plan involved considerably more brush, which limited our ascent to 5000'
We found 12  inches of low-density snow above the 12/22 crust
This slab cracked and collapsed readily on a layer of weak snow resting atop the crust
We retreated back to the road where we tested cut banks with the snow machines
Cut banks produced shooting cracks up to 100 feet long on the same weak layer

Despite recent low-elevation snowfall. there are many exposed (or just barely concealed) hazards.
Instability Comments: Skier triggered cracks/collapses up to 20'  on heavily vegetated low elevation slow.  Snowmobile triggered cracks up to 100' on road side cut banks.
Route: Road riding to 4300'  and a brief bush battle up to 5000'

Weather Summary: •	Overcast or obscured skies with heavy snow, S2, sometimes higher at times. Snow density is currently very light, about 5%.•	Winds where light where I toured, but moderate to strong in the Canyon to the west.•	Temperatures were in the single digits °F.
Observation Summary: •	Observed a handful of small (D1) sluffs and soft slabs running on steep cut banks above Highway 2 and the Middle Fork.•	Visibility of the avalanche terrain above the rail was very limited or non-existent.•	Snowfall picked up last night and by 1700 hours the Canyon floor accumulated 5-9”.•	Wind loading was severe between Java East and Devil Creek with fresh wind slabs forming on the Canyon floor.•	On the ski tour I observed a few signs of instability such as some shooting cracks and audible collapses. These were at low elevations and likely occurred on a depth hoar layer.•	There is about a foot of snow in the Burnout path with a layer of weak depth hoar at the ground (photo). No shooting cracks or collapses observed in this path.•	Dug a snow pit in the Shed 4D avalanche path at 5800’ elevation on east facing slope (pit profile). Generally found soft, weak snow in the upper and mid-pack with a layer of facets on top the uppermost crust about 20” down from the surface.•	Overall, the snowpack is weak and somewhat brittle and missing a cohesive slab resting on these weak layers. But I was mostly in wind-sheltered terrain and suspect conditions were more unstable where wind slabs are more prevalent.
Route: •	Windshield tours in the Canyon and a short tour up into the Burnout and Shed 4D avalanche paths.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Continuous S1 from late morning to mid afternoon. Intensity seemed to pick up then, though it didn't seem to hit S2. Winds calm.
Observation Summary:
W investigated the terrain north and west of WMR, looking for the Persistent Slab structure identified last week in the terrain to the east. 

The forecast for fluff did not materialize. 4+ inches of new snow by midafternoon, but it was more like 10%  density than 5%. Hardly a dendrite in sight. 
We triggered one small soft slab avalanche that involved just the new snow, though it seemed to propagate widely. 
One ridgeline drift cracked but did not move. 
We only found a distinct persistent slab structure on a southwest facing slope at 6600 feet. It was not reactive in tests, though the weak layer was obvious.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: Limited cracking, mostly just short (< 0.5 m) around skis. One drift at a ridgeline cracked when I stepped on and started to slide, though it didn't move more than a few inches. 1 triggered soft slab avalanche (See avalanche tab)

Weather Summary: Moderate winds
Observation Summary: Many sluffs were observed in the new snow layer. All were confined to the new snow. Did come across a crack that stepped down to the persistent slab layer at a road cut, though not sure when this happened. New snow sluffs were 2-3" deep.

Had about 8" of new snow the night before and a total of about 3 1/2' in that area. Weather has been cold as usual and some fairly heavy wind gusts. Traveling across the debris field was very cautious making sure not to potentially trigger another slide from higher up. Stability of the remaining snow I would say is fairly good.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Cold, Calm on the valley floor. Light winds at upper elevations. Broken clouds becoming overcast by late afternoon.
Observation Summary: Snowpack was generally very stable and snow conditions were good - albeit still very cold. - No cracking or other sign of instability observed while traveling- Hand pits at 6000 and 6500 revealed the top 30cm+ was soft and well bonded - Some wind affected snow right on the ridge and at the summit- Snow on the North side at higher elevation was a bit slabby on top but not reactive- Dug a quick pit at 7000'. CT29
Route: South aspect of Link Mountain

Weather Summary: Cold and clear. The only wind transport we saw was at Marias Pass.
Observation Summary:
Field day turned to windshield survey. 

Observed three natural avalanches that were not previously reported. These likely failed sometime during the storm. More info in the avalanche tab. 
We observed active wind transport in southern Glacier National Park near Marias Pass. Did not observe transport on any other peaks.

Below 5k frequent settling and cracking 2-6’ around and away from skis. Quite the hollow and bottomless snow pack here. Found mixture of light and dense powder. On more open terrain on south and east there was buried non reactive wind slab. Dug one out at 5900’, north aspect, depth 115 cm. Ectx. Saw the crowns on Nyack that Cam photographed, there were similar crowns lower down in cascadilla as well. Wind transport apparent on all the major summits and ridgelines but none where we were.  Come on mid week storm….
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed  Collapsing Observed

Weather Summary: Clear, Calm, and Cold. Winds were calm on the valley floor and appeared to be light up high in this portion of the forecast area. Minimal active wind transported snow observed today on the high peaks of this valley. However the south facing slopes of Mount Stanton were visibly rippled from afar and textured from recent winds this weekend.
Observation Summary: Mostly clear skies offered vantage of the avalanche country surrounding the McDonald Valley. Expected to see a good bit of recent activity, but only observed one slide on Mount Cannon. Recent wind blown snow may have erased the evidence of others from this cycle.Established a new Beacon Checker site at the Lake McDonald Lodge parking area/ Sperry Trailhead/ Going-to-the-Sun Road access. We thank our partners, Flathead Nordic Backcountry Patrol, for their donation and maintenance of this equipment in the park this winter. We hope that this will serve as a convenient reminder to all  backcountry users to check their party's transceivers prior to entering the avalanche country accessed from Lake McDonald.
Instability Comments: Spotted a recent avalanche on the south face of Mount Cannon.
Route: Windshield tour of the McDonald Valley.

Weather Summary: Cold with light snowfall scattered throughout day, skies clearing up high in late afternoon.
Observation Summary: Very few signs of avalanche problems.  No fees slides or crowns observed (though visibility was limited most of the day).  Small to medium cornices mostly on south-facing ridges above 6,000.  Easily avoided.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.  Below 6,000 feet, most pole plants hit dirt or rock below the thin, soft snowpack.
Route: Lake McDonald Lodge to Mt. Brown Lookout on standard trail route.  Descent the same way.

Observation Summary: Approaching the saddle between Calf Robe and Summit, there were many signs of instability. Encountered heavy wind loading in random directions. As always it was windy. Two medium to large slides were spotted on Summit and Little Dog. Eight to twelve inches of light powder in some areas. In deeper areas I encountered shooting cracks that spread up to four feet out from under my skis. Phone was too cold and died after a few minutes (no photos). Turned around about halfway.Overall impression was that the snowpack out here is unstable as predicted.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)

Weather Summary: •	Awoke to mostly clear skies with occasional thin fog above 6000’. No precipitation.•	Absolutely frigid. Overnight lows around -30 °F on the Canyon floor and down to -40 °F at upper elevations. It hasn’t warmed up much this afternoon.
Observation Summary: •	Another 4-10” thick, soft slab ran on a steep cut bank above the Middle Fork sometime last night. Failure layer looks to be depth hoar at the ground.•	Visibility was much improved this morning and observed several avalanches in Railway terrain that ran sometime during the storm. Most events appeared to be wind slabs failing on the storm interface. All debris terminated well above the grade.o	Multiple crowns in Path 1163 above 6000’ and seemed to be concentrated on the western side of the path. Crown thickness varied from 10-20+” with some running at least a couple hundred feet wide. Destructive size 1s and maybe some D2s.o	Multiple shallower crowns in the very upper reaches of Shed 10 above 7000’.o	A thicker (est. 2-3’ at thickest) wind slab ran in the upper reaches (est. around 6500’) of Shed 9. Visibility of this terrain from the Hwy is very limited.•	Other observed avalanches outside of the Railway terrain included:o	A larger crown (D2+?) above Shields Creek on Snowslip Mtn, around 6800’ on an E aspect.o	A couple D1.5 size crowns on a NE aspect above Devil Creek around 7000’ on the Devils Hump. •	Trace of snow overnight with wind-loading finally tapering off and then gradually confined to ridge crests above 6500’ today with minimal transport.
Route: Windshield tour in JFS Canyon

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Cold. Strong winds.
Observation Summary:
Today we visited the crown of a reported avalanche just outside the WMR boundary. 

The large (D2) avalanche we visited was 2.5 to 3 feet thick. This avalanche propagated 150 feet wide and ran 400+ linear feet. It is worth noting that this avalanche propagated the full width of the start zone. The avalanche failed on a layer of facets above the 12/22 crust. 
We observed multiple thin soft slabs. These slabs occurred naturally and were just a couple of inches thick. 
We experienced localized cracking around our skis in the new snow.
 Wind speeds increased throughout the day and we observed intense wind transport at low and middle elevations. 

additional photos/video to come.
Instability Comments: Most recent snow was reactive. Easy to trigger very thin soft slabs.

Observation Summary: Couple new small soft slab avalanches today. One on a steep Railway cut bank and another on a steep cut bank above the Middle Fork. Suspect these also failed on weak faceted snow near the ground and ran sometime early this morning.Visibility was limited, but I could not find any avalanches up in the higher elevation terrain. Maybe tomorrow with better vis?Another 3-4"of snow on the Canyon floor today with raging easterly winds and plummeting temperatures. Blizzard conditions and widespread wind slabs at all elevations.
Route: Windshield tour in JFS Canyon on Hwy 2

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Intermittent S-1 and graupel.  1-2 inches fell throughout the day. Winds calm gusting to light from the SW.  Arctic air and easterly winds did not arrive at this location during daylight hours.
Observation Summary:
I forgot what is was like to put in an honest day of breaking trail, though I did hijack a few sled tracks where I could (Thanks!):

2-6 inches of new snow on yesterday's wind stiffened surfaces at all elevations 
This snow fell under calm winds and concealed the usual hallmarks of wind-affected snow
Identified hard windslabs where my skis rode on top of yesterday's snow surface (2-6 inches deep as opposed to 12  or more inches deep)
Aside from two collapses, slabs were unreactive in response to my stomping
Intentionally triggered a small windslab at upper elevation by kicking a wee cornice
Noticed one other small natural wind slab triggered by cornice fall
Large column test at 6100', E aspect yeiled unstable results with moderate force

I enjoyed safe solo skiing on low angle slopes.  
Media and Avalanche details to follow.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: Intentionally triggered a small windslab by stomping a wee cornice along a ridge at 6800'. Cracking and collapsing were mostly under foot. 2 larger collapses at mid elevation on SE aspects. Seemed like collapses were from hard wind slabs on top of less dense storm snow.
Route: Mostly a mid-elevation walkabout on test slopes of varying aspects, topping out at 6800'

Weather Summary: •	The Arctic air arrived around daybreak today and temperatures rapidly dropped from the high-20s °F to the teens, then single digits, and now below 0°F. •	Overcast skies today with moderate easterly winds and plenty of blowing and drifting snow.•	Snowfall was very light for most of the day but picked up around sundown with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour.•	Air temperatures this evening above 6000’ range from 0°F at the western end of the Program to   -18 °F at the eastern end.
Observation Summary: •	One small slab avalanche just above the shed roof in Shed 6 located at 4600’ on an east aspect.o	SS-N-R1-D1.5-O, crown was 5-10” thick (photo) and about 75’ wide. Debris ran about 100 vertical feet terminating above the shed. Failure layer was depth hoar at the ground.•	Another small (D1) soft slab on a north-facing cut bank along Hwy 2 just east of Marias Pass (photo). Failure layer also appears to be some older snow and a persistent grain type.•	No other avalanche activity observed. But visibility up into the higher terrain above the road was limited. •	Snowfall last night totaled 4-6” on the Canyon floor with 5-10” above 6000’.•	Another 2-3” accumulated on the Canyon floor today with 2-8” above 6000’.•	Easterly winds intensified after the Arctic air arrived around daybreak. Blizzard conditions resulted and fresh wind slabs were forming at all elevations.
Route: •	Windshield tours in the Canyon and a quick trip to investigate a small avalanche in Shed 6.

Observer Affiliation: The Mountain Riding Lab / AIARE Mechanized Instructor
Observation Summary: Rode up Beta Lakes Road into the basins below Doris Lakes. Observed multiple sluffs across the road going in. The photo was at 5,000 ft on a southeast facing slope. Overall this area had significantly less snowpack than Lost Johnny and drainages to the south. Many more hazards under the snow and a far less developed base.Mechanical issues kept us from getting Obs at the higher elevations.

Cracking observed above the crown of the avalanche, as well as wind affected and drifted snow. Elected to ride with caution in low angle trees on the west aspect. Manageable sluffing where wind slabs were not present, considerably unstable conditions where they were.

Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with moderate to strong SW winds and plenty of blowing and drifting snow.•	Snowfall was most intense (S2) around daybreak, then tapered off and was intermittent in nature. After noon precipitation consisted entirely of graupel.•	Daytime high air temperatures just reached the freezing mark on the Canyon floor and low-20s°F above 6000’.
Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	New snowfall from last night totaled about 2-3”. Another 2-3” accumulated on the Canyon floor today (0.20-0.40” water) and 3-4” above 6000’. Quite a bit less than expected. The uppermost 2-4” of today’s snowfall consisted entirely of graupel.•	The storm may have lacked in precipitation, but it made up for it with wind. Moderate to strong SW winds were observed at all elevations. Extreme wind gusts were noted at ridgetop locations.•	The wind affects and slight warming throughout the day created an upside layering in the upper snowpack with denser snow (wind slab and graupel) on the surface resting on top of low-density powder.•	Despite this snowpack structure, signs of instability were limited where we travelled. Several ski cuts resulted only in minor cracking underfoot. The propagation propensity just wasn’t there. I suspect slabs were more developed and reactive in locations with more severe wind loading.•	Dug a hasty snow pit on a southerly aspect at 6500’ in a location sheltered from the wind: Snow depth was 51” with about 24” of soft snow resting on top of a late-December crust. Extended Column Test resulted in ECTN6 about 6” down from the surface on the interface between the dense graupel layer and the lighter powder snow underneath.•	The snowpack on the Canyon floor is finally about foot deep.
Route: •	Climbed up into Grizzly Gulch to a high point at 6500’.

Weather Summary: Light to moderate winds were felt starting at 6200 feet. Cold. No precip. New/Recent snowfall total is overnight accumulation. Storm totals ranged from 55 to 65 cm.
Observation Summary:
Yup. The rumors are true. It is deep. Like real deep. Today we went to the northern Swan Range to check snow depth, wind transport, and suss out any buried persistent weak layers. 

We observed one large (D2) natural avalanche. This avalanche failed on an east-facing slope at roughly 7000 feet along the Swan crest. The crown was in wind-loaded terrain and just downslope of a large cornice. 
We experienced one audible collapse on a SE aspect at roughly 6200 feet. At this same elevation, we noticed more wind-affected snow. 

We found a weak layer of near-surface facets resting on a thin crust. I suspect this was the layer that collapsed.

In a different location with the same aspect, we triggered a 2 foot thick soft slab. This slab failed on the same facet/crust combination. The avalanche occurred on a short, yet steep slope. This slab failed while the second person was crossing it. No one was caught. 
Fresh cornices were easy to trigger but did not trigger any slabs below. I would assume the story would be different in areas experiencing stronger winds.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (specific)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Instability Comments: Audible collapse on a SE aspect at roughly 6200 feet. The triggered avalanche occurred on a SE aspect at roughly 6300 feet. More information is in the avalanche tab.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Overcast, with clouds dropping below ridgeline early afternoon. Calm except for a few gusts and light winds at the summit. S1 snow showers in the early afternoon, though visibility on the front side of the mountain was good.
Observation Summary:
Deep, yet shallow...
Guy and I checked out conditions in the avalanche terrain adjacent to Whitefish Mountain Resort (video summary). 

Knee- to thigh-deep snow above 6000  feet
Head-high alder above the floor of the canyon, thanks to the shallow snowpack this season.
While the recent snow has stiffened, we did not observe any cracking or collapsing and weren't able to release any slabs or sluffs with our slope cuts. 
There was a natural avalanche cycle during the storm. The crowns we saw and investigated were shallow yet with wide propagation (Avalanche tabs, pics)
The natural avalanches we saw were on sunny slopes and the debris indicated soft slab avalanches. There may have been more slides we didn't find on shady slopes. 
The most concerning layer in the snowpack was a layer of rounding facets below a thin, friable rime crust (video in snowpack tab). This propagated or collapsed in tests, and while the rime crust is widepspread below 6700 feet, it's not clear yet if there's more than an isolated persistent slab avalanache problem.
LOTS and LOTS of snow is available for wind transport. And there are powerful winds coming.
Instability Comments: Slope cuts on steep, easterly slopes entering the canyon did not release slabs, despite new and recent snow feeling slabbier than Sunday/ further north in the range.

Observation Summary: Small loose dry avalanche was only sign of instability. Recent storm snow was 10"-12 and low density, but gaining strength throughout tour. No wind transport on Paola ridge or any other visible sites (Tunnel Ridge, small portion of Flathead Range). Light snow throughout day. Good stability. Skied slopes < 35 degrees. World class  bushwhacking abounds below ~5200' on all aspects. GET SOME!

Weather Summary: Pleasant temps and super terrific S2 storm skiing all morning and early afternoon .. 3-4 inches fell during tour. S1 was tapering off by 3 pm.  Visibility was 3 out of 5… Arctic  air definitely arrived today and seemed  to slowly nudge its way down onto  the valley floor.
Observation Summary: Finally some good coverage at all elevation bands.
Instability Comments: Virtually no instability observed.. small cracks in new snow and essentially no sluffing.
Route: Up fs road to summer trail.  Got up to shoulder below summit ridge.  Aspects traveled on were mostly S  and SE

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Cold, calm, and cloudy. Exception was a burst of downsloping northerly wind mid-morning that left some drifts at low elevations. Snow showers (S-1 to S1) mid to late afternoon.
Observation Summary:
Famine, now feast. 

15-30 cm of very low-density new snow. Snow heights increased with elevation. 
Above ~ 5000 feet, the new snow is sitting on even lower density recent snow, making for upside-down blower conditions. 
No collapsing or shooting cracks, though the fresh snow may get more cohesive/ slabby as it settles.
Knee-deep but relatively easy trailbreaking above 6000 feet. 
A few fresh drifts down low, but calm conditions and almost no sign of wind effected snow. Lots of snow available for wind transport. 

We were surprised to see several fresh, large natural slab avalanches. 

N, E, and W aspects at mid-elevation.
2-3.5 foot thick crowns. 
Investigating one crown (which meant much less feasting) showed the slab was composed of sluff debris and did not appear to fail on a buried persistent weak layer. 
One slide looked to be a soft slab involving the new and recent snow. 
We also witnessed a small natural loose snow avalanche but did not see much evidence this was widespread. 
We were not able to trigger sluffs that moved more than a few feet on a 35+ degree slope. The snow did, however, blow overhead with turns. 

Rec day ski. More media in the AM.
Instability Comments: No collapsing in new or recent snow, short (< 1m) cracks immediately around skis while breaking trail. No slabs on test slopes. Some wrinkles on steep slopes where the new snow wanted to slab up and go.

Observation Summary: -Observed about 2 feet of very low density new snow above 5600ft. About 5-8in at lower elevations - Did not experience signs of instability on our route other than dry loose activity- The bottom half of Marion trail had just enough snow for the skin up but we opted to boot down- Consistent snow flurries throughout the day, with short breaks of clouds lifting - Temperatures stayed below freezing and wind remained calm- Watched 8 different gaggles of geese head south

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Snowed.  All day, without wind.  We didn't linger at the notch at the end of the day, but it was blowing lightly by the time we dropped down to the sled (4 pm).
Observation Summary: We were (SO) pleasantly surprised by the snow totals today.  Stepped off the sled in Noisy Basin and were up to our knees in it.  Trail breaking was consistenly above-the-knee all day, yet fairly effortless given the super light density of the snow.  It felt quite right-side-up, and despite there being 2+ feet of snow from the get-go, we couldn't find any signs of instability other than very loose, very dry sloughs on steeper (35 degrees) slopes.  Stomped around on some test slopes and again, nothing.  The runs down to Black Lake had closer to 3 feet of fluffy new snow, rather than 2.  Hard to believe there were 60 mph gusts above us the night previous, since there was zero wind affect on the snow from start to finish.  This is all true - not your average day in the Jewel, to say the least.  Especially since we only encountered one other party out there all day. "Top 5 deepest days in the Flathead" was overheard by one very stoked skier in the group.It snowed consistently throughout the day, at plenty of times hard, with no wind.  Likely gained at least another 4+ inches throughout the day up high, and 3 or so inches on the truck by the time we returned to it.
Route: Traveled via sled to Noisy Basin. Best condition I've ever had the road in?! Skinned up to the 'notch', skied a couple of mellow-ish Black Lake shots, then returned to the sled from the notch.

Observation Summary: The storm finally delivers! Observed lots of wind transported snow on ridges and set off multiple storm slabs avalanches throughout the day, many of them remotely triggered. The instability was consistent on all aspects and mainly observed around 6000 - 6500 ft.  Both of the bigger slides I posted photos of appeared to be sliding on the Dec 22nd layer.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)

Observation Summary: Testing the waters here to see the coverage etc...Drove up to before where the skid trail entrance is to the ridge. Way too little coverage to try from there, continued on road until ridge trail and used that to try to access the bowls on the ridge. Made it to below the South bowl of the ridge, decisions were made that ended with using the skin track to get back down as most of the skiable runs are well covered for about 2-300 feet and then become an elk weed labyrinth. Avalanche activity was confined to the areas where enough snow was present to slide, and appeared to have been back during our warming events in the prior weeks. Conclusion that most of the swan range won't be rideable until more prevent snowfall persists for time. Unless you like the UFC fight with the brush.

Weather Summary: Overcast skies with flurries of light snowfall. Winds were light. We could not see the surrounding peaks due to the cloudy conditions.
Observation Summary:
Another stroll in the mountains to improve our post-holing skills and check on conditions in southern Glacier National Park

New snow ranged from 1 to 3 inches.
At 6800, the new snow rests on a crust-facet-crust combo. 
Winds were calm today, however there was evidence of previous wind-drifted snow along the upper elevation ridgelines.
This area has less snow than the Flathead, the Whitefish, and the Swan Range.
No instability was observed, but we will track these crust layers when we get more snow.
Instability Comments: No cracking, collapsing, or recent avalanches observed.

Observation Summary: Wind slab formation isolated to ridge lines, small pockets and easy to avoid. Sheltered terrain held 8-10 inches of new snow. Hand pits showed low slab potential. Moderate snow all day. We felt comfortable skiing avalanche terrain on N and W aspects.

Moderate snowfall  and light winds (never made it to ridge tops) all afternoon in the Lost Johnny zone. Still some obstacles to avoid but conditions above 6000 ft are looking good. Steep treed slopes showed no signs of slab formation. No cracking or sluffing on 40 degree pitches. 15-20 cm new over the last couple days sitting on settled powder and/or crusts make for fun conditions. 10 cm fresh at reservoir elevation.

Weather Summary: Most of the day was overcast skies, with some light ridgetop winds.
Observation Summary:
Today we went to Sixmile to skin a lot and ski a little.

Storm totals were underwhelming, especially at middle and low elevations. 
At 6800 feet, we measured 45 cm (17 inches) of soft snow above the most recent crust, in a sheltered location.
At middle elevations, snow totals ranged from 6 to 15 cm (2 to 6 inches).
We did not encounter signs of instability, except for isolated pockets of harmless, baby wind slabs.
We found our best turns between the elevation of 6400 to 6200 feet.  
Below 6000 feet, conditions remain difficult with surface obstacles (rocks, logs, brush, beargrass, etc).
Instability Comments: No avalanche activity was observed.

Observation Summary: Better snow quality than expected. We found 2-5” of fresh snow on a supportable crust.  Still plenty of low tide hazards out there.
Route: Goulie to Chicken Bones. Skied easterly aspect.

Weather Summary: clear overnight skies left SH on all aspects that I traveled on BTL and NTL. Winds were moderate out of the W-SW, slightly more than had been predicted last night. Cloud cover varied between scattered and broken until late in the afternoon.
Observation Summary: Temps didn't get above freezing so loose wet problem never materialized today, unlike yesterday afternoon. Winds reached moderate speeds out of the W-SW although they were only predicted to be low. Minimal snow was transporting, because it has moved around the past few days at NTL and above. Small wind slabs have formed around larger terrain features and could be triggered with the weight of a person. Widespread SH throughout NTL elevation band was protected from moderate winds during the day.
Instability Comments: small pockets of reactive wind slabs above tree line around rock outcroppings and larger terrain features.
Route: Trade route on Great Northern. mostly W and SW faces.

Weather Summary: Light ridge top winds. Broken sky cover at the end of the day.
Observation Summary:
We went up to the Northern Whitefish Range to put eyes on the Link Mtn weather station and track surface conditions before an upcoming weather pattern change.  Generally, we found excellent riding conditions with no signs of instability. 

Avalanche terrain above 6000 feet appears to have relatively better coverage than further east in the forecast area. 
Southeast facing aspect at 6000 feet:  4 inches of weak, facted snow resting on a smooth crust (12/22). This crust was supportable to ski.
North aspect at 7000 feet: we observed a layer of preserved surface hoar underneath 2 inches of recent snow. 
East aspect at 7000 feet: near surface facetes exist underneath 2 inches of recent snow. No surface hoar observed.

•Took advantage of vehicle access to 5600' in the very dry Skyland region•As observed a couple days prior on Patrol Ridge, the snowpack is thinner and weaker in this region. •The 12/22 crust exists to 7000' with 1-3" of recent snow from New Years eve storm sitting atop it. This crust is ski-supportable up to 6500'. Above that the crust gradually tapered away and at 7000' was non-existent. •Skinning became difficult above 7000' as the snowpack was almost entirely faceted except for the degrading 12/7 rain crust which was sometimes supportable dependent on terrain.•Older, point release, wet loose avalanches (D2) plagued the E facing chutes off Slippery Bill. Many of these slides initiated near the top of the path, near rock bands (7400') and gouged the entire snowpack away in a runnel-like fashion leading me to believe the E facing terrain snowpack was just as faceted as the W. These slides ran full path (~1500') and likely during last weeks warm, sunny weather. •I opted to boot the last 500' to the creek due to poor-non-existent coverage•Wind has continually been benign throughout the Middle Fork region
Route: Drove to Morrison TRHD via Skyland Road. Skinned W aspect of Slippery Bill Mtn to 7500'. Descended NW aspect. 1100-1600

Observation Summary: 4-5 inches of new snow with cloudy skies, temps around freezing or below, and no wind.  We found from 50 to 70 cm HS on various aspects. No signs of recent instability but did observe small to medium piles of debris around rocks and cliffs.

Weather Summary: Double layer inversion with fog constantly rolling in and out.
Observation Summary: Absolutely perfect conditions however I think it will be short lived due to the constant  cross loading. We observed this pretty much the entire day but the snow just wasn’t bonding to form a wind slab. There was about 4-5 inches of new snow on Comeau pass. And very little avy debris along the west face of  Edwards.
Instability Comments: Zero instability, minor sluff.
Route: North/ NW aspect of Gunsight above como pass.

Observation Summary: 2-3 cm at Fitzimmons trailhead. 5-10 cm fresh snow between 6000 and 7000 ft. Little wind effect and no slab formation. New snow was softening the suncrust on the S and W exposures. Great riding and no signs of instability. Temps in the mid 20’s partly cloudy skies.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy with some sun poking through in the afternoon.   At or slightly above freezing for the high.
Observation Summary: The new snow was dry and bonded well with the somewhat wet surface snow from the warm days before.   ECT produced no signs of instability and minimal sluffing observed on steeper slopes.  Winds were moderate as well overnight.  No new slabs were found but older slabs and some drifting and small cornices observed near the ridges that still held some potential.
Instability Comments: No major signs of instability observed.  Kicked off some baby roller balls in the afternoon.
Route: Toured up the Moose Creek drainage and on up to the summit of Hornet.  The NE aspect was the only game in town for decent turns.

Weather Summary: High clouds with a few minutes of sunshine and no wind.
Observation Summary:
Some days we go out, so you don't have to.  Today we went to the Middle Fork to thrash the brush and check on access for the last day of 2023.

Surface hoar and rime were clues that air temperatures had cooled from yesterday; however, the snow directly under the surface hoar was still moist.
Settlement cones around the alder indicated that the 7 cm (approx. 2 inches) of snow above the 2 cm 12/22 crust had consolidated over the past few days.
We did not observe recent avalanche activity or signs of instability.
We did not get propagation in our snowpack tests. There was too much brush. Snow depths varied from 40 to 70 cm.
Instability Comments: There were no recent signs of instability or avalanches.

Weather Summary: S2 started around noon and continued through exit at 4:30 with visibility decreasing in PM .
Observation Summary: Challenging conditions at low elevations gradually transitioned to dry storm skiing at upper elevations.
Route: S, E and NE aspects

Observation Summary: On SW slopes that were exposed to yesterday's sun, the wet snow had mostly refrozen making travel without crampons in areas that had been baked by the sun a bit tedious. The SW aspects had large roller balls and snails from the previous day that were completely frozen. The NE slopes had surprisingly good snow, with up to a 6 in layer of drier, loose snow in some areas making for good skiing. I even triggered some very small dry loose slides on these E/NE aspects. Very light snow was coming down for most of the day. The top of the ridge was completely shrouded in clouds and visibility was about 200 feet.
Route: Hiked from the hairpin on Jewel Basin Road through the notch to ski the far side of the ridge. Passed through NE and SW aspects primarily

Observation Summary: •Spent the day skinning Patrol Ridge (6600-7200') under overcast skies with temperatures hovering around freezing.•A M/F crust exists on solar aspects from the last few days of sunny weather. It ranges from supportable (S aspect) to break-able (E/W aspect)•Surface hoar was noted, but only in shaded, sheltered and appeared to be decomposing with the recent temperature inversion•Our ECT didn't produce any results, but the upper half of the snowpack is weak and faceted above and below the 12/22 crust which was 3cm thick and degrading (friable) at 6900'•Light snowfall began around 1300 and persisted throughout the day. Below 6000' it was raining. •Overall, the snowpack is very thin in the Skyland area in comparison to the Flathead Range
Route: Drove to Challenge Cabin. Took sleds up FS road 1651 to 25 Mile Creek divide (6600'). Skinned south along Patrol Ridge to S facing chutes above Granite Cabin along the Middle Fork. 0900-1500

Observation Summary: The goal was to find winter snow on what could be the end of the endless high pressure  inversion. Goal achieved on an E/NE slope between 7800 and 6200 ft. Slight refrozen surface crust on due East exposure but NE was still dry settled powder.  Some surface hoar but spotty distribution up high and more prevalent in lower forest zones. Facets are all baked off on sunny slopes. 12/7 crust stout at 7800 ft with some sugary facets above and below.  Snow above was not really slabby but seemed to be starting to dry out and facet throughout. The shallow layer above the 12/22 minor crust was somewhat reactive and cracked a bit but didn’t slide, except late in the day, where the top 5-10cm was wet sliding on south slopes with ski cuts.

Weather Summary: Inversion
Observation Summary: Warming from afternoon sun.
Instability Comments: Wet loose and some isolated wind slabs
Route: West/ south west face of Great Northern. Happened around 2 in the afternoon. Set by the second skier in a group of 5 no other slides occurred on the same aslh

Weather Summary: Warm, sunny, and almost wind-less. Ideal for climbing mountains.
Observation Summary: -Motivated by a forecast for low winds on the east side of the Park we went out and found very pleasant conditions. Basically calm, or with occasional light puffs of wind from the west, even up in the alpine.-Very little snow,  and skis optional. Our objectives were to climb and we didn't bring them. There basically isn't any snow below about 6500'.  Above that, where there was snow, it was generally supportable in boots with penetration 3-6", with some occasional deeper post-holing.-We climbed a face on a SE aspect up to our high point which consisted primarily of steep snow climbing.-No recent avalanche activity and no signs of instability observed on our route or in any of the visible, distant terrain.-Despite the very warm temperatures and sunshine, the snowpack was generally aged and consolidated and we were not concerned about surface instabilities/loose snow avalanching.-The snowpack surface crusts were slightly wet/moist all the way above 9000' feet on sunny aspects.-There were some more recent wind slabs, likely formed in the past few days. These were bonded well to the underlying snow and were capped by a surface crust. We could not initiate any failures.
Route: Up the Dry Fork and climbed to a high point at ~9400'

Observation Summary: Skied Jewel Basin area. Noticed some facets in snow around 5500ft in shaded areas off of road on skin up. Snow on southern aspects was getting wet and gloppy by mid day. Snow in shaded NE aspect was heavy and creamy but great to ski and had little evidence of roller balls (picture). Felt comfortable in the shade but skied one at a time on steeper terrain. Our exit was on a western aspect slope that, by midafternoon, was getting rollerballs and hit by sun. Skied one at a time through the short section of steeper terrain in those conditions. Saw lots of large roller balls (appears to be) on southern aspects in steeper terrain in the distance getting hit by the sun - didn't go there, so couldn't tell if it was from today or previous. Overall, great day out there!

Weather Summary: Above the strong cloud deck about 4000 feet, there was mostly clear skies and sun.
Observation Summary:
It felt like spring skiing in the Swan today, with mild temps, no winds, and sunshine.

The snow was moist up to approximately 7000 feet, even on northerlies.
Evidence of a recent upper-elevation thermal belt in the form of a thin surface crust was noted in northern aspects. 
Recent snow above the 12/22 crust is consolidating with ambient above-freezing air temperatures.
Evidence of trees shedding snow was a good indicator that the sun had been warming above 6000 feet.
In the terrain we traveled, we did not observe wind-drifted snow.
Below 6000 feet, we snowmobiled on very thin coverage. Yikes!
Instability Comments: No avalanches or instability observed.

Observation Summary: • Great visibility, above freezing temps, no wind at our high point of 6700'• We climbed the E ridge above Pinnacle Rd and descended the skin track. Skiing was good above 5600' (recycled pow) • Old (wet loose) and a somewhat recent slide (storm slab) were observed in the alpine terrain of the Flathead range. • Coverage is scant on S aspects (up to 6500')• Little in the way of obvious instability. No surface hoar observed but the recent snow (12/23) is faceting above the 12/19 crust.
Instability Comments: Isolated windslabs (8") (4F) were experienced along ridgeline. Shooting cracks were limited to the skin track and didn't exceed more than skis length.
Route: Snowmobile access via Pinnacle road. Skinned to 6700' and descended skin track.

Observation Summary: Despite the unbelievably low snowpack at reservoir level it’s still a reasonable facsimile of winter above 6000 ft. The dense underlying snowpack feels like a great day in March, but with countless trees and stumps to avoid. Great climbing and sidehill conditions in 15- 30 cm of soft snow above the crusts. No major surface hoar visible, light winds and temps in the low to mid 20’s. Biggest surprise was the sizeable cornice formation, which we haven’t seen too much of year this season. Numerous cornices in the upper basins have collapsed and slid, although none too recently. That gave us confidence to ride steep easterly slopes one at a time. Also, as we worked our way south, each basin got progressively deeper, indicating that the Swan experienced strong lake effect with the last dump.

Weather Summary: Light SW wind.  Increasing cloud cover throughout afternoon.
Observation Summary:
Afternoon outing to check access in the Swan Lake area.  Seems like we're losing snow..

2-4 inches of "recent" snow above the 12/22 crust
Snow was wet on sunny slopes  up to 6000', and moist to 6200' (high point)
Shaded slopes were moist up to 5500' and dry above
Noticed a handful of natural rollerballs
Winds were calm to light with no sign of blowing snow
A thin, breakable crust was refreezing on SE slopes by 3:00pm

Ran out of snow at 5000' on my descent.
Route: Drive to 4200' Ascend southerly aspects to 6200' and descend same route.

Observation Summary: • Very little snow below 5000’ elevation on the Marion trail • Temperature inversions were strong (cold, below freezing temps entrenched in the valley bottoms with above freezing temps at higher elevations) -- "hot pow" conditions noted between 5500-6500' (skin wax recommended) • Moderate winds from the W were transporting nominal amounts of snow along the Flathead crest, and although isolated pockets of windslab were experienced along ridgeline; all were benign and no instability was observed• Overcast, sometimes partially broken skies kept the sun from wreaking havoc today and no wet-loose was observed. Southerly aspects have a thin (2cm) M/F crust to at least 6800'• We skied an E aspect (7200-6000') and experienced no sluffing. Surface snow was dense and felt more like April powder than late December powder with the higher temps.Visibility was excellent and no recent avalanche activity was observed in the leeward, alpine terrain of the Flathead Range.
Route: Skinned to 7200' via Marion Lake trail. On the descent we walked from 5000' to the IWI ski trails

Observation Summary: We observed:•no obvious signs of instability. •moderate southwest wind along the ridgesWe found good skiing conditions above 6000 feet in sheltered locations but the shallow snowpack in the northern Swan makes riding off trail less than ideal.
Instability Comments: We observed no obvious signs of instability.
Route: Road up to Doris Ridge TH

Observation Summary: What a lovely day out. Hard to argue with sunshine and soft snow up high, except for one encounter with a fierce local resident: “That’s no ordinary rabbit”, if you get our drift. Surface hoar was beginning to develop overnight. Noted on all aspects/elevations above 6k ft. No instability noted, other than sloughing on steeper, north aspects. Skies were clear then partially cloudy in the afternoon. Temps were below freezing.  Ridgetop winds were light, but no wind at lower elevations. No evidence of active snow transport.

Weather Summary: Increasing cloud cover and wind throughout the day.  Winds were moderate from the SW above 7000' on isolated terrain features.
Observation Summary:
We set out to cover some ground in the Northern Swan Range:

Last weekend's snow is behaving: no dry or wet sluffs during our tour
Moderate SW winds caused light blowing snow along upper elevation ridges and isolated terrain features
We came across one thin, hard slab (1F) in a particularly windy constriction
Baby cornices were growing, several tiny tips broke and slid downhill with little consequence
We met many people during our travel that were enjoying the sun and snow

We were comfortable skiing slopes steeper than 38 degrees.  We navigated around convexities and terrain traps and skied one at a time in steep terrain.
Route:
Sleds to 5800´ 
Skinned to high point of 7474' 
Skied E, N and W

Weather Summary: Clear skies with a few clouds and calm winds.
Observation Summary: Clear skies and calm winds were the theme for the day. Despite the stable weather, we found unstable results in the snowpack. After skiing a short, steep, north facing line as a warmup the plan was to ski a steeper, longer, and more consequential line on north facing terrain. An ECTP9 at 6800' on the same aspect as our intended line caused us to quickly change plans. Switching to sheltered east facing terrain led to fun powder skiing without the risk of an anomalous slab ruining the day.
Instability Comments: We observed shooting cracks and slabs breaking off on the switchbacks of our skin track on north facing terrain at 6800' and above.
Route: Toured up to 6900' on N facing terrain.

Weather Summary: Shockingly warm temperatures for late December. Standard East side winds were moderate to strong at all times during the day. Several gusts after sunset nearly knocked over a weary traveler around Sun Rift Gorge.
Observation Summary: Moderate to strong winds out of the W and SW were transporting snow at mid and upper elevations. Snow was actively adding to previously formed wind slabs from earlier in the season. By 1400 snow was actively being transported on SE to NE aspects on Mt. Jackson across the valley. Sloughing of surface snow was occurring on SW-SE slopes that held more than 25 cm of snow between 7400-8000'. Small rollerballs were observed coming off of rock faces. Snow totals on GTTS road never exceeded 5cm. However, near surface faceting helped to speed up the descent on the way back. Snow line started around Jackson Glacier Overlook.
Instability Comments: Areas of with wind slab formations showed poor bonding with old snow on slopes over 40 degrees in and around the diorite band. Snow depths varied between 0 and 100 cm.
Route: Observed W to SE slopes on Mt. Siyeh, and various other aspects on nearby peaks. Snowline started around 5300' to over 10k.

Weather Summary: •	Mostly cloudy with the bottom of the cloud ceiling hovering around 7000’ elevation. Occasional very brief snow flurries with no appreciable accumulation over the day.•	Light SW winds with moderate gusts on the ridgetops.•	Inverted air temperatures this morning with single digit values °F on the Canyon floor and low-20s°F above 6000’. Afternoon high temperatures reached the high-20s°F at all elevations.
Observation Summary: •	Observed a handful of old Destructive Size 1 (D1), wet loose avalanches that ran last week during the warm weather. These were isolated to steep slopes on sunny aspects primarily between 6000-7000’ elevation.•	Very little snow below 6000’ elevation. “Storm” totals from 12/23 were 2-3” on the Canyon floor and 5-8” above 6000’.•	Above 6500’ the snowpack generally consists of 5-8” of recent powder snow on top of the 12/22 crust (~1” thick), then another 5-10” of soft (4F) snow down to the 12/7 crust (2-4” thick), with melt-freeze crystals below that to the ground. Total snow depths ranged from 2-4’.•	Below 6000’ the snowpack, where it exists, consists of 2-3” of powder snow on top of a sometimes boot-supportable melt-freeze crust to the ground.•	Surface hoar was prevalent on the Canyon floor and up to about 5000’ where it gradually disappeared.•	On sunny aspects, the snowpack surface was slightly stiffened by the recent solar heating/nighttime cooling, but no real crust was found.•	No signs of instability observed. We cut a mini fridge-sized cornice chunk onto a steep northerly slope at about 7300’ with no results.•	Recent wind-loading has been minimal at all elevations.
Route: •	Climbed up the Second Slide avalanche path, then further into the upper reaches of Shed 10, and then into the uppermost reaches of Grizzly Gulch to our high point on the summit of Running Rabbit at 7674’.

Weather Summary: Clouds draped the mountains all day.  Winds were calm and temperatures were balmy (20's).
Observation Summary:
Besides whiteknuckling the luge course that is the spur road to the trailhead, we had a great visit to Mt. Marsten.

There exists 3-4 inches of unconsolidated snow atop a very thin (<1 cm.) melt freeze crust from 12/22.  This snow is well bonded to the crust and only moved on very steep terrain.  Even then it was only able to run 10-15 feet.  
What little surface hoar we did find on the surface was rimed and fairly beat up.  
Wind has not been a player at this location.  We found little in the way of drifting snow, dunes or windslabs.
The 12/7 crust was reactive in our stability tests at 7,300 ft.  We stumbled across a sweet spot of shallow snow, shaded aspect and steep terrain.  We felt numerous collapses which prompted us to dig and expose the weak layer.  The surface hoar/facet combo that plagued us in the  weeks prior was the culprit.  I assume this is one of the last vestiges and was an isolated bit of action.
Boot pen is 30 cm with the skiing being great.
Instability Comments: We were skinning along the ridge by the lookout at 7,300 ft and felt several collapses.  Upon digging we found the weak layer to be the surface hoar/facet combo below the 12/7 crust.
Route: Up the snowmobile road to the Lookout.

Observation Summary: Spent the day in the clouds; about 1/2” of snow accumulated on the sled when we got back to it. Skies obscured; no to light winds where we were. Temps below freezing. Chose to travel on some steeper terrain and saw the sloughing mentioned elsewhere here. No propagation in anything we saw. No wind affect anywhere we traveled today.
Small weak layer sitting around 40cm deep.  Larger weak layer, crust, and  then fresh surface hoar on the surface that could lead to future instability.
Route: Traversed across the ridge, dug a pit around 6400' on a low angle south facing aspect.

Observation Summary: Kicked off some sizeable sluff on a previously non-skied slope, and some more sluff skiing it a 2nd time. No sluff on run 3. No sluff on previously skied slopes. No other signs of instability.

Weather Summary: Clear skies in AM with cold temps (19* at car). Similar temps up high, though shadowed cirques felt cold. Some fog and clouds built around peaks through the day, then overcast mid-late afternoon. Calm to light winds.
Observation Summary:
It's not only not all bad, it's very good in places. Thanks, Santa.

The midweek warming and rain left a friable melt-freeze crust that's now buried by 5-20 cm of new low density snow from the end of the week.
The end of week snow is cohesionless and dry, with very little slab characteristics. 
The recent snow is already faceting and sidehilling/ skinning is difficult. 
We found very few signs of wind slabs, even at the top of chute that would normally collect drifted snow.

Day off ski.
Instability Comments: No cracking or collapsing observed.

Observation Summary: Santa delivered just what we were hoping for in the central Swan range. 20-30cm of right side up pow seemingly glued to a variety of crusts. Most hasty pits showed the underlying dense crusts breaking down a bit, i.e. saving us from a dust on crust feel. SE exposures, had firmer underlying crusts than E and NE slopes. No avalanches observed, but lots of sluffing in the top few cm’s. Very little wind effect visible and only the slightest fresh cornice development.

I took a short Christmas Eve stroll to view old avalanche debris and assess access in the Middle Fork.

The December 5-6 Atmospheric River initiated a moderate-sized avalanche cycle on Mt Penrose. 
The lack of snow on the ground during the AR limited the size and extent of the debris.
Many avalanche paths visible from Skiumah Lake do not have enough snow to produce avalanches.
There are only 6-8 inches of snow on the ground at the lake.

-One small naturally triggered soft slab avalanche observed-Height new snow: 4-8'' -Drifted snow under north facing ridge line >1' F hard (observed stiffening of snow on NE aspect and stayed out of it)-Clouds cleared out around13:00, winds from the west to northwest (upslope in the afternoon on north aspect above 7000')-We utilized several test slopes and triggered dry loose D1 entraining more snow and moving fast-We skied steep north facing terrain and minded our slough

Weather Summary: Calm winds all day, even along the ridgeline. Very light snowfall occurred until noon.
Observation Summary:
We went to the Apgar Range with low expectations today to check out snow totals at mid-elevation ridgelines.

New snow along the ridge (6400 feet) was a meager 3 inches. At the trailhead, we measured 2 inches of new snow.
Snow was moist at 6400 feet. When we made our few turns, the new snow easily sluffed on the supportable 2-inch pencil hard crust.
Winds were calm all day and we did not observe active wind transport in this area.
Skiing remains challenging with surface hazards and low snow coverage.
Instability Comments: No avalanches or instability were observed.

Observation Summary: Went to see if the Swan had any more goods than the rest the region and were pleasantly surprised. It had snowed 6-8” overnight and came down hard throughout the day. The Snotel measured 36” when we walked past it and the cars had 3-4” of fresh by the end of the tour! We skied several lines at different aspects and elevations and found a variety of conditions. The crust seemed more noticeable at mid to lower elevations, mostly below 6500’. In these areas, sloughing was prevalent with loose dry snow on top of the crust. This was noted even while skinning but the new snow had not formed slabs and the crust would support a skiers weight. As you got higher and on more northerly aspects, the skiing was actual deep and good! There are definitely still sharks in the water, so ski carefully, but there is good skiing to be found if you hit the right spots!

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Party reported band of snow as they drive up Lake McDonald. Started snowing at the trailhead at low-moderate intensities (S1). Several bands of higher intensity snow as they approached climb, but only 1 intense enough to put up hoods (S2). Accumulations at low elevation amounted to 2-4" over the course of the day. The party felt no wind, heard no wind above them, and could hear McDonald Creek during their climb.
Observation Summary:
A climber was caught and injured when he was overrun by a large natural avalanche on the northwest face of Mt. Brown. He and his partner self-evacuated. 

The accident occurred during a storm with moderate snowfall intensity and seemingly calm conditions. 
The party was exploring a climb known as the Right Gully, or, if completed to the summit, Mile of Smiles.
They were on the 1st pitch of technical climbing. The leader had set an anchor and belay on the climber's right side of the gully, below where it forks. The 2nd a was partway up the pitch on a relatively easy snow slope, also on the climber's right side of the gully.
