The 2nd climber reported seeing a powder cloud blooming overhead. The debris ran below him in the gully. 
A second, much larger slide followed very quickly after. The 2nd climber took two steps to escape the debris but couldn't climb out of the gully. He crouched and anchored himself with his tools.
The debris from the slide washed over the 2nd climber for roughly a minute. It pushed him downhill and when he came tight against the rope, it folded him over, ribs against pelvis. He was able to get a hand above the debris.
When the debris stopped, the victim climbed to the belay where he and the leader - who was only hit by the powder cloud - rigged a rappel. 
Several more natural slides followed, all smaller and confined to the gully. 
After 2 rappels, the party was able to escape into lower angled trees to climber's right. 
An urgent care visit day after the accident revealed the victim had suffered injured ribs, possibly cracked.

The victim noted that the forecast for snow the previous night had concerned him. They left the trailhead before reading the day's avalanche forecast, which rated the danger as Low (Level 1 of 5) at all elevations and identified Wind Slabs as the primary avalanche problem (possible, D1-1.5).
On Dec. 23rd, wind sensors at the nearest weather stations (Sperry Glacier and Garden Wall) were rimed so no wind speed measurements are available for the hours leading up to the accident. On the night of Dec. 22-23, Garden Wall did record southerly winds averaging in the mid teens with gusts to 28. The Flattop Mountain snotel recorded 0.3 inches of SWE and 3 inches of new snow on Dec. 23, with winds veering from southeast to northwest about daybreak.
The party had an exploratory mindset, because the terrain was new and it was their first technical climb of the winter. They were aware of the potential overhead hazard and potential conseqences of the terrain they planned to explore. On their approach, they saw no spindrift and didn't hear winds above them. The new snow was cohesionless and accumulations did not seem significant. They were surprised by the abrupt onset of natural avalanching, as well as the amount of snow entrained by the slides and the distance the slides ran. Although they're still digesting the event, the victim noted "I won't be getting into gullies during seemingly inconsequential storms anymore."
The FAC forecast team is glad the party is relatively unscathed. Thanks for sharing the event so our community can learn.
Instability Comments: Natural slides from overhead, not triggered.
Route: From gate at McDonald Lodge up GTSR, then through woods to climber's right side of Right/ West Gully ice route.

Weather Summary: Thick valley floor clouds and fog with a continuous cloud deck around 7000 feet. Generally dry with no wind.
Observation Summary:
Following an FAC morning meeting, we took a quick trip to the southern Whitefish Range.

The 12/20 rain and warm temperatures initiated a small mid-elevation wet loose avalanche cycle. We noted just two wet loose slides but a fair amount of rollerballs. Interestingly, most rollerballs started from tree bombs. Steep slopes without trees or exposed brush were generally clean, with no evidence of rollerball activity.
We jumped on steep (45-degree) mid-elevation slopes but had no luck triggering rollerballs or wet loose slides.
The surface snow was developing a crust above 6000 feet this afternoon due to cool temperatures.
Instability Comments: None observed

Observation Summary: A lot of melt and run off. Areas of exposed rock.

Very wet, heavy conditions. It basically rained all day with no new snow observed at lower elevations. Went to the second bridge in Kimmerly Basin and observed dirt on the trail. Did not go very high in elevation due to rock exposure.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Soggy. Light-Mod precip from mid morning to mid afternoon. Mostly mixed, though mixed means a few snowflakes tagging along with rain. Rain line about 6000 feet, though bounced up and down a few hundred feet. Light winds.
Observation Summary:
Spruce juice, tree pee, whatever you call it, we were in it today:

Light to moderate mixed precipation. The exact mix varied throughout our tour.  
The snow surface was wet on southerly slopes and moist on shaded aspects.
Natural and human triggered rollerballs were running below 6000'.
We found the 12/7 crust down 12-18 inches and of variable hardness (see snowpack).
Large and small column test yeilded stable results in our test pits. 

The travel was generally sticky but still  enjoyable for a soaker day.
Instability Comments: Natural and triggered rollerballs, mostly below 6000 feet.
Route: 5800' - 6600'  on aspects S through E to NW.

Observation Summary: Rain from 3400' to 6500', sleet at 6500-7000'. Wet snow falling at 7000-7800'. Stable snowpack at upper elevations, but wet slides running on steep slopes below 6500'. Early season hazards abound.
There was some new snow at higher elevation, but mostly rain below. Coverage was very light and snow was wet and heavy.  Weather was on the warmer side, and rainy.

Weather Summary: Tuesday (today) was a relatively nice day, however, the clouds dropped and it started to precipitate around 1:00.  Rain was happening at around 6,000 ft when we were leaving.
Observation Summary:
Despite low snow, young spruce trees and snowberry brush we made the pull up the hill to Jenny Lake on the back of the Swan Crest today.

We dug several pits above 7,000 ft. and did find the buried surface hoar.  It was unreactive in our stability tests, and we saw no evidence of avalanches today.
Skiing was good with old, settled powder.  Height of snow was roughly 100 cm deep.
The weather came in around 1:00 pm.  The rain line was 5,500 ft.
The snow surface (as of our leaving) above 6,000 ft. was moist.  
We saw rollerball activity starting around 6,000 ft.
Just below the ridgline at 7,400 ft. we found small isolated windslabs that were 6-8 inches thick.
Instability Comments: We found a couple small, isolated and unreactive windslabs.
Route: Sleds up the Doris road, through the brush and skin up Blaine Mtn.

Observation Summary: Light snow falling above 6400 ft. Sleet and rain below that. 6800-6400 ft held great dense powder on east and north aspects. We tried hard to sled cut steep mini features but saw no signs of instability. Great riding at those upper elevations. I’m guessing the warm air overnight may negatively affect snow quality, even up high.
No new snow at lower  elevation . Snow coverage was sparse. Observed several large rollers.

Against my better judgement I took the layup into the den of alder. Dug one pit on NE aspect at 6600’, depth of 105 cm, thick crust 55 cm down, another thinner crust 15 cm down. Did find the rain crust all the way up to my high point of 7100’. Some breakable wind board on open aspects with Easterly tilt. Saw some old point releases D1 on steep alpine faces otherwise nothing else visible.

Weather Summary: Mix of sun and clouds. Moderate to strong winds at upper ridgelines. No observed wind transport.
Observation Summary:
Went up to the Tunnel Ridge weather station to do a wellfare check. 

We observed no signs of instability and no recent avalanches.
We observed two notable debris piles that likely failed during the early December Atmospheric River. See avalanche tab for more information.
Moderate to strong winds at ridgetop. No snow transport observed.
The 12/7 crust was thick and supportable with no observed weak layers in a snowpit at 6400 feet. 
Surface snow is faceting on norths. Sun crust exists on souths.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.

Weather Summary: Stratus sandwich.  Cold (20's) below 6,000 ft.  Above that it was warmer, still below freezing though.
Observation Summary:
This days objective was to get into the Northern Whtiefish Range and get an eye on the distribution of the buried weak layer, surface snow condition and total depth of snow.

We found the 12/7 crust to be widely distrubted and robust (3-4 inches thick) on all aspects and up to our high point of 7200 ft.  It was generally underneath 40 cm of dense faceting snow.  We did notice it to be slightly thinner below 6,000 ft.  
We did not find any buried surface hoar.
The inversion has affected the surface snow above 6,500 ft.  There is a weak, breakable crust on SW-S aspects.  While being of notice on the way up, it was of no concern during our descent.  North, shaded, aspects are recycled powder without a crust.
The snowpack is generally stable and right side up.  Total depth was 100-130 cm above 6,000 ft.
Instability Comments: Things were very stable in our travels today.
Route: Snowmobiled to the end of the groomed road and skinned from there.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Observation Summary: Skiing on the slope felt stable, upper shaded slopes were lighter snow. As we got into more sun-affected areas, a thin crust appeared on the surface. No cracks/collapsing observed. Snowpack is very thin, with brush and downed trees still a hazard to look out for.
Route: Skied down into Canyon Creek. East /Northeast aspects. The elevation of the pit was 6470 ft.

Observation Summary: Wind slab, cornice formations above steeper E, SE facing aspects that drain into Crystal Creek We ski cut above a very steep 35-40° line/chute we were considering skiing that drains into Crystal Creek at the top, no results, but opted to ski more conservative, anchored terrain due to uncertainty of snow coverage on the steeper, full commitment part of the chute (worried it could be very shallow and unpredictable in the steeper, rockiest part). We skied anchored, open treed (bush) terrain,  into Crystal and Cascadilla Creeks, no signs of instability between 5-7000 feet.Non solar affected snow up high skis great!Solar impacted slopes are developing surface hoar and a crust layer that currently is fairly easily breakable at 6500-7000'2-4" of snow at highway.. 30+" of snow up high. N facing holding significantly more than S facing as you climb. 6000' is an average elevation to be above for good skiing right now.

Weather Summary: Overcast conditions until early afternoon when the clouds cleared, and it became primarily sunny. Moderate winds at ridgelines elevations but little to no blowing snow. We had good views of nearby prominent peaks and did not observe transport off their summits.
Observation Summary:
We traveled to the eastern edge of the advisory area for the first time this season to gather snowpack data.

We confirmed that the early December Atmospheric River soaked this location, leaving a thick, firm crust in the mid-pack.
Similar to other observations, the snowpack below 6000 feet is thin. 
The prevailing westerly winds have stripped exposed west-facing slopes of snow. 
Due to the lack of snowfall, the cornice line along the Elk Mountain bowl is quite small for this time of year. The cross-loaded cornice line in the Backstrap ski run is almost non-existent.
We had great views of many large avalanche paths but noticed no avalanches or notable debris piles.
Instability Comments: None observed

Observation Summary: A pit was dug by our instructors  and this was our observation as a group. Column test results showed Sudden Collapse but no propagation in the moderate to hard range on the 12/01 buried Surface Hoar layer
Route: Went up past twin lakes and over the saddle then dropped into Stryker basin right around 6,400

Observation Summary: We observed no obvious signs of instability and no avalanches. We dug a put on a southwest aspect at 7300 ft. Snow depth was 110cm. Mostly unremarkable snow structure. Extended column test result: ECTX. However, one more tap (31) resulted in partial propagation on a layer of surface hoar 52 cm from the surface. This layer is difficult to find in a snow profile and the crystals are mostly flattened. We also found the early Dec. rain crust just above this layer at this elevation. Overall, great skiing above 5500 ft. in sheltered terrain.

Stable conditions for now. South aspects  are heavily sun affected. North aspect has dry snow with multiple crust combinations. Test pit dug in open scree/boulder field showed preserved surface hoar  5cm, 45 cm, and 55cm down. 2-3mm depth hoar on the ground.
Instability Comments: Cracking in wind drifted snow on north to east aspect - stubborn 4” slab under cornice
Route: Skook Peak to Kimmerly Creek

Observation Summary: Quick evening run to the lake to see conditions. 8-10” of total snow at lake elevation. Alder guards access to the upper drainage and the NW face of Penrose appears to have run near full path presumably during the warm rain event
Snow was soft, dry, and fluffy. Could not make snowballs either day Avalanche  chutes in second canyon have very little snow. Also  observed rollers.

Weather Summary: Sun with building high stratus to the West.   Generally calm with SW gusts light to moderate.
Observation Summary:
Went to check on the access and snowpack in the Swan Lake area:

Total snow depth was 1 foot or less up to 6000'
Above 6000' the snowpack doubled to 2 feet
We found the 12/7 crust burried 10-16 inches deep up to 6800' (our high point)
Dry powder snow was in limited supply on our southerly route
Current snow surfaces featured: a thin melt freeze crust, wet snow, and surface hoar (which was getting nuked in the sun)
We briefly dipped onto a North aspect at 6800' and found buried surface hoar which yeilded an unstable result in a large column test.

The descent was rough.
Media to follow
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability

Observation Summary: Toured out to Ghoulies east of WMR. Dug a pit on a south facing aspect at 6500 ft. ECTX - No propagation in extended column test. CT 13, failed about 10 cm down, above the crust from 12/10.Sun crust was present in open south facing areas, shaded areas still had soft snow.
Real nice conditions for late October. Weather was clear with moderate to strong winds on the ridgetops. Not much cloud cover. Temps around freezing.
Route: Up the Edna Cr trail; skied the North and NW bowls. Descended trail.

Observation Summary: Skinned /hiked up the Great Northern summer trail on 10-20 cm of total snow until 6000 ft where  conditions improve dramatically. On the Spur ridge we saw surface hour on sheltered north slopes and a crispy rime layer below 10cm powder. First headwall on ridge to summit was a sketchy combo of no snow, ice on summer trail and wind blasted spines so we quickly abandoned summit ideas. Also super windy on the ridge at 20-40mph. Opted to ski a SE line into Stanton Ck drainage to look around. Some decent redistributed powder on suncrust around 7000 ft, rock hard wind slab mid line and 10 cm soft snow with small surface hoar below 6000 ft in runout. Snowed over debris piles below NE ramps and chutes indicated a decent avalanche cycle during the 12/7 crust event. That observation, combined with no cornice development, gave us a green light to climb and ski a steep NE line below the summit bridge. High point of 7300 ft. Hasty pits showed 20-50 cm snow above 12/7 crust and in one spot around 6500 ft a noticeable decomposing facet layer below the 12/7 crust. Surface snow had a couple thin rime layers in it and lots of wind effect. Also some near surface small facet layers being buried by wind distributed snow. Fun skiing with some soft snow redistributed above the wind effect. Incredibly low snowpack for the location. Lots of hiking on the return to trailhead.

Weather Summary: Clear skies from the Lake to the summit and across to the Park.Light winds above 6500'
Observation Summary: No signs of any avalanches in any of the big steep terrain in the area
Instability Comments: Low snow conditions
Route: Sw face Uniwah

Weather Summary: Clear and mostly calm with ridge top gusts
Observation Summary: Old avalanche activity
Instability Comments: Old loose slab avalanche, that was began as a single point release

Observation Summary: Lots of old rollerballs/ pinwheels on W and S aspects.  Creek is “skiiable” but still open.    Need a lot more snow to improve conditions..
Instability Comments: Small glide cracks spotted
Route: Up the road, up the creek, then back down

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Inversions Sandwich most of the day. Mostly calm with light/moderate winds out of the south near ridgetop. Cool temps up high all day. Air temp felt slightly above freezing at Snyder Lake in the late afternoon sunshine.
Observation Summary: Traveled into upper elevations of Glacier Park to help reduce uncertainties with the snowpack structure above 7500'.• The 12/7 rain crust is present up to 8100' in this area. • Average HS at upper elevations was about 90-110cm. Our highest observation from a previously loaded east facing slope held 120-170cm snowpack.• Aside from several wet slides from Wednesday, we observed no signs of instability, while traveling uphill or downhill today. • We descended a steep east facing chute into the south facing bowl of Mount Brown.• Despite seeing sunshine off in the distance in other parts of the advisory area, we spent most of the day in an inversion sandwich. The lower level stratus deck holding firm around 4500-5000', and the upper level cloud deck perched itself at about 8000' sometimes fluctuating upward a bit.• Around 2:00pm the upper level deck began to break down a bit, revealing sunshine and unobstructed views of the higher peaks in the area• We found the riding quality to be so so. But we knew what we signed up for.• We were able to successfully travel downhill on skis to about 4500'.  Off trail ski travel below 5500' remains not recommended.
Instability Comments: Numerous recent wet loose avalanches observed across the Upper Snyder Headwall between Mount Brown and the Little Matterhorn. And one D2 wet loose observed in the exit gully from Mount Brown to Snyder Lake. (possibly skier triggered from Wednesday?)
Route: Lake McDonald to Mount Brown Lookout. And beyond up the SW ridge to about 8100'. Descended to Snyder Lake.

Observation Summary: Low coverage at lower elevations

Weather Summary: Calm day with a two layer inversion. Low elevations were in the clouds and overcast skies kept the temperatures from rising.
Observation Summary:
We got an early start and headed to Glacier Park to get to 7500 feet.

The atmospheric river crust buried December 7th extends at or above 7500 feet in this location.
Yesterday (December 13th), an inversion baked the upper elevation’s surface snow, creating a surface crust ranging from an inch to 1.5 inches thick. 
At middle elevations, the crust was thinner, which we speculated was a result of the cloud inversion that kept these elevations from receiving direct sunlight.
Our takeaway for backcountry travel in this area remains that conditions are challenging. Low snowfall, surface crust, and downfall hazards meant less skiing and more walking.
Instability Comments: No recent avalanches or signs of instability observed today.
Route: We traveled mostly on a trail on southwest, west, and northwest aspects.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Intermittent sun early with clouds developing as the day went on. Light winds.
Observation Summary: Intermittent sunshine and calm winds accompanied the tour today. Digging atop the Picnic Lakes we found the 12/7 crust and felt comfortable skiing steep terrain down to picnic lakes. We skinned back up to the ridge north of the radio towers. Stomping cornices along this ridge yielded no concerning results. We skied a wind scoured steep W aspect in the Crown Bowl. The apron had a 2-4 inch wind slab on it. Looking above us we saw another steep run that appeared sheltered from the wind. We then skinned to near the top of the Crown Bowl again. This time choosing to ride a steep NW aspect skier's left of the top of the bowl. The snow up top was unconsolidated without a slab and we decided to ski this slope. The first rider was a snowboarder that rode the slope with no problems. The second rider was a skier that after descending about halfway down the slope, triggered, was caught and carried by a D1 Wind Slab avalanche. The skier was not buried and sustained no injuries, see avalanche tab for more information.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche
Instability Comments: Throughout the tour we checked for weak layers and signs of instability. None were readily apparent. We found the 12/7 rain crust above the basal facets and cornices were unreactive to stomping throughout the day. Throughout the tour there were no major red flags, which matched the low avalanche danger rating for the day. However, we triggered and a skier was caught and carried in a D1 Wind Slab avalanche.
Route: We toured from Camp Misery to the top of Radio Tower Ridge, skied down to Picnic Lakes, back up to Radio Tower Ridge, skied a steep W aspect on Crown Bowl, skinned to just below the top of Crown Bowl and skied a steep NW aspect on Crown Bowl.

Observation Summary: Spent the day above the inversion with stable conditions, nominal wind, and great snow.-The stratus deck hovered around 5500' all day. Above that, the sun was taking it's toll on South aspects; rollerball activity was noted in the 6500' band on the truest of S aspects. Above that and along ridgeline, the surface stayed cool. -Rain from last week washed away the base below 5500' (snow intercept from trees kept totals from the recent snowfall to about 2-4"), above 5500' there is a decent base with 6-10" of powder which made for easy skinning. -Wind was non-existent, but around noon I noticed flagging on the bigger peaks of the Flathead range and soon was experiencing winds strong enough to transport snow, although minimal. -Sloughing within the recent snow was observed in steep, alpine terrain and ran moderate distances. -I dug a pit at 7300' on an E aspect. Snow depth in this leeward location was 190cm (!)         -Within the recent snow (~40cm) there was a friable M/F crust (~1cm) near the           surface. Not really sure when or how it was formed, but it existed everywhere I travelled above           6000'        -The structure looked really good. 40cm of recent snow well bonded to a knife-hard rain crust 3"           thick.         -Underneath the rain crust is a conglomerate of saturated, but now frozen grains that was hard           to dig into.        -I probed a more representative slope and measured 90cm at 7000'-I skied an East aspect and experienced wonderful conditions (the friable M/F was hardly noticeable and quite surfy)-Stomping cornices along ridgeline produced no results and no surface hoar was observed throughout the tour. -Overall a great day out with plenty of good snow to be found in areas above 6500' that aren't heavily timbered

Weather Summary: Beautiful day. Stratus deck was around 6k in the south fork. Burned off by 1400.
Observation Summary:
Overall we found excellent skiing conditions and a mostly stable snowpack. There was however one outlier that alaramed us.

We observed only a few recent natural avalanches. These were all loose dry and likely occurred over the last few days.
Solar aspects warmed and ititiated numerous small rollerballs. We felt a cold south wind pick up soon after, which helped keep surfaces cool. 
This past weekends storm snow totaled up to 16 to 20 inches of snow. This snow appears to be bonding well to the underlying crust. We did find the 12/10 freezing rain crust within the new snow. This crust was unoticable while riding.
The 12/7 crust was 1 to 2 inches thick at our high point of roughly 8100 feet. 
We got unstable snowpit results on a very sheltered slope at 7400 feet on a northwest aspect. Here the crust was thin and a very tall layer of surface hoar existed a few inches beneath the crust. 

This structure was found no where else. I'll add that we dug 10 to 15 other hand pits and could not replicate it. 
Surface hoar is notoriously spotty, and for whatever reason the crust was thinner there than anywhere else. My takeaway was that I have zero interest in finding a slope with that structure. Deep enough to trigger, and deep enough that you won't know the slope is unstable until it fails. 



We ruled out sheltered slopes and headed over to a west faceing slope where we were confident the supportable crust existed, and the buried surface hoar got destroyed. We enjoyed excellent turns in steep terrain.

Weather Summary: •	A sometimes-broken stratus deck of clouds hung around 6500-7500’ elevation band all day with clear skies above. No precipitation.•	Calm to light westerly winds with daytime high temperatures ranging from the low-20s°F above 6000’ to right around the freezing mark on the Canyon floor.
Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	Not much snow. 3-5” total snow depth on the Canyon floor, about 8-10” at 5000’, 12-18” at 6500’. Snow depths increased quickly above here with typical depths of 2-3’ around 7000’. Deeper snow exists on the shaded aspects.•	Above-freezing temperatures and rain last week extended to at least 7200’ and likely much higher.•	No signs of instability observed save for one propagating result in an Extended Column Test (see pit profile below). This occurred in a layer of moist facets below the Dec. 7th crust•	The snowpack basically consisted of 5-10” of soft powder, newer snow on top, then the 2-4” thick Dec. 7 rain crust below that, then moist, rounding facets below that to the ground (pit profile).•	The more recent snow resting on top of the Dec. 7th crust seems to be mostly stable. Ski cuts on some wind slabs atop the crust did not produce any cracking or slab releases. No fractures in this layer with ECTs.
Route: •	Climbed the Shed 11/10.7 ridge to the Shed 11 weather station at 6400’ elevation. Then continued up the ridge above Shed 10.7 to the top of Path 1163 at 7200’.

Weather Summary: Periods of clear skies with sun, when we were above the inversion layer of clouds. Winds were calm all day. When we made our exit, temps were above freezing.
Observation Summary:
We took a quick tour to Noisy Basin to check on new snow totals and to get above 7000 feet in elevation.

At 7000 feet, we found the atmospheric river crust which was buried on December 7th. Below the crust, the snow was moist with basal facets.
We were surprised by a lack of wind-affected surface snow. 
Snow depths at mid-elevations ranged from 115 to 130 cm (45 to 50 inches). The brush is not covered.
As the day progressed, warm temperatures caused trees to shed snow and our sled back to the truck included some dirt riding.
In the parking area, there were bear prints. They aren't hibernating just yet.
Instability Comments: We observed a few isolated sluffs of 2 inches of soft, overnight snow that appeared to slide on the freezing rain crust. No cracking or collapsing was observed.

Weather Summary: Light precipitation throughout the day.
Observation Summary:
We took a short trip to the neighboring country around WMR. In general we were pleasantly surprised to find more new snow than expected.

9 to 13 inches of storm snow rests on the 12/7 crust. We observed no signs of instability in the new snow.

We found the thin 12/10 crust roughly 4 inches below the surface. This crust is eggshell thick and hardly noticable while riding.


Snowpit tests revealed unstable results on north aspects. North aspects appeared to have a weaker snowpack structure than southerly aspects. See snowpack tab for more information.
The snowpack is still moist to wet below the 12/10 crust. I suspect this will continue to gain strength over time.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.

Weather Summary: Light steady snowfall through the day. Maybe 1-2 inches of total accumulation by 4pm.We did not observe the freezing rain / rime crust that was described elsewhere in the region.
Observation Summary: Thin coverage in this part of the region. More information in the snowpack tab.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability
Route: FNBP annual skills/ rescue refresher up at Blacktail.

Observation Summary: New layer of freezing rain capping all the recent snow all the way up to at least 6200’, found about 8-10” of new snow on top of the prior rain event, but it was less of a defined crust compared to the Flathead range yesterday. The bottom of the snowpack was moist and uniform. Intermittent snow flurries once the freezing rain stopped.

Observed no obvious signs of instability. Light precip throughout the day. There is a thin, new, zipper crust on the surface of Saturday night's snow. This crust is less present underneath trees and thicker in open terrain. Despite the crust we found the skiing quite enjoyable.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: A calm day with broken cloud cover that limited our view of surrounding peaks.
Observation Summary:
We visited Glacier National Park to get a better sense of new snowfall and at what elevation the December 5th rain extended to.

Access is challenging. We boot-packed up to 6500 feet via the trail (no skiing). 
At 6500 feet, total snow depth was 60 to 55 cm.
20 cm (about 8 inches) of new unconsolidated snow, above a 3-inch semi-supportable crust.
Below the new snow, the snowpack was moist to wet, verifying that the recent atmospheric river soaked this area.
We did not observe a layer of surface hoar that has been observed in other zones of our forecast area.
The good news is that our lower snowpack will likely strengthen as the moist/wet snow freezes overnight.
Instability Comments: No cracking or collapsing was observed. We did not observe recent avalanches.

Observation Summary: Traveled up in mellow and primarily low angle terrain and down in steep treed and low angle terrain. Obvious crust for most of the ski down with about -8” of new snow.At 7400’, SW slope, protected area near ride - total depth 100cm. CTM17SP at 40cm down on a crust (didn’t get to analyze).  We saw no cracking and did not feel any collapsing throughout the day. Low wind most of the day- cloudy/foggy with moderate visibility above 700’. Winds light.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Light snow throughout the day. Winds picked up in the afternoon and transported moderate amounts of snow.
Observation Summary:
FAC lacks observations from the Flathead Range, so we headed to Grant Ridge to collect baseline snowpack data. 

This morning's 24-hour snowfall total was 2 inches at 4,500 feet and 5 inches above 6,000 feet.
Snowfall through the day favored lower elevations, as we found six dense inches on our truck (3500 feet) at day's end. 
The recent snow rests on a 1 - 2 inch thick crust unsupportable to a person on skis. This crust extends from the valley floor to our high point at 6300 feet and formed during the recent warm rain event.
At our high point, we noted the rain had percolated through the snowpack to the ground. All of the snow below the crust is moist, and we did not find evidence of a buried surface hoar layer.
Obscured skies limited our view of neighboring avalanche terrain.
Instability Comments: None observed
Route: Up and down the Grant Ridge pack trail.

Weather Summary: It was a nice calm storm day out in the mountains. Light snowfall (S1) most of the day, and around 230pm snowfall rates picked up at bit (S2-S5). About 4 inches accumulated at Walton Ranger Station during my tour. Calm winds all day, even on ridgetop and up at the lookout.
Observation Summary: Decided to head out to Southern Glacier Park to help fill some gaps in recent data for the FAC. I had a few questions for the day.• Did it actually rain less in this part of the forecast region? Did it rain at all above 6500?• How has the Dec 1 Surface Hoar responded to recent changes. And how about the other faceted crusts that we had brewing out there last week?• Will I be able to put skis on at my car?I was successful at answering these questions. More information in the snowpack tab. And today's real blessing was that I got to observe my favorite type of snow... The great paradoxal and fleeting crystals known as "moist facets". Riddle me that!
Instability Comments: Two audible collapses on a flat section of the ridgetop at 6500'. Suspected weak layer is moist facets immediately below the Dec 6th rain crust.
Route: Walton Ranger Station to Scalplock Lookout. Much like Russ' Street, uphill travel is permitted from trailhead (barely) , but skiers must download below 6000'. Coverage is not yet suitable for downhill skiing below that elevation.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Low cloud obsurred skies.  Bursts of graupel after noon with some moderate gusts. Drove through rain in Bigfork transitioning to heavy snow south of Soup Creek.
Observation Summary:
We visited the Northern Swan Range to get a snapshot of the snowpack between the waning rain event and incoming snow:

We noted 2 small wet avalanches that occured in the previous 48 hours
Wet snow near the surface was begining to refreeze into a crust at upper elevations
Large column tests yeilded mixed results on buried weak layers
Snowfall and wind gusts began in the afternoon as temperatures decrease
We relied on extreme back-seat skiing to overcome wet snow and abundant obstacles on our descent
Instability Comments: Noticed two small wet avalanches that likely failed during rain on snow.
Route: 5000'  to 6500' on NW through N to E

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: A balmy day in the WF range.  Temps above freezing, no precip and generally good visibiliy with high clouds.
Observation Summary:
What luck we had today with high clouds and no rain! We took in the final day of the pre-WMR season and toured out to Ghoulie's point. 

As imagined, the top 8-10 inches had soaked up the 1.3 inches of rain that fell 12/4-5.  This top layer is uncohesive and sits atop the crust/surface hoar from last week.
We saw evidence of many wet loose avalanches from 12/4-5.  These were all confined to the upper elevations (above 5500').
The snow surface on shaded aspects already had a very thin crust forming at the surface. This crust should get thicker and more supportable as temperatures cool this week.  
Today, boot pen is as deep as your legs are long, with postholing a guaranteed thing once the skis are off.
Terrain below 5,000' has so little snow that avalanches were not a concern.
Route: We skinned out to Ghoulie's point.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken scattered clouds looking south down valley with bits of open sky and sunlight. Looking north over the Whitefish Range was darker grey skies that looked to slowly open up into a drizzle towards afternoon.
Observation Summary: Very wet saturated snow with high ski and boot penetration. On our SW solar aspect, the surface hoar layer was a melt/freeze crust that was roughly 2-4cm thick. Clustered rounds of moist snow grains below crust with slightly increasing hardness to the ground.  Snowpack temp was primarily at 0 F throughout.Pretty terrible skiing. Was not there for that.
Instability Comments: Collapsing in CT on buried surface hoar/ crust. No Propagation during ECT.
Route: East route of WMR. Found open area around Whiteys west of Chair 5 towers. 6200’ SW.

Weather Summary: WARM.  It rained off and on throughout the day, however probably less than .10" total.
Observation Summary:
We took a hike, both up and down, today in the Middle Fork.  

Rain happened all the way to 7500'.
There was 4-6 inches of old snow  in the valley bottom in the morning.  By the time we left there was only 1-2 inches .
We saw rollerballs on open N and NE slopes.
Total snow depth at 7000' was 75 cm.
Skies were overcast all day, rain was intermittent.
Temps were WARM.
Instability Comments: The only sign of instabily we saw today was rollerballs at mid elevations.
Route: We skinned/hiked up into the Wolverine Peak area.

Weather Summary: Rain at the parking lot and drizzling at our pit 5000’. Little to no wind, full overcast.
Observation Summary: Lots of rain. Heavily saturated upper snowpack of the most recent snowfall. Wet but still preserved Surface Hoar around midpack of our 30cm pit. Sudden Collapse on this interface of newly wet snow.
Instability Comments: CT7. SC. Q1.  No propagation, but collapse on buried surface hoar at new snow surface interface.
Route: Around 5000’ on Expressway in a very small clearing free from trees and tracks. South- Southeast.

Observation Summary:
The snowpack is rather talkative these days. Our travel today remained consistent with observations from this past weekend. 

We triggered dozens of shooting cracks and heard many audible collapses while traveling. These occurred on all of the aspects we traveled and above 6500 feet. 

One noteworthy observation was that we initiated a crack next to a wind-loaded ridgeline that propagated approximately 200 feet and into flat terrain on top of the ridge. This is a result of wind drifting snow into a hard slab. This was a clear sign to give start zones a wide berth. 


Recent storm totals left a 4 to 8-inch soft slab in wind-sheltered terrain, and a 10 to 16-inch hard slab in wind-loaded terrain. 
Slabs are sitting on top of a variety of persistent weak layers. The most impressive weak layer is the buried surface hoar which primarily existed in terrain sheltered from southwest winds.
Instability Comments: We observed audible collapses and shooting cracks anywhere there seemed to be more than 8 inches of recent snow. The only locations where we didn't see or observe these signs were in wind-scoured terrain. We didn't observe these until we got above 6500 feet. This could also be due to the elevation where we got off the pre-existing skin track.

Observation Summary: Consistent with other observations, we found a slightly upside down storm snow, depth of 25-30 cm of new snow poorly bonded. We had cracking while skinning and had several collapses in the snowpack while skinning. Not windy while we were out yesterday, but evidence of wind seen in the surface snow and a couple down trees.

Weather Summary: Light to moderate winds throughout the day kept air temperature below freezing.
Observation Summary:
We went to the eastern edge of the advisory area to check the snowpack..

At the trailhead, the 6 to 8 inches of recent snow barely covered the green bear grass.
True to the east sides reputation, the wind blew light to moderate throughout the day
Wind scoured and wind drifts were the predominant snow formation.
At 5400 feet, wind had formed 1-finger hard slabs that ranged from 55 to 80 cm in depth. These slabs were reactive in multiple tests. They all failed while cutting the back of an ECT with a snow saw.
Instability Comments: As we traveled through wind-drifted snow, we had isolated cracking around our skis.

Observation Summary: WMR Snow Safety was conducting early season avalanche mitigation work on a North aspect just below 7000' in elevation.  We found reactive to touchy conditions on a 90 cm snowpack with 25 cm new snow sitting on  a mixed bag of surface hoar, facets and crusts all the way to the ground.  Large collapse whumphs and cracking were occuring everywhere we went and areas steeper than 35 degrees were reactive to explosives use.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Instability Comments: Many small, recent natural avalanches were observed.
Route: WMR Snow Safety was conducting early season avalanche mitigation work on a North aspect just below 7000' in elevation.  We found reactive to touchy conditions on a 90 cm snowpack with 25 cm new snow sitting on  a mixed bag of surface hoar, facets and crusts all the way to the ground.  Large collapse whumphs and cracking were occuring everywhere we went and areas steeper than 35 degrees were reactive to explosives use.

Observation Summary: Widespread shooting cracks of up to 50 feet on untracked slopes above 30°. Seemed to be on a density change 10-15cm below the surface soft slab and 3cm  above the hard old snow layers. Classic upside-down storm slab situation.Light gusting, moderate Southwest winds up top forming fresh wind slabs in open terrain.

IT SNOWED! Good day in Jewel Basin with some fun early season obs. We noticed that the wind had been strong out of the west all Friday and last night, and we were expecting to see a few inches of new snow.We had active precipitation all day and observed wind drifting snow with strong winds 30mph out of the west. We experienced many skier triggered cracks on east facing aspects in Jewel Basin.  Cracks propagated up to 40 feet and were about 4-6 inches thick.  On a more northerly aspect we came across a natural crack which had propagated around 40 feet and was 9 inches thick.  This cracking appeared to be happening between a wind slab formed overnight and a less dense layer of fresh snow below from  yesterday.From our observations we did not see any avalanches but we did experience poor visibility throughout the day with a low cloud ceiling.
Widespread instability in new storm snow.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Instability Comments: Touchy dense storm slab 15cm thick producing widespread shooting cracks and collapses on walk up.  Remote triggered 1 slab avalanches off of inspo into the fault 1  from 10 feet back from slope. Slab depth in cross loaded terrain estimated to be a 1.5 feet deep by 50 feet wide. Two additional much shallower soft slabs released onto inspo.

Observation Summary: Widespread cracking and propagation limited our exposure today. We stuck to mellow slopes away from wind loading. Even so, every turn saw some cracking. Wind and snow load increased throughout the day.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)
Instability Comments: Storm slabs slid and cracked readily on the facets and buried surface hoar.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: •	Clear skies in JFS Canyon in the morning with light easterly winds and single digit °F temperatures.•	Winds clocked to the S-SW around noon but remained light. Clouds increased throughout the day and by sunset a few snow flurries had begun.•	Daytime highs reached the low-20s °F on the Canyon floor and the teens °F above 6000’ elevation.
Observation Summary: •	0-3” of snow on the Canyon floor, thicker patches around 5000’, then a continuous snowpack from 6000’ on up ranging from about 5-20” deep (photo). Deeper snow exists on the shaded aspects.•	The snowpack is generally weak and composed almost entirely of facet layers with some crusts interspersed between.•	Snowpack surfaces consist of several inches of facets and widespread surface hoar (photo).•	Southerly slopes that receive more direct daytime solar radiation had the least amount of surface hoar and a weak crust capping the surface with facets underneath.•	No signs of instability today, but all this weak snow will become a concern once loaded by an overlying slab.
Instability Comments: • No avalanche activity or signs of instability observed.
Route: •	Climbed the Shed 6/7 ridge to the Shed 7 weather station at 6300’ elevation. Then continued up the ridge above Shed 7 West and Shed 9 to the Snowslip weather station at 7000’.

Weather Summary: The day began with high pressure and another inversion. The stratus deck hovered around 5000'. By mid afternoon the inversion began to dissipate and was replaced by overcast skies and light snow showers. Calm winds all afternoon.
Observation Summary: Climbed up to the Apgar Crest to assess conditions prior to the pattern shift this evening. Trial run today with a new Gadget that FNBP has in their arsenal (The Snow Scope by Propagation Labs). This electronic probe is fashioned with two sensors. One measures force and the other measures depth. It allows the user to rapidly collect hardness profiles.Attached to this observation are several pit graphs generated from the Snow Scope. (I can provide more upon request, 8 image max on this form) I selected a handful that I believe represent the average for their aspect/elevation. (aspect and elevation are listed in the title of each graph). I completed one manual observation to take a closer look, and to confirm that the fancy probe knows what it doing.Biggest takeaways from the day were how widespread, large, and upright the surface hoar was in this range. I'm not sure what's more troubling, 20+mm feathers on north and east facing slopes or the facet crust combos developing on southerlies? Light snowfall began around 2:00pm today...
Instability Comments: Stable conditions.
Route: Apgar Crest to ~6400'. Mostly traveled on southerlies, but explored other aspects near and on the ridgetop.

Weather Summary: Inversion with cloud deck at  ~4000-4500 feet. Temperature climbed above freezing at mid elevations.
Observation Summary:
Went for a waddle near the southern tip of the advisory area:

No recent avalanches or obvious signs of instability
Spotty snow (0-4") at 5000'  becoming more continuous (2-6") at 6000'
Found a couple small and isolated patches 14" deep above 6500'
Surface conditions consisted of facets and surface hoar in sheltered areas and crust on sunny,  wind-exposed slopes
Mid-elevation snow in direct sunlight became moist during the afternoon
Instability Comments: No signs of instability
Route: Ascend SW aspects and walk ridge to 6800'

Observation Summary: Hollow and weak snowpack at 6000’, pretty much non existent below that.  Surface hoar wide spread but not as tall as at Blacktail! North facing slope had released naturally, must have run about 1000+

Toured around the hornet lookout area on Sunday, made it above the inversion. Surface hoar was present on all aspects. Snowpack was variable in depth. Dug a pit at 6100ft on a NE slope, no stability test but fairly consistent density down to bottom  crust layer.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Never broke through the inversion layer, though it was thin about 6800 feet. Cold in the cloud. light southwesterly winds.
Observation Summary: Recreational tour to feel out the snow and shake out the cobwebs. Supportable but soft, weak snowpack above about 5600-5700 feet (image). Large surface hoar nearly omnipresent below about 6600 feet. Some drifting and scouring on exposed aprons and some confined features (image). On the exit trail, discretion got the better part of valor about 5100 feet.
Instability Comments: None observed

Observation Summary: Widespread surface hoar noted on all aspects (primarily S, S/E) from snowline ~5000' and up. Something to keep in mind once our snow dances pay off, or the wind gets to it.
Route: Snow line ~5000ft on Apgar lookout trail, primarily S, S/E aspects

Observation Summary: “You can tell it’s a big ol wind slab by the way it is.”We headed up to Comeau Pass in search of some soft snow and a deeper snowpack. The Sperry trail is largely bare or with modest, faceted accumulations for a long ways. Surface hoar present. We switched to skis around 6k ft. One of our primary concerns was solar input on the south facing slopes of Mt Edwards, which carry far more potential than you can see while under them. While we did see some minimal rollerball action, it never warmed enough during the day to initiate any kind of loose wet problem although one small slide had done this at some point in the past and reached the summer trail in the basin above the chalet.All slopes we traveled on had some form of crust. SW aspects this from solar melt and refreeze and it wasn’t much fun to ski. Wind had transported some soft snow into harder lenses with varying distribution  atop this crust in the areas above 6500ft. Primary direction for this west of the pass was from the SW, while we found the opposite east of Comeau Pass. These lenses could be over a foot thick in places. We steered clear of the larger ones on the face. At upper elevations (above 8500ft) on north aspects we found a hard, rimed crust that was supportive of body weight and highly textured. The big takeaway for me from a snowpack standpoint: every one of the current snow surfaces we saw can be a culprit once it gets buried and loaded in the next or subsequent storms. We saw light N winds at the pass and glacier basin. Skies above the inversion were clear. No transport of snow was observed.

Rode up to Whitefish Mountain into the upper bowls.Observed a Total Snow Depth of 95cm at 6900ft NE facing slope.-Facets near the ground- Pencil hard crust layer from 30 - 40 cm, was reactive on a shovel shear test- Surface rain crust at all elevations, we noticed the another crust 10cm from the surface at lower elevations

Found a wide array of snow conditions. Snow depth of 55-65 cm above 7k. Consolidated powder is sheltered polar aspect, scoured and sun crust on every other aspect. Gusty winds from the south with snow transport with minimal wind slab development. Disheartening future forecast….

It’s giving cooking. Below the inversion, we saw road carnage with a collision that blocked a bridge and another car that had rolled. Yikes. Above it, it was hawt. Like, snow melting out of the trees hot. Take off your long johns while standing on the skinner hot. Brilliant sunshine and light winds confined to ridges meant that all solar surfaces (which had 1.5cm surface hoar) were wet to the touch. Didn’t see any local wet loose action but the attached avalanche observation on Great Northern appears to have run over 1000ft (sorry for the potato camera photo). On polar aspects, lighter density snow from our most recent storm was deposited atop a hard crust near the dirt. In places, wind had stiffened this, but distribution varies. We skied one lap on this, skinned up it to the shack, and called it a day. For what it’s worth, most of the skiing and skinning west of the ridgetop just isn’t deep enough to be much fun yet. You can ski more or less from the yurt on the road, but it gets slick and snowy before that.

Really warm in the alpine today. Sun crust on solar aspects in the morning. Trees shedding snow and ice by 0900 at 7300’.  About 15-20 cm of new  snow. Skied without instability above 6500, roller balls and wet snow below. There were two natural point releases in the new snow that ran 800’.

All solar aspects were hit hard by the warm temps and sun, but upper elevation NE to NW aspects hung onto 5-10 mm surface hoar. I imagine Sunday’s light snow could cover this surface hoar without collapsing it. Could be  a layer to watch.

Weather Summary: Clear skies with some valley fog. Light SW winds with moderate/occasionally strong gusts.
Observation Summary: -Trace of crusty snow at 4200', 3-4" of recent snow (11/15 storm) on crust at 5000'. -Between 6000-7000' recent snow from Wednesday totaled 8-12" and was resting on a breakable crust with facets underneath. Snow depths below the crust were about 5-10" on the slopes we were on. Snow depths on shaded, leeward aspects were deeper, 2 or more feet. - From 7000-8700' the terrain we travelled on was heavily rimed and generally wind scoured down to a sometimes supportable, but mostly breakable crust. Total snow depths were highly variable, ranging from only a few inches to 4+ feet deep in pockets that were loaded with wind slab. -No signs of instability save for a few afternoon rollerballs from the rocks on southerly slopes below 6500' and a couple tiny slabs (2-3' wide by 10" thick) failing with ski cuts on top of the crust. -Observed a few old crowns (D1.5) on steep, unsupported northerly slopes between 6500-7500'. These crowns were about 10" thick and likely failed during the storm on Wednesday. -Slopes on solar aspects at 6500' and below in full sun were cooked during the day and then crusting over during our exit in the evening.
Route: Ridge route to 8700' elevation. Some travel on mostly southerly and westerly slopes, but great views of all aspects.

Weather Summary: A beautiful day out in the mountains. Clear skies. Observed calm winds except at upper ridgelines along the Swan Crest. We felt moderate to strong winds at our high point of 7200'.
Observation Summary:
Today we went to the northern Swan Range to work on weather stations and collect field observations. Overall we were pleasantly surprised at the quality of the snow and lack of red flags. We also hit fewer rocks than originally expected thanks to a supportable rain crust.

We parked the truck at 5800 feet in about eight inches of new and old snow. 
From 5800 feet to 6500 feet, we found eight to ten inches of unconsolidated snow resting on hard rain crust.
New snow accumulations at upper elevations (6500 and above) were slightly greater, and the total snowpack height was roughly two feet. 
We observed no signs of unstable snow on northerly aspects (no whumpfing, no cracking, and no recent natural avalanches).

We did observe natural rollerballs on solar aspects at around 1200. 
During our descent on a southwest aspect, we triggered two very small and harmless loose wet avalanches. See the avalanche tab for more information.

location: Two Medicine/ Marias Pass/ Saint Marys Valley
Weather Summary: 50-60 mph gusts through out. Poor driving and ground blizzard conditions along the Saint Marys Road.
Observation Summary: Large scale wind transport along the Rocky Mountain Front effecting Marias Pass, Two Medicine and Saint Marys Valleys

Route: North East Aspects above 6000

location: Western edge of the Flathead Range
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Obscured skies and cool temperatures above 5000 feet kept the snow surface cold and dry. Brief periods of afternoon sunshine below 4500 feet with snow falling from tree branches and sections of ground melting out.
Observation Summary:
We traveled to the western edge of the Flathead Range to get a generalized overview of the snowpack. 

Before the 11-15-23 storm, most locations below 5500' had melted out with no snow remaining.
We were pleasantly surprised to find two feet of snow on the ground on north aspects above 6000'.  Solar aspects around this elevation held up to 18".
We found no evidence of wind-transported snow on the exposed slopes we traveled but assumed westerly winds had drifted snow onto leeward terrain along the Flathead Crest. 
Snowpack and snowpit data can be found under the Snowpack header.
Instability Comments: None observed
Route: Ousel Peak West trail

Weather Summary: •	Mostly clear skies with no precipitation.•	Light westerly winds in the terrain I travelled through. But I could see small plumes of snow blowing off peaks reaching above 7000’ elevation by mid-afternoon.•	Daytime highs reached the low-40s °F on the Canyon floor and near the freezing mark around 6400’ elevation.
Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed on the terrain I travelled on. But there really wasn’t enough snow to create much if any avalanche hazard.•	Visibility of distant terrain in the Flathead Range and Glacier National Park was excellent and no avalanche activity observed in the higher alpine terrain which has a deeper snowpack.•	No snow on the Canyon floor. Sunny aspects around 5000’ had thin (2-3” deep), scattered patches of snow, mostly in the shade.•	By 6000’ on sunny slopes the snow cover was more consistent, but still patchy in coverage and ranging from 5-10” deep.•	The snowpack exposed to full sun was composed of a weakening melt-freeze crust (2-4” thick) on the surface with wet grains below that to the ground.•	Snow in the shade was dry with a thinner breakable crust on top with rounding facets underneath. •	Views of distant terrain indicated that the snowpack deepens considerably above about 6500-7000’ elevation, especially on shaded aspects.
Route: •	Climbed the Shed 11/10.7 ridge to the Shed 11 weather station at 6400’ elevation.•	Objectives were to work on the weather station and observe the developing snowpack.

Observation Summary: Around 7400’ found snow depth to be around 55 cm. Melt freeze crust around 20 cm. 5 cm of new snow. Variable wind effect on west south and east aspects. Minimal surface hoar development on north aspect. On our descent solar aspect at6500’ and below we’re getting wet, small rollers with ski turns. And 500’ later we were on dirt…

Weather Summary: Mostly clear skies. Calm winds with occasional light gusts from the SW. Temps remained below freezing in the shade.
Observation Summary: Took advantage of the high snow line to travel up the sun road by bike & skis today. I wanted to get a sense of our slow to develop snowpack and see what this next storm might be landing on. I also was curious to see if there have been any recent avalanches  on any of the taller peaks that actually have a measurable amount of snow on them. Was dismayed to find that even here near the continental divide things are looking meager.No significant avalanches to report. And hardly enough snow for the hazard to exist yet. Low elevations simply aren't holding. Middle elevations are still mostly rough with lots of vegetation and rock showing. And upper elevations actually have some snow, but unfortunately didn't get out the door early enough to find out how much.
Instability Comments: No signs of any recent slab avalanche activity. A few recent small (D1-1.5) loose snow slides in steep gullies. Possibly triggered by icefall/rockfall.
Route: Avalanche Creek to The Weeping Wall. High point of ~5800'. Travel confined to the road corridor.

Observation Summary: On the SW aspect up Toni Matt the snow didn't get deep until close to the summit. The N and NE aspects on the backside had three distinct layers with a drier snow layer close to the ground covered by a thin crust. When probed with a ski pole the melt freeze  crust gave way to a vacuous dry layer of snow close to the ground. SW facing slopes and trees had a notable rime forming. Good skiing but definitely will be keeping an eye on the drier snow at the ground present on NE aspects as the season continues.
Route: Hiked up toni matt to the summit and skiied halfway down the backside, then skiied toni matt.

Observation Summary: At the lake looking back toward the east facing ridge the coverage looked much better with most of the shorter alders covered. Driving up and down US 2 it seems there is a big difference in east aspects which are loaded at high elevations and west aspects which even at high elevations look thin in all the areas observed. This seemed to be a factor in all west aspects observed from Mt Stanton in the park all the way to Elk Mountain at Morias Pass. Better skiing to be had on east aspects along US 2 right now.
Route: Hiked up to Marion Lake on the trail

Weather Summary: Partly sunny to mostly cloudy skies all day. No precipitation with light westerly winds and very sporadic stronger gusts. Minimal wind-loading confined to terrain above 7000'.  Daytime high temperatures estimated in the  high teens to low 20s *F above 7000'.
Observation Summary: -Bare ground or 1-3" of melt-freeze (MF)/crusty snow at 5000' elevation.-Recent snow from Nov 7/8 was about 4-8" deep around 6000' and resting on top of older melt-freeze crusts 3-4" thick.-This recent snow depened quickly to 10-12+" in areas sheltered from the wind between 6000-7500'. The underlying MF crust was sometimes supportable, but other times not, and  boot penetration reached 15-20" to nearly the ground.-In the wind exposed alpine terrain above 7500', the snowpack was highly variable, but generally westerly aspects were scoured down to rock or a supportable and cramponable MF crust, while easterlies held more recent soft storm snow and wind slabs (4F hard).-Post-holed through a few wind pillows with no slab releases or any cracking.-Generally stable conditions with the base of snowpack consisting mostly of melt-freeze crusts on the terrain observed.
Instability Comments: No avalanche activity observed. No signs of instability.
Route: Hiking and mountain climbing, multiple aspects up to 9500'.

Observation Summary: 5000'-6500':                      -Snow coverage became consistent at ~5000' (1-2" of moist M/F grains(rain)) This rain crust existed to my high point of 7300',  more on that                        below...                      -Powder snow around 5500' with 4-8" of recent accumulation to 6500' on top of the moist, M/F basal layer (4-6")6500'-7300':                      -Travel on foot became difficult above 6500'.                       -Recent accumulations topped out around 10" (F+) and appeared well bonded to the rain crust which became supportable around 7200'                      -This M/F layer was pencil-knife hard (boot supportable), 10" above the ground                      -Below the rain crust I observed frozen M/F grains with no alarming basal layer (no depth hoar in this location)                      -Small, harmless cornices existed at ridgeline. Cracking was confined underfoot, but present.
Route: Ascended NE ridge from 3800'-7300'

Observation Summary: Temperature Inversion and unstable vertical ice conditions in all aspects. Especially afternoon sun effected slopes on Mount Cannon. Upper elevations above freezing while lower elevations below. Observed large and small ice feature failure and falling debris from mid day onward. Events large enough to injure or kill a climber In hazard terrain.

Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Cold (low 20s?) with a light southerly breeze at ridgeline.
Observation Summary: Snow from trailhead to summut, though snow depths didn't exceed 5" until about 5-5500 ft. 6-8 inches at ridgeline. Not much evidence of wind drifting. Snow is dry above 4000 ft and coehsionless, and generally consists of one layer that doesn't exceed surface roughness. Surface hoar in open areas above about 5000 feet, with the snow below faceting.

Weather Summary: Light rain and snow showers above 6000’ throughout the day, with occasional clearing. A classic fall day in GNP.  No accumulation of new snow.
Observation Summary: No recent avalanches or snow being actively transported. NE slopes above 7500’ still holding snow after the warm week.  All other aspects appeared melted out.
Instability Comments: No instability observed.
Route: Gunsight Mountain via Comeau Pass
